🌱 Bioregioning Earth
Organizations · Sources · Ontology · How to Explore · About
← Back to Dashboard

Bioregional Organizations

Bioregional actors and initiatives currently mapped

110 records

Detail
Name Website Description Place Category All info
r3.0 🌐 r3.0 crowdsources open recommendations for necessary transformations across diverse fields and sectors, in response to the ecological and social collapses humanity is experiencing, in order to achieve a thriving, regenerative and distributive economy and society. Support Provider
Yukon River Inter-Tribal Watershed Council 🌐 An Indigenous-led grassroots coalition founded in 1997, uniting 70+ First Nations and Alaska Native Tribes across the Yukon River watershed. Guided by a vision to one day safely drink water directly from the river, YRITWC works through collaborative programs in water-quality monitoring, community education, stewardship, and sustainable resource governance. Their YRITWC Water Health Indicators complements scientific water quality monitoring with "cultural indicators", including elements like the taste of fish, the behavior of indicator wildlife species, and the quality of plants used for traditional medicines—dimensions of watershed health that conventional metrics might miss but reflect deep, multi-generational knowledge of the system. Alaska Hwy, Yukon, YT, Canada Bioregional Organizer
Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative (Y2Y) 🌐 The Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative (Y2Y) is a pioneering transboundary conservation network that unites governmental bodies, Indigenous groups, NGOs, scientists, landowners, and communities across a 2,100-mile corridor spanning five U.S. states and two Canadian provinces and territories. Launched in 1993, Y2Y's mission is to maintain ecological integrity and habitat connectivity from the Greater Yellowstone ecosystem in Montana/Wyoming to the Yukon Territory. The organization’s success is reflected in a 300% increase in protected habitat since its inception, along with new corridors and species recoveries . Bozeman, MT, United States Bioregional Organizer
Wij land 🌐 In 2016, Wij.land started exploring how the 4 Returns can help restore ecological functions of the peat meadow landscape in the Vechtstreek. This has grown into a learning network of almost 200 cattle farmers today who are futureproofing their farm by learning how to work more and more alongside nature and in connection with the landscape. It plays a key role in connecting different stakeholders in the landscape to create natural, social, and financial value and to inspire others in joining this cause. The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands Bioregional Organizer
Whanganui River — In New Zealand, the Whanganui River catchment forms the basis for both ecological governance and cultural identity for local Māori iwi (tribes). The recognition of the river as a legal person (Te Awa Tupua) in 2017 acknowledged both its ecological significance and its central place in Māori cultural identity and governance traditions. Whanganui, Manawatu Whanganui, New Zealand Bioregional Organizer
Wellspring Commons 🌐 Wellspring Commons is a bioregionally grounded initiative dedicated to cultivating mutually thriving relationships between human communities and the larger web of life—from protecting ecological lifelines and developing local food systems, to building a forest-based materialshed and funding ecosystems for regenerative action. Wellspring Commons also contributes to a growing knowledge commons by sharing tools, information, and practices that align with the limits and generative capacities of place. White Mountain National Forest, 719 N Main St, Laconia, NH 03246-2741, United States Bioregional Organizer
Théra 🌐 Théra is a Bioregional Learning Center in the southwest of France, through ecological restoration, economic self-reliance, and cultural heritage preservation. Projects include waterway restoration, affordable housing, and establishing regenerative culture facilities. Toulouse, Occitanie, France Bioregional Organizer
The Pivot 🌐 Stewarding transformational leaders of regenerative living soil projects who are vested in community thriving. Building Catalist, a tool for creating local knowledge commons. Support Provider
Territoires à Vivres 🌐 A community organization experimenting with new forms of cooperation to develop local, shared food systems that are more sustainable, more supportive, and more democratic. Montpellier, Occitanie, France Bioregional Organizer
Terran Collective 🌐 A community of practice and care working to build the cultural, technological, and governance infrastructure for a regenerative future. Terran focuses on five core areas– community weaving, regenerative technology, commons stewardship, collaborative mapping, and cultural storytelling. California St, San Francisco, CA, United States Bioregional Organizer
Tarun Bharat Sangh 🌐 A community-led water conservation organization restoring India's river systems through traditional knowledge and modern watershed management. TBS has revived 14 rivers, built thousands of water harvesting structures, and established a network of water stewards combining technical expertise with cultural revival. Rajasthan, India Bioregional Organizer
Stroomgebied Zuid-Veluwe 🌐 A collaborative network of stakeholders working to restore the relationship between people and place in the South Veluwe watershed through community gardens, regenerative agriculture, and cultural celebration that brings together diverse communities. Veluwe, Purmerend, Netherlands Bioregional Organizer
Spruce Root 🌐 Spruce Root is an Indigenous-led Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) serving 23 communities, building a regenerative regional economy by supporting Alaska Native and rural entrepreneurs. Juneau, AK, United States Bioregional Organizer
South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group 🌐 The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group is a voluntary network reimagining ecoregional and bioregional governance for South Asia, challenging rigid nation-state boundaries through biocultural perspectives. India Bioregional Organizer
Sinal do Vale 🌐 Sinal do Vale runs a bioregional hub in Brazil's Atlantic Rainforest, serving as a catalyst for regeneration through community-based entrepreneurship and systems integration. Guanabara Bay, Rua do Catimbau, Paquetá, Rio de Janeiro - RJ, 20397-210, Brazil Bioregional Organizer
Samsø Island 🌐 Samsø is a Danish island community internationally recognized for its pioneering transition to 100% renewable energy. The community achieved energy independence through a combination of wind turbines, solar, and biomass district heating systems powered by local agricultural waste, with many installations community-owned. The island has set an ambitious target to be completely fossil fuel free by 2030, initiating conversion to electric transportation starting with the marinas. Samsø, Central Denmark Region, Denmark Bioregional Organizer
Salmon Nation 🌐 A network of public benefit companies whose purpose is to inspire, enable, and invest in regenerative development across the Salmon Nation bioregion. BC, Canada Bioregional Organizer
River Ericht Catchment Restoration Initiative 🌐 Award-winning initiative bringing blended finance approaches to nature restoration, focusing on Atlantic salmon spawning grounds. Combines riparian woodland restoration, peatland protection, and habitat enhancement. Led primarily by community organizations, addressing the funding gap in nature restoration while maintaining community leadership. Bridge of Ericht, Pitlochry, Scotland, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
River Dôn Project 🌐 The River Dôn Project's fundamental approach centers on creating a framework that gives legal personhood to the river, challenging traditional dualistic views of nature and human systems as separate entities. River Don, Five Weirs Walk, Sheffield, S9 3, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
Resilience Earth 🌐 Resilience Earth is a nonprofit worker cooperative based in Catalonia, Spain, dedicated to transformative change through community resilience, regenerative design, democratic innovation and the social and solidarity economy. Olot, Catalonia, Spain Bioregional Organizer
Regenesis Group 🌐 Regenesis is a global pioneer in regenerative development, offering both consulting services and education to advance land use, community development, and the built environment beyond sustainability toward true ecological and cultural regeneration. Since its founding in 1995, Regenesis has worked to shift development practices from minimizing harm to actively contributing to the health of people and place, integrating deep systems thinking and a holistic understanding of natural and human dynamics. Learning Network
Regenerosity 🌐 Supporting grassroots, community-led organizations in threatened or degraded landscapes through capacity development grants, and by inviting members of the communities Regenerosity works with to design participatory trust-based programs according to their needs. Funder
Regenerate Cascadia 🌐 Regenerate Cascadia implements a carefully structured approach that flows from bioregion to watershed to ecoregion to local landscape, integrating indigenous knowledge and stewardship practices across the Cascadia bioregion. Cascadia, OR, United States Bioregional Organizer
Regen Sydney 🌐 Regen Sydney is taking a systemic view to help transition Sydney as a city and bioregion toward a life-affirming economy, moving from anthropocentrism to ecocentrism through neighborhood-scale programs and regenerative action. Sydney, NSW 2000, Australia Bioregional Organizer
Regen Network 🌐 Regen Network is a blockchain-based ecological accounting platform that enables the verification, issuance, and exchange of ecological assets—such as carbon credits and biodiversity outcomes—grounded in land-based regeneration. Built on the Cosmos blockchain, Regen Network supports transparent and science-based methodologies for tracking ecological health, allowing farmers, land stewards, and communities to receive compensation for verified ecosystem services. Support Provider
Regen Foundation 🌐 Regen Foundation is a nonprofit organization that supports the governance, equity, and cultural integrity of regenerative finance and ecological stewardship networks. It facilitates participatory research, funding, and capacity-building for bioregional groups, while stewarding commons-based structures that bridge blockchain innovation with community-led environmental restoration. Support Provider
Reconnecting Northland 🌐 Reconnecting Northland supports socio-ecological transformation and community-led regeneration across Northland, New Zealand. It empowers communities through long-term partnerships and localized projects that enhance biodiversity and resilience. They are working across approximately 150,000 hectares of forest, watersheds, rivers, wetlands and farmlands in Northland, with a goal of 800,000 hectares by 2050. Northland, New Zealand Bioregional Organizer
ReVillage Green Valley 🌐 ReVillage Green Valley is a place-rooted initiative reweaving community through land-based regeneration and collective care, responding to wildfires, housing displacement, ecological degradation, and social isolation. West County Museum, 261 S Main St, Sebastopol, CA 95472, United States Bioregional Organizer
ReGenerOsa Collective 🌐 ReGenerOsa is a biocultural regeneration collective led by women who are reclaiming land, language, and local governance in the face of rapid ecological degradation, cultural loss, and speculative tourism. Corcovado National Park, Costa Rica Bioregional Organizer
ReFi Uganda 🌐 ReFi Uganda brings decentralized finance and Web3 education to rural communities in Northern Uganda, empowering farmers, youth, and climate activists with financial tools that bypass traditional banking restrictions. Gulu, Uganda Bioregional Organizer
ReFi DAO 🌐 A regenerative finance ecosystem incubating local ReFi nodes, as well as learning and communicating around regenerative finance. Learning Network
Puget Sound Partnership 🌐 A state-led initiative in Washington dedicated to the long-term recovery of the Puget Sound ecosystem. Through its evolving Action Agenda, the Partnership outlines strategies and near-term actions to restore water quality, habitat, species health, and human well-being. The Partnership uses a science-based approach and tracks progress through its Vital Signs indicators, while also seeking public input to shape direction and improve accountability over time. Puget Sound Way, San Jose, CA 95133, United States Bioregional Organizer
Planet Drum 🌐 Planet Drum Foundation was founded in San Francisco, CA in 1973. In association with community activists and ecologists, they developed the concept of a bioregion. Planet Drum works to research, promote and disseminate information about bioregionalism, a grassroots approach to ecology that emphasizes sustainability, community self-determination and regional self-reliance. Support Provider
Pioneer Valley Grows 🌐 This initiative provides flexible financing and technical support to farmers and food entrepreneurs in the Pioneer Valley, helping them grow resilient, community-rooted businesses. Their Pioneer Valley Agriculture Investment Fund is a community investment fund which provides due diligence and financing. Local resident investors are drawn by the potential for a local and viable food system, creating a pool of 'social impact notes' returning 0-2% per annum, with loan amounts ranging from $1,000 to $250,000 for all types of local farm and food businesses. Springfield, MA, United States Bioregional Organizer
Panama Restoration Lab 🌐 The Panama Restoration Lab is an emerging field-building effort working to demonstrate that ecological restoration can serve as a strategic tool for climate resilience, drought mitigation, and livelihood regeneration—not only for Panama, but as a model for the world. Panama Canal, Panama Bioregional Organizer
Open Future Coalition 🌐 Open Future Coalition is a global initiative supporting place-based climate and community resilience by providing shared digital infrastructure, ecosystem-aligned funding, and coordination tools. Through the Open Impact platform, they help hundreds of organizations across dozens of countries align around shared goals, track progress, and access resources. Their work emphasizes bioregional approaches—supporting local stewards, regenerative initiatives, and landscape-level collaboration—while fostering long-term systems change through regional fellowships, learning exchanges, and the development of resilient funding models that prioritize equity, trust, and ecological integrity. Learning Network
Open Civics 🌐 OpenCivics convenes and facilitates a civic innovation network of leaders, innovators, change agents, and public servants actualized in service to the public good. Learning Network
OHAI Tonga 🌐 OHAI Tonga is a non-governmental organization working to strengthen climate resilience in the Kingdom of Tonga and across the South Pacific. Their Tongan Whale Corridor initiative establishes a whale corridor through the Tongan Archipelago as part of a broader plan to regenerate ocean ecosystems from the Arctic to the Antarctic. Building on the 2024 "He Whakaputanga Moana" declaration granting whales legal personhood, OHAI combines legal innovation, ecological stewardship, and Indigenous leadership through a Marine Keystone Species Treaty, a regional protection center, and a pioneering "Whales Bank" to finance whale conservation, treating whales as ecosystem engineers vital to ocean health and Pacific cultural identity. French Polynesia Bioregional Organizer
Northeast Bioregioning Collective 🌐 Also known as (or closely overlapping with) Alliance for the Mystic River Watershed, this collective brings together Indigenous and settler organizations across New England to regenerate land, housing, and livelihoods through Indigenous governance, ecological restoration, and regenerative infrastructure. ME, United States Bioregional Organizer
NoVo Foundation 🌐 A key funder in the bioregional ecosystem. Funder
Nila Vena Organizing Team — A tribal-led initiative focused on protecting the Nila Vena (Lake Iliamna) region through Indigenous land trusts, cultural revitalization, and economic regeneration in partnership with health and conservation institutions. Iliamna, AK, United States Bioregional Organizer
Mira River Guardians 🌐 Mira River Guardians is a collective of concerned inhabitants working to regenerate the Mira River watershed in Portugal by focusing on its potential rather than its problems. Through mapping, relationship-building, and collaborative projects, the Guardians connect diverse stakeholders to create a polycentric network of bioregional stewards addressing desertification and ecological regeneration. Odemira, Portugal Bioregional Organizer
Ma Earth 🌐 Ma Earth is a values-driven network and movement committed to reweaving humanity's relationship with the Earth through the principles of care, reciprocity, and regeneration. With an emphasis on Indigenous wisdom, sacred activism, and embodied learning, Ma Earth supports transformative change by curating immersive experiences, creating aligned media content, and funding local leaders. Support Provider
MIKIA Kenya 🌐 My Indigenous Knowledge in Action (MIKIA) works to document, preserve, and regenerate indigenous knowledge in Northern Kenya, particularly among Maasai pastoralist communities. Through intergenerational knowledge transfer, integration with scientific knowledge, and community-led initiatives, MIKIA supports climate resilience, biodiversity conservation, and indigenous self-determination. Lake Turkana Academy-Kalokol, Isiolo, Kenya Bioregional Organizer
Long Tom Watershed Council 🌐 The council takes a collaborative, science-based approach to improving water quality, fish and wildlife habitat, and community resilience. Recognized as a model watershed council in Oregon, LTWC integrates rigorous data collection with public engagement to ensure that land and water stewardship efforts are effective, inclusive, and grounded in place. LTWC, 751 S Danebo Ave, Eugene, OR 97402, United States Bioregional Organizer
Landschaftspflegeverein 🌐 Works to preserve and restore one of the most species-rich regions in Europe through network building, community involvement, and sustainable grassland management. They connect people and organizations to create a network of natural areas. Austria Bioregional Organizer
Landscapes for 1 Billion People 🌐 A global initiative that aims to restore 500 million hectares of degraded land and support one billion people through nature-based solutions. The initiative focuses on regenerative landscape approaches—such as agroforestry, farmer-managed natural regeneration, and watershed restoration—that increase food security, enhance biodiversity, and sequester carbon. Support Provider
Laconia 🌐 A bioregional network in the Peloponnese region of Greece, connecting cultural initiatives, community associations, and local creators to honor life in all its forms and create transformative change through shared cultural experiences and collaborative action. Sparta, Peloponneso, Palaiologou Konstantinou, 231 00 Sparti, Greece Bioregional Organizer
Kwiakah Nation 🌐 A small First Nation in British Columbia led by Chief Steven Dick, focused on developing an Indigenous-led, conservation-focused regenerative economy in their ancestral territory. They're implementing regenerative forestry practices, developing sustainable businesses from kelp farming to ecotourism, and creating replicable models for climate-resilient economic systems. Vancouver, BC, Canada Bioregional Organizer
Kotahitanga mō te Taiao Alliance 🌐 KMTT is a partnership of 17 iwi (Māori tribes), councils, and government agencies working collaboratively to regenerate land and sea ecosystems across the top of New Zealand's South Island. They blend Indigenous knowledge and Western science to deliver large-scale biodiversity restoration, climate adaptation, and regenerative economic development. The alliance strengthens local initiatives by creating shared strategies, aligning efforts across diverse actors, and facilitating community-led action. Lake Tekapo, Domain Rd, Lake Tekapo 7999, New Zealand Bioregional Organizer
Kogayon Association 🌐 Oltenia de sub Munte is a collective of people working to preserve heritage, living traditions, and authentic ways of life in the mountainous region of Oltenia, Romania. Through guided tours, silk weaving workshops, traditional agriculture, and community-based tourism, they bring traditions into daily life rather than transforming them into museums. Olănești, Băile Olănești, Romania Bioregional Organizer
Koalisi Ekonomi Membumi 🌐 A collective movement catalyzing sustainable investment ecosystems across Indonesia through their Value Chain Collaboration Canvas approach. KEM connects local governments, smallholder farmers, businesses, and financial institutions to build nature-based commodity value chains. Bali, Indonesia Bioregional Organizer
Inuit Circumpolar Council (ICC) 🌐 An international non-governmental organization representing approximately 180,000 Inuit across Alaska, Canada, Greenland, and Chukotka (Russia). Founded in 1977, ICC was created to unify Inuit voices across the Arctic and advocate for their shared cultural, environmental, and political interests on the global stage. With Consultative Status at the United Nations, ICC works to uphold Inuit rights, safeguard the Arctic environment, and promote self-determined development throughout the circumpolar North. Greenland Bioregional Organizer
Inabita 🌐 A bioregional initiative in central Italy's Fiastra Valley, working to strengthen collaborative relationships and shared identity between villages through cultural celebration and participatory planning. Born from the Borgofuturo festival, the initiative has evolved into "Qui ValdiFiastra"—a 15-project action plan addressing built infrastructure, culture, environmental education, and local production. Lago di Fiastra, Via Circonvallazione del Lago, 62035 Fiastra MC, Italy Bioregional Organizer
Hylo 🌐 Hylo is a community-led, prosocial coordination platform for purpose-driven groups, with a specific focus for place-based and bioregional organizing Support Provider
Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve 🌐 HKY Intercultural Reserve is an Indigenous-led nonprofit initiative in Huni Kuin territory, dedicated to restoring Amazonian ecosystems while preserving and celebrating Huni Kuin culture. It aims to create sustainable livelihoods through agroforestry and cultural revitalization. AC, Brazil Bioregional Organizer
Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort 🌐 Supports regenerative transition through a guild-based approach focused on food, shelter, ecology, and culture, working across the Hudson River estuary region to foster local collaboration, fund place-based solutions, and steward long-term bioregional resilience. New York, NY, United States Bioregional Organizer
Hansalim 🌐 Hansalim is South Korea's largest consumer-producer food cooperative, founded in the 1980s at the intersection of the pro-democracy movement and grassroots agricultural reform. What began as a small store offering pesticide-free rice and eggs has grown into a cooperative with over 900,000 members. Its model centers on a reciprocal relationship– producers safeguard consumer health through ecologically grown food, while consumers support the livelihood and dignity of farmers. Hansalim embodies a philosophy of interconnectedness—summed up in its mantra, "the universe in a bowl of rice." South Korea Bioregional Organizer
Guayabillas DAO — Pioneers a regenerative governance and financing model using blockchain technology for community-led conservation and restoration in the CABR biodiversity hotspot, combining participatory design, nested governance structures, and innovative funding mechanisms. Guayabillas, Bolívar, Colombia Bioregional Organizer
Green Releaf Initiative 🌐 Green Releaf designs and implements climate resilience programs through labs and learning journeys. They integrate indigenous knowledge, permaculture principles, and arts-based approaches to support communities recovering from climate disasters such as Category 5 supertyphoons. Their work spans watershed restoration, sustainable agriculture, and building collaborative partnerships among government, civil society, and local leaders to strengthen ecological and social resilience. Philippines Bioregional Organizer
Garrison Institute 🌐 Working toward biodiversity stewardship, ecological cultures, regenerative economies, and the common good. Organizer of bioregional efforts in the Hudson Valley through the Pathways to Planetary Health program. Garrison, NY, United States Support Provider
Forever Sabah 🌐 A 25-year transformational program facilitating Sabah's shift to a diversified, equitable circular economy. Operating as a social collaborative movement rather than a traditional NGO, they weave connections across watersheds, forests, and coastal zones. Sabah, Malaysia Bioregional Organizer
Findhorn Watershed Initiative 🌐 They practice "stealth bioregioning" - working within existing structures like the Rivers Trust while gradually introducing systemic change. Their approach integrates ecology, culture, and economics through careful relationship building and stakeholder engagement. Findhorn, Forres, Scotland, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
Fibershed 🌐 Fibershed began in California as a non-profit movement to connect regional fiber farmers, natural dyers, mills, designers, and wearers in a "farm-to-closet" value chain. The concept defines a strategic geography for fiber (a "fibershed"), similar to a foodshed, within which raw materials and textile production are linked... biodegradable textiles produced and eventually composted within the same bioregion, blending traditional crafts with innovation to meet local needs sustainably. California St, San Francisco, CA, United States Bioregional Organizer
Enlivened Cooperative 🌐 A global network (with roots in Hawaii) dedicated to reimagining learning, research, and leadership as relational, regenerative practices. Rooted in diverse knowledge systems and shaped by place, the collective designs community-led, cross-cultural learning experiences that support collective action and ecological-cultural renewal. HI, United States Bioregional Organizer
Ecosystem Restoration Communities 🌐 A global network that harnesses the collective power of individuals and communities to restore ecosystems. Support Provider
ETH Zurich 🌐 ETH Zurich, one of Europe's leading science and technology universities, includes interdisciplinary research centers and programs focused on sustainability, climate adaptation, and regenerative land use. ETH Zurich supports the design of bioregionally attuned solutions for food systems, water management, urban metabolism, and ecological restoration. Support Provider
Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium 🌐 The Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium unites a diverse collective of community leaders, Indigenous allies, and regenerative projects in Costa Rica's southern region. Their focus is on land rematriation, water protection, and grassroots bioregional governance. Costa Rica Bioregional Organizer
Design School for Regenerating the Earth 🌐 A global effort founded by Joe Brewer, focused on regenerating Earth's bioregions through place-based action and a growing planetary network of learning exchanges. Learning Network
De Plaatsen 🌐 The Dutch Foundation De Plaatsen (translated– The Places) is working with farmers and various system players (including banks, marketing cooperatives, supermarkets and citizens) on the realization of 700,000 hectares of sustainable soil management. A vital soil contributes to a better climate, more biodiversity, an attractive landscape, a healthier environment and the regionalization of our food system. There will be at least four Places for the four different soil types in the Netherlands. Cooperative "De Kleine Aarde" in Boxtel is the first Plaats in the Netherlands, focussing on the Zuidelijke Zandgronden bioregion. Boxtel, North Brabant, Netherlands Bioregional Organizer
Dark Matter Labs 🌐 Dark Matter Labs is a transdisciplinary research and design organization focused on reimagining the foundational systems of society—such as governance, finance, infrastructure, and legal frameworks—to support a regenerative and equitable future. Operating globally with a strong European base, Dark Matter Labs collaborates with cities, governments, and civil society to prototype new civic infrastructures and shift societal defaults toward long-term stewardship. Support Provider
Cuyahoga River Area of Concern (AOC) 🌐 The Cuyahoga River Area of Concern is a major restoration effort focused on bringing life back to 46 miles of river. Local and state partners are working to remove long-standing environmental impairments—like poor water quality and habitat loss—through a Remedial Action Plan. The effort is guided by a diverse coalition of agencies, nonprofits, and community members, all contributing to a healthier, more resilient watershed through practical actions like wetland restoration, dam removals, and neighborhood-scale stewardship. Cuyahoga River Bridge, Riverview Rd, Peninsula, OH 44264, United States Bioregional Organizer
Curridabat Sweet City 🌐 The city follows a distinctive "Sweet City" framework that treats pollinators and other non-human life as citizens– vital members of the urban community. This approach reimagines urban governance by recognizing biodiversity—and even emotion—as essential infrastructure. Curridabat, San Jose, Costa Rica Bioregional Organizer
Cultivate Ireland 🌐 A workers' cooperative committed to sustainability and regenerative practices based in the Cloughjordan Ecovillage. Their Community Climate Coaches initiative trains local facilitators to engage communities in climate resilience planning through a collaborative evaluation program. The program develops competency frameworks, toolkits, and training manuals for climate coaches who facilitate dialogues in four bioregional hubs. These coaches document community-led initiatives through specialized mapping systems, ensuring community perspectives directly inform local climate policy and create socially and politically binding outcomes. County Tipperary, Ireland Bioregional Organizer
Crown Managers Partnership (CMP) 🌐 A voluntary collaboration among federal, state, provincial, Tribal, and First Nation agency managers and universities spanning Montana, Alberta, and British Columbia. The partnership works across jurisdictional boundaries to address shared ecological challenges in the Crown of the Continent Ecosystem, recognizing that no single agency has the mandate or resources to independently address the region's environmental concerns. MT, United States Bioregional Organizer
Costa Rica Regenerativa 🌐 A holistic development initiative launched in 2018 that focuses on demonstrating regenerative practices as viable alternatives to extractive systems, shifting from passive to participatory societies, upholding feminine principles and cultural preservation, and supporting locally led decision-making and inclusive economic models. Guanacaste, Costa Rica Bioregional Organizer
Coral Triangle Initiative 🌐 The Coral Triangle Initiative on Coral Reefs, Fisheries and Food Security (CTI-CFF) is a multilateral partnership launched in 2009 to safeguard the rich marine and coastal ecosystems of the Coral Triangle region. Comprised of six countries—Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, and Timor-Leste—the initiative supports collaborative governance, ecosystem-based management, and community resilience across some of the most biodiverse waters on Earth. Denpasar, Indonesia Bioregional Organizer
Comunidad Indigena José Luis Caniulef 🌐 A Mapuche community in southern Chile working to document environmental changes, strengthen biocultural memory, and develop regenerative land management practices through the integration of traditional knowledge and scientific approaches. Parques de Afunalhue, 4930000 Villarrica, Araucania, Chile Bioregional Organizer
Commonland 🌐 Commonland brings people together to restore landscapes with their holistic approach to landscape restoration - the 4 Returns framework. Support Provider
Colombia Regenerativa 🌐 A network of regions working from the grassroots level to promote ecological restoration and agroecological production, focusing on returning fertility to soil and communities. The initiative collaborates with the Global Network of Regenerative Communities to share knowledge and experiences with other regions worldwide, particularly in Latin America. Colombia Bioregional Organizer
Carolinian Canada 🌐 Carolinian Canada is a cross-sector network working to regenerate the unique ecosystems of the Carolinian Zone in southern Ontario, from Toronto to Windsor. Grounded in the principles of reconciliation, the organization connects science, community, and business to support healthy landscapes, biodiversity, and climate-resilient communities. ON, Canada Bioregional Organizer
Capital Institute 🌐 This initiative brings together a global ecosystem of over 1,500 changemakers who blend modern science, storytelling, and timeless wisdom traditions to guide the shift from an extractive to a regenerative economy. Their work focuses on fostering economic systems that support the long-term well-being of people, the planet, and business. Learning Network
COBALT 🌐 Collaborative for Bioregional Action Learning & Transformation (COBALT) guides fellows through a shared journey of discovery centered on bioregionalism. Their work focuses on three core areas– immersive Bioregional Learning Journeys, the use of Data & Storytelling to deepen understanding and connection, and the development of Bioregional Digital Twins to support place-based systems thinking and decision-making. Learning Network
CEDPO 🌐 Centro Estratégico de Desarrollo de la Península de Osa (CEDPO) is a collaborative platform working to unify local, Indigenous, and institutional actors around a shared vision for a regenerative future in Costa Rica's Osa Peninsula, rooted in system mapping, community governance, and ecological stewardship. Costa Rica Bioregional Organizer
Burrenbeo Trust 🌐 The Burren Programme supports sustainable farming in western Ireland through its "pocket, head, and heart" approach– creating viable economic incentives for farmers ("pocket"), offering technical guidance and knowledge-sharing ("head"), and fostering trust and emotional connection with local communities ("heart"). Burren, Ennis, Ireland Bioregional Organizer
Buckminster Fuller Institute 🌐 The Buckminster Fuller Institute (BFI) supports systemic approaches to solving global challenges through the lens of regenerative design. In its bioregional work, BFI focuses on enabling place-based transformation by fostering "trimtab" initiatives—small, strategic interventions with outsized impact. The institute supports bioregional movements by curating learning exchanges, mapping regenerative projects, and advancing design principles rooted in local ecological and cultural systems. Support Provider
Bosque Fractal 🌐 Bosque Fractal is a biocultural regeneration initiative rooted in the Andean headwaters of Colombia, on sacred Muiska territory where male and female rivers meet. It began as a grassroots effort to protect an 80-hectare forest from private sale, and has since evolved into a living laboratory for land restoration, spiritual healing, and commons governance. Deeply grounded in place, the project weaves ecology, ritual, and relational finance into a vision of territory as teacher. Bosque Fractal does not aim to scale, but to inspire fractal repetition — a pattern of community-held regeneration that can ripple across other watersheds and sacred sites. Observatorio Astronómico de Zaquencipá: Villa de Leyva, colombia, 154247 Santa Sofía, Colombia Bioregional Organizer
Blueprint for Impact 🌐 Blueprint for Impact is a cross-sector collaborative formed in response to the catastrophic flooding of 2021 in Southern Appalachia. The initiative emerged as a grassroots response to the failures of conventional recovery systems and evolved into a long-term, community-driven effort to catalyze regeneration across multiple systems — including food, housing, health, forestry, water, and arts. Blueprint brings together over 50 organizations, practitioners, artists, healers, and policy advocates to build resilient, bioregionally grounded systems that can withstand future shocks. GA-2, Blue Ridge, GA 30513, United States Bioregional Organizer
Blue Ridge Foodshed Fund 🌐 Blue Ridge Foodshed Trust emerged in the aftermath of Hurricane Helen as a response to the mounting instability of the global food system and the deep vulnerabilities of rural communities. The Trust's mission is to localize food systems by supporting small-scale farmers, building regenerative distribution infrastructure, and addressing food access through community-centered investment. Southern Blue, 132 W Bankhead St, New Albany, MS 38652-3313, United States Bioregional Organizer
Bloom Network 🌐 Place-based social network that pays grants and dividends to bioregional and other scale place-based community development work who are members of its network. Learning Network
Bioregioning Tayside 🌐 Bioregioning Tayside takes a non-anthropocentric, place-based approach to regional regeneration, centering ecological systems alongside human communities. Guided by the principle "change the frame, change the story," the initiative aims to cultivate new narratives of place that support resilience in the face of climate disruption, biodiversity loss, and economic instability. Perth, Scotland, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
Bioregional Weaving Labs 🌐 A collective of 25+ international organizations, funders, and impact investors, (including Ashoka and Commonland) aiming to establish 10 Bioregional Weaving Labs across Europe by 2025. Learning Network
Bioregional Weaving Alliance Ireland 🌐 BWL South East Ireland is a bioregional initiative functioning as a transition hub, learning centre, and living lab. It brings together a network of Irish stakeholders, partners, and the living systems of the region itself. Established as a non-profit in 2022 and formally constituted in 2024. County Waterford, Ireland Bioregional Organizer
Bioregional Learning Centre South Devon 🌐 The Bioregional Learning Centre (BLC) in South Devon works at the intersection of geosystems, human systems, and ecosystems to build long-term climate resilience and civic capacity. Its work is structured around four key pillars– developing place-based funding ecosystems; enabling citizen-led data collection and analysis to track vitality and adaptive capacity; supporting community groups with evidence for effective representation in governance; and fostering systems thinking through education and cultural engagement. River Dart, Greenway Road, Brixham, TQ5 0, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
BioFi Project 🌐 A collective supporting bioregions to design, build, and implement Bioregional Financing Facilities that connect financial resources and regenerators. Learning Network
Bay Delta Trust 🌐 The Bay-Delta Trust, fiscally sponsored by the Buckminster Fuller Institute, operates as a weaving and convening initiative focused on improving network coordination and increasing regenerative investments in the bioregion. Their approach builds on a rich history of bioregional work in the area, including Peter Berg's influential contributions through the Planet Drum Foundation in the 1970s, which helped establish early municipal environmental programs. California St, San Francisco, CA, United States Bioregional Organizer
Barichara Regenerativa 🌐 Fundación Barichara Regenerativa aims to regenerate the regional climate system of the Northern Andes through cultural regeneration, watershed restoration, regenerative agriculture, and economic development using traditional knowledge. Barichara, Colombia Bioregional Organizer
Bangladesh Environment and Development Society 🌐 Bangladesh Environment and Development Society (BEDS) is an organization implementing integrated ecovillage solutions in Bangladesh's vulnerable coastal regions. Their holistic approach encompasses mangrove restoration, renewable energy provision, clean water systems, climate-resilient housing, and sustainable livelihoods. BEDS engages communities through participatory design processes, connecting ecological conservation with economic empowerment while preserving local cultural practices. Their work has restored 150 hectares of mangroves and provided climate adaptation benefits to thousands of families. Sundarbans, Kashipur Road, Khulna, Bangladesh Bioregional Organizer
Australian Earth Laws Alliance (AELA) 🌐 The Australian Earth Laws Alliance (AELA) is a group of volunteer lawyers connecting earth jurisprudence with indigenous knowledge to critique western industrialized systems and address root causes of ecological and social justice problems through a bioregional lens. Melbourne, VIC 3004, Australia Bioregional Organizer
Atelier LUMA 🌐 Atelier LUMA operates as a biodesign lab in the Camargue region of southern France, connecting human and non-human elements through design interventions. The organization transforms undervalued local resources into valuable materials and products while maintaining a multidisciplinary team of approximately 20 people. The Camargue, Lieu-dit Arnella, 13460 Saintes-Maries-de-la-Mer, France Bioregional Organizer
Association Française d'Agroforesterie 🌐 The Association Française d'Agroforesterie (AFAF) supports the agroecological transition across France by promoting agroforestry practices. Since 2007, it has worked with over 130 partners through research and development, farmer training, knowledge dissemination, territorial coordination, and policy engagement. Toulouse, Occitanie, France Bioregional Organizer
Anou 🌐 The Anou Cooperative is a collective of over 600 artisans representing cooperatives, associations, and workshops throughout Morocco. It is owned and operated by members of the Moroccan artisan community and is open to artisans who demonstrate a commitment to quality craftsmanship and transparent practices. Casablanca, Casablanca - Settat, Morocco Bioregional Organizer
Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance 🌐 Established in 2017, the Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance unites indigenous nationalities across Ecuador and Peru to protect their ancestral territories. The alliance operates through indigenous-led governance, cross-border collaboration, integration of traditional decision-making, and strategic partnerships between indigenous communities and NGOs to safeguard one of the Amazon's most biodiverse regions. Ecuador Bioregional Organizer
Alvelal 🌐 A collaborative alliance operating across over 1 million hectares in southeastern Spain, using Commonland's Four Returns Framework to reverse desertification and restore prosperity. Since 2014, AlVelAl Association has grown to more than 490 members including 280 farmers, developing regenerative businesses and coordinating landscape-scale restoration through the Altiplano Regeneration Manifesto, community machinery banks, and regenerative education programs that merge art, biodiversity, and emotional connection to land. 15506 County Road 22, Doniphan, NE 68832, United States Bioregional Organizer
Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest 🌐 A partnership of 25 organizations committed to collaborative action for Scotland's temperate rainforest. Formed to address one of the world's most biodiversity-depleted countries, the Alliance works to restore and double the size of Scotland's rainforest by 2045, creating a more resilient and better functioning ecosystem. Edinburgh, Scotland, United Kingdom Bioregional Organizer
Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca 🌐 Bioregional regeneration work on Mallorca operates through multiple interconnected initiatives, from the quiet facilitation work of Daniel Christian Wahl to the formal Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca formed in 2022. The work centers on cultivating conditions for deep, place-based transformation through convening, listening, and connecting. Palma, Balearic Islands, Spain Bioregional Organizer
Acción Serrana 🌐 Acción Serrana focuses on biocultural restoration, reforestation, community organizing, and building long-term ecological and cultural resilience in mountainous regions as part of the larger Acción Andina coalition. Cordoba, Argentina Bioregional Organizer
Abundance Project 🌐 The Abundance Project in the Valley of Grace is working to create a living laboratory of different kinds of economies incentivizing all kinds of regenerative activities. Located in Western Cape, South Africa, the initiative addresses deep racial tensions from apartheid, climate change impacts from deforestation, and the challenge of building regenerative systems through a stewardship-based approach rather than ownership models. Western Cape, South Africa Bioregional Organizer
ASPROCIG 🌐 This community-based organization has operated for over 32 years. The organization explicitly rejects conventional development models in favor of an aesthetics-first approach that prioritizes beauty and harmonious living. Their framework emphasizes collective knowledge construction and views humans as indivisible parts of nature rather than owners of it. They take comprehensive approach to empower younger generations through innovative governance and financial structures. 90 Chima, 232017 Chimá, Colombia Bioregional Organizer
7-Generation GTB 🌐 An initiative fostering social and ecological regeneration in the Greater Tkaronto Bioregion through a Bioregional Learning Center, portfolio of regenerative projects, and bioregional funding ecosystem, weaving together Indigenous and Western knowledge systems. Junction Area, Toronto, ON, Canada Bioregional Organizer

🟣 r3.0

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

r3.0 crowdsources open recommendations for necessary transformations across diverse fields and sectors, in response to the ecological and social collapses humanity is experiencing, in order to achieve a thriving, regenerative and distributive economy and society.

Network Affiliation

🟡 South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group 🟡 Regen Sydney

🔵 Yukon River Inter-Tribal Watershed Council

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

An Indigenous-led grassroots coalition founded in 1997, uniting 70+ First Nations and Alaska Native Tribes across the Yukon River watershed. Guided by a vision to one day safely drink water directly from the river, YRITWC works through collaborative programs in water-quality monitoring, community education, stewardship, and sustainable resource governance. Their YRITWC Water Health Indicators complements scientific water quality monitoring with "cultural indicators", including elements like the taste of fish, the behavior of indicator wildlife species, and the quality of plants used for traditional medicines—dimensions of watershed health that conventional metrics might miss but reflect deep, multi-generational knowledge of the system.

Bioregional Context

The Yukon River—stretching over 3,100 km through Alaska, Yukon, and British Columbia—drains an expansive watershed of approximately 850,000 km². It holds profound ecological and cultural significance for dozens of Indigenous nations—Gwich’in, Koyukon, Yup’ik, Tlingit, among others—whose subsistence, social structures, and spiritual traditions are intertwined with its flows and salmon migrations. The watershed faces growing threats from climate change, permafrost thaw, mining runoff, pollution, and waste management challenges.

Place
Alaska Hwy, Yukon, YT, Canada

🔵 Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative (Y2Y)

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Yellowstone to Yukon Conservation Initiative (Y2Y) is a pioneering transboundary conservation network that unites governmental bodies, Indigenous groups, NGOs, scientists, landowners, and communities across a 2,100-mile corridor spanning five U.S. states and two Canadian provinces and territories. Launched in 1993, Y2Y's mission is to maintain ecological integrity and habitat connectivity from the Greater Yellowstone ecosystem in Montana/Wyoming to the Yukon Territory. The organization’s success is reflected in a 300% increase in protected habitat since its inception, along with new corridors and species recoveries .

Bioregional Context

The Y2Y region stretches across North America's Rocky Mountains—from Wyoming through Montana, Idaho, Washington, and Oregon, into Alberta and British Columbia, and ending in Yukon Territory. Covering over 1.3 million square kilometers, it overlays the territories of 75+ Indigenous nations and stands as the continent’s most intact mountain ecosystem. The region encompasses high-mountain forests, glacial valleys, and critical watersheds essential for species migrations and climate resilience.

Place
Bozeman, MT, United States

🔵 Wij land

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

In 2016, Wij.land started exploring how the 4 Returns can help restore ecological functions of the peat meadow landscape in the Vechtstreek. This has grown into a learning network of almost 200 cattle farmers today who are futureproofing their farm by learning how to work more and more alongside nature and in connection with the landscape. It plays a key role in connecting different stakeholders in the landscape to create natural, social, and financial value and to inspire others in joining this cause.

Bioregional Context

Typical for Holland, peat meadows are home to cows, windmills and (migrating) bird populations. However, the intensification of farming activities and systemic dewatering of peat soils are turning the Dutch western peat meadows into 'green deserts' where both nature and farm economics are under pressure.

Place
The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

🔵 Whanganui River

Bioregional Organizer

In New Zealand, the Whanganui River catchment forms the basis for both ecological governance and cultural identity for local Māori iwi (tribes). The recognition of the river as a legal person (Te Awa Tupua) in 2017 acknowledged both its ecological significance and its central place in Māori cultural identity and governance traditions.

Bioregional Context

The Whanganui River catchment lies in the central-western North Island of Aotearoa New Zealand and encompasses the country's third-longest river. The catchment includes rugged native forests, volcanic highlands, and remote rural communities, stretching from the central plateau near Mount Tongariro to the Tasman Sea.

Place
Whanganui, Manawatu Whanganui, New Zealand
links
https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13642987.2024.2314532#d1e1529

🔵 Wellspring Commons

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Wellspring Commons is a bioregionally grounded initiative dedicated to cultivating mutually thriving relationships between human communities and the larger web of life—from protecting ecological lifelines and developing local food systems, to building a forest-based materialshed and funding ecosystems for regenerative action. Wellspring Commons also contributes to a growing knowledge commons by sharing tools, information, and practices that align with the limits and generative capacities of place.

Bioregional Context

A region spanning the northeastern U.S. and Canada, characterized by its mixed hardwood forests, glacially carved landscapes, and a legacy of agrarian and industrial land use. While increasingly threatened by climate disruption, development, and ecological fragmentation, the area retains significant biodiversity, intact ecosystems, and deep cultural memory.

Place
White Mountain National Forest, 719 N Main St, Laconia, NH 03246-2741, United States

🔵 Théra

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Théra is a Bioregional Learning Center in the southwest of France, through ecological restoration, economic self-reliance, and cultural heritage preservation. Projects include waterway restoration, affordable housing, and establishing regenerative culture facilities.

Bioregional Context

Southwest France is known for its rural character, strong agricultural traditions (including viticulture, livestock, and mixed farming), and cultural identity rooted in Occitan and Basque heritage. The region's ecology ranges from coastal wetlands and river valleys to upland forests and mountain pastures, making it rich in biodiversity but also vulnerable to climate-related pressures.

Place
Toulouse, Occitanie, France

🟣 The Pivot

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Stewarding transformational leaders of regenerative living soil projects who are vested in community thriving. Building Catalist, a tool for creating local knowledge commons.

🔵 Territoires à Vivres

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A community organization experimenting with new forms of cooperation to develop local, shared food systems that are more sustainable, more supportive, and more democratic.

Bioregional Context

Montpellier is a historic city known for its vibrant university community and Mediterranean climate, Montpellier is a hub for agricultural research, environmental science, and urban innovation. The surrounding region faces increasing pressures from drought, biodiversity loss, and rural land abandonment, while the city itself grapples with urban expansion and social inequality.

Place
Montpellier, Occitanie, France

🔵 Terran Collective

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A community of practice and care working to build the cultural, technological, and governance infrastructure for a regenerative future. Terran focuses on five core areas– community weaving, regenerative technology, commons stewardship, collaborative mapping, and cultural storytelling.

Bioregional Context

The California Bay Area is a globally influential region known for its cultural diversity, ecological richness, and technological innovation. Historically inhabited by Ohlone and other Indigenous peoples, the Bay Area is shaped by both a legacy of extractive development and a vibrant history of social movements.

Place
California St, San Francisco, CA, United States

🟡 Tarun Bharat Sangh

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A community-led water conservation organization restoring India's river systems through traditional knowledge and modern watershed management. TBS has revived 14 rivers, built thousands of water harvesting structures, and established a network of water stewards combining technical expertise with cultural revival.

Bioregional Context

Rajasthan is India's largest state by area and one of its most water-scarce regions, characterized by arid and semi-arid landscapes with minimal annual rainfall (200-400mm). The region's water crisis is exacerbated by depleting groundwater tables, desertification, and increasingly unpredictable monsoon patterns due to climate change.

Place
Rajasthan, India
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit|https://www.ceew.in/sustainable-agriculture-initiatives/tarun-bharat-sangh-johad-water-conservation-harvesting-and-revitalisation

Tags

💧 Watershed_Repair ⛲ Water_Management 🔘 Governance_Bodies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 🎒 Learning_Programs 📣 Political_Advocacy 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🪢 Network_Weaving 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices

Background


Rajasthan is India's largest state by area and one of its most water-scarce regions: arid and semi-arid country with typically 200–400mm of rain per year. Falling groundwater, desertification, and a more erratic monsoon deepen the crisis. Harvesting traditions that had sustained communities for generations were neglected in recent decades, contributing to farm decline and outmigration.

Historically, the Alwar region was water-rich into the early twentieth century. Communities practiced rainwater harvesting through johads—earthen check dams that hold runoff so it can soak in and recharge aquifers. By the 1980s scarcity was severe; parts of the region were “dark zones” in state irrigation records—groundwater critically low.

Land grew unsuitable for farming or herding, so many working-age men left for cities, leaving women and elders behind. Villages turned to Tarun Bharat Sangh after barren fields and collapsing livelihoods had already done deep harm.

Origins and Network


From 1985, Tarun Bharat Sangh (TBS), a Rajasthan-based NGO, focused on water conservation, beginning in Gopalpura in the Alwar area. The team stayed in the village to understand conditions. An elder described the old johad approach—still sound, but weakened by years without resources, backing, or confidence.

The first johad, chabootre wala johad, was built in 1985 to show the method still worked. When it held water and some farmers could grow bajra again, the team added mewalon ka baandh in 1986. Both structures helped refill wells, recharge groundwater, and restore fields.

Activities

🔸

Watershed Restoration and River Revival

 🔸

Cultural Revival for Ecological Kinship

 🔸

Community-Led Governance

 🔸

Water Literacy and Education

 🔸

Water Warriors

 🔸

National and International Advocacy

Vision and Legacy


The programme still prioritizes highly marginalized communities—currently including Scheduled Tribe and Scheduled Caste villages in Rajasthan’s Karauli district—showing how traditional knowledge, community ownership, and long commitment can move landscapes from dry wells toward flowing water and from despair toward hope.

TBS describes five decades of work with Rajasthan communities as helping revive 14 rivers, raise forest cover by 30%, return about 70% of once-barren land to cultivation, and protect five rivers from encroachment—bringing water security to over one million families. With water came more food security and livelihood; agriculture and milk production rose in 100% of project areas and farmer incomes doubled in participating villages, giving young people reason to stay or return.

As Rajendra Singh and Indira Khurana write in “Rejuvenation of Rivers”: “Change is possible. Through love and respect: Love for nature and respect for communities, and then nurturing of both. Through understanding the terrain – the endless recharge possibilities that the fractured rocks offer – and then healing the environment through appropriate water conservation structures and involving the people and their contribution. Lastly, through maintaining transparency.”

Sources

📄

Water Management Programme- Tarun Bharat Sangh

 📊

Bioregional Governance - Asian Case Studies & Framework

🔵 Stroomgebied Zuid-Veluwe

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A collaborative network of stakeholders working to restore the relationship between people and place in the South Veluwe watershed through community gardens, regenerative agriculture, and cultural celebration that brings together diverse communities.

Bioregional Context

The Zuid-Veluwe bioregion features wooded hills and fertile valleys shaped by glacial activity. Water flows are polluted and diverted, land is exhausted, and social connections are fragmenting, requiring restoration of both ecological health and community relationships.

Place
Veluwe, Purmerend, Netherlands
links

🟡 Spruce Root

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Spruce Root is an Indigenous-led Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) serving 23 communities, building a regenerative regional economy by supporting Alaska Native and rural entrepreneurs.

Bioregional Context

Southeast Alaska is a coastal and island-rich region spanning over 500 miles from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south. This bioculturally significant area is the ancestral and present-day homeland of the Tlingit, Haida, and Tsimshian peoples, whose governance, economies, and lifeways are deeply tied to the region's forests, salmon streams, and marine ecosystems.

Place
Juneau, AK, United States
links
https://www.biofi.earth/biofi-book

Tags

✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🪢 Network_Weaving 🎒 Learning_Programs 🔀 Grants_Programs 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 💬 Peer_Learning

Background


Southeast Alaska is a coastal and island-rich region spanning over 500 miles from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south. This bioculturally significant area is the ancestral and present-day homeland of the Tlingit, Haida, and Tsimshian peoples, whose governance, economies, and lifeways are deeply tied to the region's forests, salmon streams, and marine ecosystems—relationships forged over thousands of years.

The region includes part of the Tongass National Forest, the largest temperate rainforest in North America, and faces a complex legacy of colonial land policies, resource extraction, and economic displacement.

Origins and Network


In 2012, Spruce Root emerged in Juneau, Alaska, as an Indigenous-led Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) with a mission that went far beyond traditional lending. Founded with $500,000 in seed funding from Sealaska Corporation, one of the regional Alaska Native corporations created by the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, Spruce Root was born from a vision of economic empowerment that honored the deep relationships between people, place, and prosperity.

That initial funding came from an unusual source: carbon credits from 176,000 acres of Tongass rainforest that Sealaska had set aside for carbon sequestration in partnership with The Nature Conservancy. After dominating the timber industry in the region until 2021, when Sealaska renounced commercial logging, this marked a profound shift—from extraction to regeneration, from short-term profit to long-term stewardship.

Today, Spruce Root serves 23 communities across Southeast Alaska, from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south.

Activities

🔸

Small Business Lending

 🔸

Business Coaching & Programs

 🔸

Path to Prosperity

 🔸

Resilience Circles

 🔸

Sustainable Southeast Partnership

 🔸

Strategic Planning

 🔸

Seacoast Trust

Vision and Legacy

What Spruce Root has built over more than a decade is something rare: a financial institution that sees its role not just as providing capital, but as cultivating economic sovereignty rooted in place and culture. They've created pathways for Indigenous entrepreneurs to build businesses that honor their values, strengthen their communities, and ensure long-term resilience.

Their work recognizes that in Southeast Alaska—a region with a complex legacy of colonial land policies, resource extraction, and economic displacement—economic development must be regenerative, not extractive. It must honor the 10,000-plus years of Indigenous history, traditions, and stewardship. It must build businesses that strengthen communities, not businesses that extract wealth and leave.

From the $900,000 in loans deployed in 2023 to the 23 communities served across 500 miles, from the Resilience Circles supporting entrepreneurs' well-being to the Seacoast Trust's vision of $100 million in permanent funding—Spruce Root is demonstrating what bioregional finance can look like when it's Indigenous-led, place-rooted, and values-aligned.

They're showing that economic development doesn't have to mean choosing between prosperity and place, between profit and principles. In Southeast Alaska, they're building an economy where businesses succeed by strengthening communities, where financial success is measured by the health of the land and the well-being of the people who call it home.

Sources

📄

Strengthening Southeast Alaska’s Businesses — Together

 📄

Resilience Circles helps Indigenous Entrepreneurs to Scale Their Business Up

 📚

Bioregional Financing Facilities Book

🟡 South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group is a voluntary network reimagining ecoregional and bioregional governance for South Asia, challenging rigid nation-state boundaries through biocultural perspectives.

Bioregional Context

South Asia faces multiple intersecting crises—climate chaos, rising nationalism, border conflicts, and ecological degradation. The region's watersheds, like the Ganges, flow through multiple countries, divided by arbitrary borders that fragment ecological and cultural flows.

Place
India
links
https://youtu.be/XFZpg92MIfQ

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 💬 Peer_Learning 🎒 Learning_Programs 🍥 Governance_Design

Background


Across South Asia, climate disruption, nationalism, border conflict, and ecological decline overlap. At the same time, thousands of grassroots initiatives are working for change.

A political map fractures the subcontinent into seven or eight countries—lines drawn by empire and colonial history. The same territory read through watersheds and culture looks different: the Ganges crosses three or four countries, its basin sliced by borders. An upstream state can dam a river with little regard for people, wildlife, or ecology downstream.

Origins and Network


In October 2019, at a Democracy Vikalp Sangam—an Alternatives Confluence in India—participants already gathered to talk about democracy asked why South Asia could not host a bioregional process of its own.

The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group formed from that question. COVID-19 soon blocked in-person meetings, but work continued online: people kept meeting digitally to reimagine South Asia from biocultural starting points and to question nation-state lines that split ecological flows, cultural exchange, and long-standing economic ties.

The group pursues several aims. One is co-creating knowledge and awareness—advanced partly through reports now feeding modest advocacy. A larger unfinished task is helping communities on the ground reimagine their ecoregions and bioregions and, from there, influencing governance.

Activities

🔸

Learning from Practical Experiments

 🔸

Bioregional Mapping of India

 🔸

Vision for 2100

Vision and Legacy


The South Asia Bioregionalism Working Group represents a growing movement of people across South Asia who believe that reimagining governance from a biocultural perspective isn't just an interesting idea—it's essential for survival in a climate-changed world where rigid political boundaries will only create more conflict and suffering.

They've produced three reports on reimagining South Asia from a bioregional perspective, with another one or two coming out. They've organized webinars learning from bioregional approaches across the world. They're hosting a training course—a certificate course on bioregionalism with about 25 participants, put together by their collaborator ATREE with the bioregionalism working group and some of their other organizations.

They're asking fundamental questions: What if governance matched ecological boundaries? What if cultural flows weren't severed by political lines? What if the people who have lived in these bioregions for centuries could once again move, trade, and care for these landscapes as they did before colonization divided them?

The answers to these questions are still emerging. But in the work of people like Ashish, Nisha, Sonam, and Utham—in their mapping, their research, their visioning, their patient building of relationships across borders—we can see the seeds of a different future taking root.

Sources

🎬

r3.0 Bioregional Open Dialogue 3 - South Asia Bioregional Working Group

Network Affiliation

🟣 r3.0

🟡 Sinal do Vale

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Sinal do Vale runs a bioregional hub in Brazil's Atlantic Rainforest, serving as a catalyst for regeneration through community-based entrepreneurship and systems integration.

Bioregional Context

Guanabara Bay is one of Brasil's largest and most iconic coastal ecosystems, framed by the Atlantic Forest and surrounded by one of the world's most densely populated urban areas. Once rich in biodiversity and cultural significance, the bay now faces severe pollution from untreated sewage, industrial waste, and urban runoff.

Place
Guanabara Bay, Rua do Catimbau, Paquetá, Rio de Janeiro - RJ, 20397-210, Brazil
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🔀 Grants_Programs 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 💧 Watershed_Repair 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 💲 Structuring_and_Capitalization

Background


Guanabara Bay is one of Brasil's largest and most iconic coastal ecosystems, framed by the Atlantic Forest and surrounded by one of the world's most densely populated urban areas. Once rich in biodiversity and cultural significance, the bay now faces severe pollution from untreated sewage, industrial waste, and urban runoff.

For over 15 years, Sinal do Vale has earned the trust and reputation in this bioregion—situated in the buffer zone between Rio de Janeiro and the Mata Atlântica rainforest. The beautiful Mata Atlântica has incredible biodiversity, but has already lost 88% of its size to date. This bioregion delivers the water, food, and other ecosystem services for Rio and surrounding areas, yet it faces urban encroachment, loss of rivers, and low economic activity leading to poverty.

Origins and Network


Against that backdrop, Sinal do Vale set out years ago to show how a biohub can act as a catalyst inside a bioregion and help grow a bioeconomy.

After 15 years of strong results on very limited budgets, the organization is now working toward self-sufficiency so it can sustain and expand that work for the bioregion.

Activities

🔸

Biocultural Hub

 🔸

Madre Frutas Social Enterprise

 🔸

Camino do Guaraú Eco-Tourism Trail

 🔸

Val Estrela Historic and Cultural Ecotourism Site

 🔸

Terra Umida Agroforestry Corridor

 🔸

SINAL Flow Fund

Vision and Legacy


What Sinal do Vale demonstrates is how a biohub can be a catalyst that creates the conditions for regeneration to flourish across an entire bioregion. For over 15 years, working with very limited financial resources yet achieving amazing results, they've shown what's possible when you anchor action in place, build trust with communities, and weave connections across scales from individual projects to regional bioeconomy.

From the women turning jackfruit into livelihoods at Madre Frutas to the quilombola communities restoring Val Estrela, from the landless farmers regenerating Terra Umida through agroforestry to the protection of golden lion tamarins in the Mata Atlântica—Sinal do Vale is showing how supply chains of value can regenerate landscapes that are so important for culture, for people, and for the web of life that connects us all.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🔵 Samsø Island

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Samsø is a Danish island community internationally recognized for its pioneering transition to 100% renewable energy. The community achieved energy independence through a combination of wind turbines, solar, and biomass district heating systems powered by local agricultural waste, with many installations community-owned. The island has set an ambitious target to be completely fossil fuel free by 2030, initiating conversion to electric transportation starting with the marinas.

Bioregional Context

Samsø is a small island in the Kattegat Sea between Denmark's Jutland peninsula and Zealand, with a population of around 3,600 residents. Traditionally reliant on agriculture and fishing, the island faced economic decline before reinventing itself through a community-led energy transition.

Place
Samsø, Central Denmark Region, Denmark

🟡 Salmon Nation

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A network of public benefit companies whose purpose is to inspire, enable, and invest in regenerative development across the Salmon Nation bioregion.

Bioregional Context

The Salmon Nation bioregion spans a vast territory defined by the traditional range of wild Pacific salmon in North America, stretching from the Salinas River in California northward to the North Slopes of Alaska, unified by salmon watersheds.

Place
BC, Canada
links
https://colabs.studio/|https://www.edgeprize.org/|https://magiccanoe.org/|https://www.salmonnation.net/|https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://www.salmonreturns.com/

Tags

💧 Watershed_Repair 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🔀 Grants_Programs ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 🪢 Network_Weaving 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🗂️ Portfolio_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation

Background


For thousands of years, the salmon's annual return defined the rhythm of life for the Indigenous peoples of North America, who developed rich cultural traditions around this natural abundance. This place—where salmon and human communities have been intertwined for millennia—represents a unique bioregion now known as Salmon Nation. Today, 30 million people live within the historical range of wild Pacific salmon, citizens of this nation whether they realize it or not. They inherit the benefits of an ecosystem that, when healthy, sustains not just salmon but entire watersheds and the communities that depend on them.

The underlying principle is elegantly simple yet profound: healthy runs of wild Pacific salmon, as they journey from mountain streams to ocean and back again, connect entire watersheds and serve as a comprehensive indicator of bioregional health. When human systems—agriculture, forestry, utilities, transportation—align with natural systems, salmon flourish. And when salmon flourish, so do we.

Salmon represent what one might call a Nature State as distinct from nation-states—a place defined by natural systems that has always existed, transcending political boundaries. The salmon's return represents the greatest nutrient transfer between habitats on Earth, nourishing forests, entire food webs, and future generations. This principle has proven transformative: in the Okanagan region, a remarkable alliance including three First Nations has revitalized sockeye salmon populations from a mere 3,000 fish 25 years ago to 800,000 in 2024. These returning salmon now represent 70% of the sockeye in the once-mighty Columbia River basin, simultaneously restoring ecological function and cultural connections.

Origins and Network


The concept of Salmon Nation emerged from three decades of conservation work at Ecotrust, founded by Spencer Beebe in 1991. Through work with First Nations and rural communities across the bioregion, Ecotrust developed deep relationships and insights about bioregional systems thinking. A formative early project involved the Haisla First Nation's protection of the Kitlope River Watershed—nearly a million acres protected through the first co-management agreement in British Columbia, an experience that shaped understanding of the inseparability of ecological protection, cultural revival, and community well-being.

In 2019, this vision crystallized into Salmon Nation Trust, a public benefit LLC designed to invest in bioregional institutional infrastructure supporting regenerative development. Governed by public-benefit-oriented Trustees, the Trust channels values-aligned capital from philanthropic and mission-driven investors who do not expect financial return. The Trust serves as the foundational organization for the entire Salmon Nation ecosystem, incubating and spawning bioregional ventures while operating on the principle that healthy ecosystems and thriving communities are inseparable.

Throughout the bioregion, Salmon Nation works with what they call edge communities—smaller communities, Indigenous communities, and rural communities at the edges of modern society. In these places, they find the Ravens– smart, tough, tenacious individuals who have said, "That old industrial system doesn't work for me, and I'm going to do it differently." This philosophy recognizes that in moments of disequilibrium, it's often those at the margins who demonstrate the most innovative approaches to regenerative living.

Activities

🔸

Storytelling of Ravens

 🔸

Magic Canoe

 🔸

Edge Prize

 🔸

Salmon Returns

 🔸

Salmon Biocultural Credit Program

Vision and Legacy


Salmon Nation is a movement built on the recognition that when salmon flourish, so do we. Through Storytelling of Ravens, Magic Canoe, Edge Prize, and Salmon Returns, Salmon Nation continues to weave together a network of changemakers, flow capital to regeneration, and demonstrate that finance can be an act of cultural and ecological healing. As the network learns to "paddle together" in the magic canoe, it offers a model for how bioregional organizations can incubate, spawn, and support regenerative transformation.

🟣 BioFi Project

🔵 River Ericht Catchment Restoration Initiative

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Award-winning initiative bringing blended finance approaches to nature restoration, focusing on Atlantic salmon spawning grounds. Combines riparian woodland restoration, peatland protection, and habitat enhancement. Led primarily by community organizations, addressing the funding gap in nature restoration while maintaining community leadership.

Bioregional Context

Fed by upland burns from the Grampian Mountains, the catchment encompasses a mix of moorland, farmland, and wooded riverbanks. Historically important for textile milling and local agriculture, the River Ericht supports Atlantic salmon, otters, and other native species, but faces pressures from land-use change, barriers to fish migration, and climate-related flooding.

Place
Bridge of Ericht, Pitlochry, Scotland, United Kingdom

 🔸
