🔵 River Dôn Project

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The River Dôn Project's fundamental approach centers on creating a framework that gives legal personhood to the river, challenging traditional dualistic views of nature and human systems as separate entities.

Bioregional Context

The River Don flows through South Yorkshire, England, with Sheffield serving as a major urban center in its watershed. The river has deep cultural significance, with its name etymologically connected to the Celtic mother goddess Dôn.

Place
River Don, Five Weirs Walk, Sheffield, S9 3, United Kingdom

🟡 Resilience Earth

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Resilience Earth is a nonprofit worker cooperative based in Catalonia, Spain, dedicated to transformative change through community resilience, regenerative design, democratic innovation and the social and solidarity economy.

Bioregional Context

Located in Northern Catalonia, La Garrotxa encompasses 21 municipalities with approximately 60,000 residents. It's a mountainous comarca best known for its dramatic volcanic landscape, featuring over 40 dormant volcanic cones, lush forests, and fertile valleys.

Place
Olot, Catalonia, Spain
links
https://youtu.be/VEORXmWyKkE

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🔘 Governance_Bodies 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


Located in Northern Catalonia, La Garrotxa encompasses 21 municipalities with approximately 60,000 residents. It's a mountainous comarca (county) best known for its dramatic volcanic landscape. The region features over 40 dormant volcanic cones, lush forests, and fertile valleys shaped by ancient lava flows. La Garrotxa has a strong tradition of environmental stewardship, sustainable tourism, and local food systems.

Origins and Network


Resilience Earth started eight years ago when two co-founders, Erika and Oscar, began working in La Garrotxa. Today, they're a nonprofit worker cooperative of 14 people, but their approach remains fundamentally the same: working from the edge, focusing on centers rather than boundaries, building theory from practice rather than applying frameworks.

They've discovered bioregioning through doing. As Oscar reflects, "We're going really bottom up. We're not following a lot of the theoretical framework—we're kind of building one little by little as we evolve and as we work more on our territories."

Philosophy & Approach


After seven years of practice, their most useful tool became “landscape units”—a framework developed by Joan M. at the University of Girona. La Garrotxa has 2.5 landscape units, each usually spanning four to six municipalities with shared characteristics. Units can act as centers because municipalities within them are tightly interlinked. River work might span seven or eight units; ecosystem work gathers the units around a feature. The frame flexes with strategy and goal.

They focus on managing interconnectivity. From that angle, boundaries create more problems than clarity; what counts are centers—places where relationships and coherence emerge.

They set out to root change in one place—under their feet in La Garrotxa—and to convene actors around a shared aim rather than lead alone.

Activities

🔸

Beuda Democratic Innovation Project

 🔸

La Garrotxa 2030 Strategic Planning Initiative

 🔸

Democracy Festival (Festival Iltir)

 🔸

Local Government Incubator (INCAT)

Vision and Legacy


Resilience Earth brings more than eight years of practice and a theoretical model refined in use. Two years ago the Barcelona provincial government asked them to define territorial resilience; they used the commission to consolidate learnings into a framework that continues to guide the work.

Their through-line is resilience thinking, interconnectivity, and centers over boundaries. They have shown democracy shifting in small towns, strategy emerging from broad participation, and—when governance is truly shared—politics edging toward service rather than capture.

Their experience suggests bioregional governance can change how people relate to place, how decisions are made, and how communities coordinate from the smallest municipality to the full bioregion—democracy practiced by the people who live there.

Sources

🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 6

 📊

Bioregional Governance - European Case Studies & Framework

🟣 Regenesis Group

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

Regenesis is a global pioneer in regenerative development, offering both consulting services and education to advance land use, community development, and the built environment beyond sustainability toward true ecological and cultural regeneration. Since its founding in 1995, Regenesis has worked to shift development practices from minimizing harm to actively contributing to the health of people and place, integrating deep systems thinking and a holistic understanding of natural and human dynamics.

🟣 Regenerosity

Funder
🌐 Visit website

Supporting grassroots, community-led organizations in threatened or degraded landscapes through capacity development grants, and by inviting members of the communities Regenerosity works with to design participatory trust-based programs according to their needs.

🟡 Regenerate Cascadia

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Regenerate Cascadia implements a carefully structured approach that flows from bioregion to watershed to ecoregion to local landscape, integrating indigenous knowledge and stewardship practices across the Cascadia bioregion.

Bioregional Context

Cascadia spans from Southeast Alaska to Cape Mendocino in California, extending east to the Yellowstone Caldera. Defined by the watersheds of the Fraser and Columbia Rivers, it includes 15 major watersheds and 75 distinct ecoregions, with 80% of its 18 million population living on just 12% of the land area.

Place
Cascadia, OR, United States
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://youtu.be/Vgt9g24gi7Q|https://youtu.be/6w_kCz9oZ_8|https://regeneratecascadia.org/

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🎒 Learning_Programs 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🔀 Grants_Programs 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 🔘 Governance_Bodies ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles

Background


The Cascadia bioregion stretches roughly 2,500 miles from Southeast Alaska to Cape Mendocino, California, reaching east to the Yellowstone Caldera. The Fraser and Columbia watersheds frame 15 major watersheds and 75 ecoregions. The Cascadia subduction zone has shaped this landscape for millennia—home to more than a thousand generations who have lived bioregionally, with Indigenous peoples stewarding the land since time immemorial.

Today 18 million people live here; 80% occupy about 12% of the land, mostly along the Seattle–Portland–Vancouver corridor. That concentration forms a “thin green line” with outsized influence—between interior extractive economies and markets across the Pacific.

Origins and Network


Brandon Letsinger’s path with Cascadia began in 2005. He founded the Department of Bioregion, a Seattle-based 501(c)(3) with a mission of “a world of interconnected bioregions and bioregional movements.”

At the first Salmon Nation Edge Prize in 2023, he met Claire Atwell—community artist, weaver, and organizer in Victoria, British Columbia. Together they asked how to regenerate a whole watershed, ecoregion, and bioregion across jurisdictional lines.

Regenerate Cascadia runs as a program inside the Department of Bioregion and is growing a charitable society in British Columbia to support cross-border organizing.

Their guiding question is practical and sharp: If $100 million reached their landscapes tomorrow, would they be ready to receive it? Would it reach the communities and ecosystems doing regenerative work, on their own terms? So far, the answer has largely been no.

They work through nested frames—bioregion → watershed → ecoregion → landscape. Brandon describes watersheds and ecoregions as rooms in a house: the bioregion is the house; ecoregions are not the whole system alone. Landscapes within watersheds are where people step outside and connect. The aim is to link action across scales so people see work happening, learn from neighbors, and build coherence within and between places.

Activities

🔸

Regional Activation Tour

 🔸

Hubs and Guilds Development Program

 🔸

Cascadia Bioregion Program

 🔸

Cascadia Regeneration Fund

Vision and Legacy


They are forming an investor design circle and a funder community of practice from the recent Bioregion Conference and preparing collaborative financing forums for mid-2026. The aim is federated grant processes—coalitions around shared landscape visions—rather than isolated, project-by-project competition.

Regenerate Cascadia’s story is still unfolding: nested mapping from bioregion to doorstep, hubs and guilds that hold knowledge and money closer to place, and a regeneration fund meant to behave like the watersheds it serves. The open question remains whether Cascadia can organize quickly enough—in governance, relationships, and finance—for major investment to land where ecological and community work actually happens.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Cascadia Across Scales

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Regen Sydney

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Regen Sydney is taking a systemic view to help transition Sydney as a city and bioregion toward a life-affirming economy, moving from anthropocentrism to ecocentrism through neighborhood-scale programs and regenerative action.

Bioregional Context

Regen Sydney operates in the Sydney Basin Bioregion, on the lands of the Gadigal people. Sydney is a metropolitan city facing challenges of transgressing biophysical boundaries while not meeting social thresholds, needing to move into the safe space of the doughnut.

Place
Sydney, NSW 2000, Australia
links
https://youtu.be/ntsUgQDkts0

Tags

🍩 Doughnut_Assessment 🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background


Regen Sydney works in the Sydney Basin Bioregion on the lands of the Gadigal people. Like many wealthy cities, Sydney overshoots ecological limits while still falling short on social foundations—the “doughnut” gap between planetary boundaries and fair social outcomes.

The bioregion mixes neighborhoods, catchments such as the Cooks River, and the wider metro area. Regen Sydney treats bioregions as open and changing—especially under climate stress—and edges between bioregions as seams of connection, not walls. Everyday rhythms—tides, seasonal bird movements, trees turning—offer handles for reconnection at neighborhood scale.

Origins and Network


Regen Sydney takes a systemic view: help shift toward a life-affirming economy by linking people already pursuing that shift, and—across generations—move Sydney as city and bioregion into the doughnut’s safe space.

It acts mainly as convenor: creating conditions for those conversations and engagements. The neighborhood scale matters because people can feel community and living systems there in concrete ways.

Activities

🔸

Sydney Doughnut Economy

 🔸

Waverley Community of Practice

 🔸

Regenerative Action in Thematic Areas

 🔸

Deep Economic Code Work

Vision and Legacy


Regen Sydney works across three layers—neighborhood programs, thematic regenerative action, and deep economic code—toward civic infrastructure of interdependence.

The shift they seek is from passive consumption to active citizenship: not a vote every few years but ongoing responsibility as custodians of place and community. Through that work they hope to surface agency, connect allies, grow a movement, and clarify complementary roles.

The through-line remains: transition toward a life-affirming economy, linking people already on that path, and—over generations—moving Sydney as city and bioregion into the doughnut’s safe space, meeting social needs without breaching ecological limits.

Network Affiliation

🟣 r3.0

🟣 Regen Network

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Regen Network is a blockchain-based ecological accounting platform that enables the verification, issuance, and exchange of ecological assets—such as carbon credits and biodiversity outcomes—grounded in land-based regeneration. Built on the Cosmos blockchain, Regen Network supports transparent and science-based methodologies for tracking ecological health, allowing farmers, land stewards, and communities to receive compensation for verified ecosystem services.

🟣 Regen Foundation

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Regen Foundation is a nonprofit organization that supports the governance, equity, and cultural integrity of regenerative finance and ecological stewardship networks. It facilitates participatory research, funding, and capacity-building for bioregional groups, while stewarding commons-based structures that bridge blockchain innovation with community-led environmental restoration.

Activities

🔸

Biocultural Jaguar Credits Program

🟡 Reconnecting Northland

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Reconnecting Northland supports socio-ecological transformation and community-led regeneration across Northland, New Zealand. It empowers communities through long-term partnerships and localized projects that enhance biodiversity and resilience. They are working across approximately 150,000 hectares of forest, watersheds, rivers, wetlands and farmlands in Northland, with a goal of 800,000 hectares by 2050.

Bioregional Context

Northland (Te Tai Tokerau) forms the narrow peninsula at the northern tip of New Zealand's North Island, surrounded by the Pacific Ocean and Tasman Sea. The region features diverse ecosystems including coastal wetlands, kauri forests, and dune lakes, but has experienced significant habitat fragmentation and biodiversity loss through colonial-era deforestation and agricultural conversion. Despite ecological degradation, Northland retains the highest biodiversity in New Zealand and has the country's highest proportion of Māori population.

Place
Northland, New Zealand
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 💧 Watershed_Repair

Background

Northland (Te Tai Tokerau) holds Aotearoa New Zealand’s highest native biodiversity, yet decline continues: underfunded volunteer efforts, especially in low-income communities, cannot match the pace of loss. Short, steep rivers could recover quickly if sediment and habitat stress were addressed systemically, but catchments remain degraded.

A large Māori population carries deep whakapapa to whenua—both a demand for justice in resource decisions and a reservoir of kaitiakitanga for regeneration.

Climate shocks and eroding ecosystems now answer decades of underinvestment in care. Central agencies work in silos; electoral cycles chop programmes before they root. Communities often see what is needed yet lack capital, coordination, and time to sustain action.

Origins and Network


Whenua ora, waiora, tangata ora — Land, Water, and People Flourishing Together for the Northland Bioregion of Aotearoa.

This is Reconnecting Northland's vision: a future where thriving ecosystems are the cornerstone of sustainable communities and regenerative local economies. As a Connectivity Catalyst, Reconnecting Northland inspires the connections and weaves the relationships required for community-led socio-ecological regeneration to occur across large landscapes.

They imagine how community environmental efforts can evolve into innovative, connected, resilient, and financially sustainable ventures—capable of working together to heal the social and environmental fragmentation inhibiting Northland's prosperity.

The movement toward sustainable communities follows a structured, phased approach.
1. In Phase 1, they facilitate community visioning and planning by supporting intergenerational dialogue and advising on the Connectivity Action Plan (CAP) process, while helping establish local governance structures.
2. Phase 2 focuses on resourcing and supporting community needs by applying Te Kete Hononga to address training, service access, and capacity building, ultimately growing local capability.
3. In Phase 3, they align funding and investment opportunities with the CAP and foster investment partnerships for local nature-based enterprises.
4. Phase 4 involves the implementation of landscape-scale initiatives, along with monitoring and reporting their impacts.
5. Finally, in Phase 5, they support the development of local regenerative economies, with nature-based enterprises underpinning sustainable community growth.

Activities

🔸

Te Kawa Waiora

 🔸

Tapere: Planning Rooted in Ancestral Boundaries

 🔸

He Ripo Kau: A Vision for the Hokianga

 🔸

Te Kete Hononga: The Basket of Connections

 🔸

Nature-Based Enterprise

 🔸

The Connectivity Partnership

Vision and Legacy

Decades of conservation spending have not reversed trendlines; Reconnecting Northland argues for intervening in systems—behaviour, finance, governance—not only isolated projects.

The group works at landscape scale—today roughly 150,000 ha, toward 800,000 ha by 2050—so whenua, wai, and tangata can flourish together.

Sources

📊

Te Mahi Tiaki Taiao Report

Bioregional Governance - Asian Case Studies & Framework

🟡 ReVillage Green Valley

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

ReVillage Green Valley is a place-rooted initiative reweaving community through land-based regeneration and collective care, responding to wildfires, housing displacement, ecological degradation, and social isolation.

Bioregional Context

Green Valley exists within West Sonoma County, California, centered around the town of Graton with approximately 1,800 residents. The bioregion is defined by rolling hills, vineyards, farmland, and forests, with the Green Valley watershed serving as a natural boundary.

Place
West County Museum, 261 S Main St, Sebastopol, CA 95472, United States
links
https://youtu.be/nowoYmBRijw

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🎒 Learning_Programs 🍥 Governance_Design 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background


The Green Valley watershed is a compact 40-square-mile bioregion in western Sonoma County, California, centered on Graton (about 1,800 residents). Rolling hills, vineyards, farmland, and forests define the area, with the watershed itself as a natural frame. Over the past century the local economy moved from apples toward wine; the community is now writing another chapter.

Origins and Network


ReVillage Green Valley grew out of grassroots organizing. They describe themselves as accidental bioregional organizers—the work began with love of place and gathering alongside neighbors who had long stewarded the land.

They envision a local economy grounded in ecology, equity, and cultural renewal, co-created with First Nations peoples, Latino farmworkers, Zen communities, and other long-term residents. Wildfires, housing pressure, ecological decline, and isolation are the overlapping crises; their response is to reweave community through land-based regeneration and shared care.

Activities

🔸

Green Valley Commons

 🔸

Arts & Museum of the Future

 🔸

Community Land Trust Formation

 🔸

ReVillage Co-Op Enterprises

 🔸

Local Governance Innovation

 🔸

ReVillaging Toolkit and Learning Exchange

Vision and Legacy


They are watching how commons, shared culture, and a regenerative economy might braid over time, and welcome partners who want to prototype village-scale regeneration built for interdependence, cultural vitality, and ecological repair.

By strengthening intergenerational, locally governed systems, they aim to offer a replicable model of “revillaging” for rural and peri-urban landscapes. The same crises—fire, displacement, ecological harm, isolation—are addressed by reweaving community through land-based regeneration and collective care.

In the Green Valley watershed—hills, vineyards, farmland, forest—people are practicing interdependence with one another, with nonhuman nature, and with the living Earth: one gas station, one firehouse, one enterprise at a time, starting from love of place and neighbors who show up to care.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 1

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 ReGenerOsa Collective

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

ReGenerOsa is a biocultural regeneration collective led by women who are reclaiming land, language, and local governance in the face of rapid ecological degradation, cultural loss, and speculative tourism.

Bioregional Context

The Osa Peninsula is one of the most biodiverse places on Earth, holding 2.5% of Earth's biodiversity on less than one-thousandth of the planet's land mass. The region faces dual pressures of conservation-driven exclusion and tourism-driven development.

Place
Corcovado National Park, Costa Rica
links
https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o|https://youtu.be/nowoYmBRijw

Tags

🔀 Grants_Programs 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🍽️ Community_Food_Access 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🔘 Governance_Bodies 🪢 Network_Weaving 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 💬 Peer_Learning 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies

Background


The Osa Peninsula in southern Costa Rica sits where Pacific coastline meets tropical rainforest. It is often called the most biologically intense place on Earth. Less than one-thousandth of the planet’s land mass holds about 2.5% of global biodiversity—rainforest, mangroves, and wildlife at a scale that draws worldwide attention.

That richness sits alongside deep strain. Globalized economic pressures erode social cohesion, intergenerational knowledge, and collective capacity. Roughly 40–70% of the local economy is informal, and about 80% depends on tourism. After gold mining and logging, the bioregion now faces both conservation-driven exclusion and tourism-driven development.

Substantial philanthropic funding reaches the Osa, yet most of it flows to conservation. Work is often fragmented and siloed, with missed chances to collaborate. Local communities are sidelined from funding and decisions; strategies lean on outside academics and scientists. Well-meaning regenerative projects from elsewhere can land as imposed fixes rather than support for people who already know the land.

Origins and Network


Trisha Stapleton is a 23-year resident of the Osa Peninsula and, from the local Cabécar population, has unlearned assumptions she once held and deepened how she relates to the land.

Around February 2020 this work took clearer shape. She looked online for what “regeneration” meant and found the Earth Regenerators Network—a mix of theory and on-the-ground practice. The Earth Regenerators Fund was forming; ReGenerOsa received support from its first round of regeneration funding.

From there came the RegenerOsa Collective: how to bring trust-based funding that supports local communities into a trust-based process on the ground?

Activities

🔸

Stone Soup Social

 🔸

Manos Cambiadas

 🔸

Food Sovereignty and Seed Saving Networks

 🔸

ReGenerOsa Women's Governance Circle

 🔸

Osa Biocultural Fund

 🔸

Osa Regenerative Learning Center (El Local)

Vision and Legacy


Over the past five years, their initiatives have held an open funding round reaching nine of twelve local communities, run the farmers market for more than four years, mobilized 1,200-plus volunteers through Manos Cambiadas, convened 30-plus bioregional stakeholder initiatives, and piloted circular economy practices, community currencies, and regenerative techniques.

The long-term aim is to co-create a municipal strategy for a fully regenerative canton—showing how a whole region can organize around ecological and social health. Many systems operate across the Osa without a shared center; little ties them together. ReGenerOsa seeks to be that weaving force—aligning elected bodies, institutions, investors, and community groups around a shared sense of where the region goes next.

🔵 ReFi Uganda

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

ReFi Uganda brings decentralized finance and Web3 education to rural communities in Northern Uganda, empowering farmers, youth, and climate activists with financial tools that bypass traditional banking restrictions.

Bioregional Context

Northern Uganda faces complex challenges from decades of conflict, youth unemployment, and land rights struggles, while holding strong potential for ecological integrity and community-led conservation.

🔵 Puget Sound Partnership

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A state-led initiative in Washington dedicated to the long-term recovery of the Puget Sound ecosystem. Through its evolving Action Agenda, the Partnership outlines strategies and near-term actions to restore water quality, habitat, species health, and human well-being. The Partnership uses a science-based approach and tracks progress through its Vital Signs indicators, while also seeking public input to shape direction and improve accountability over time.

Bioregional Context

Puget Sound is a large estuarine system in northwestern Washington State, encompassing over 2,500 miles of coastline and a watershed that spans 12 counties. Home to more than 4 million people, it is a region of complex ecological, cultural, and economic significance. The Sound includes critical habitat for salmon, orcas, shellfish, and seabirds, as well as culturally important lands and waters stewarded by Coast Salish Tribes since time immemorial.

Place
Puget Sound Way, San Jose, CA 95133, United States

🟣 Planet Drum

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Planet Drum Foundation was founded in San Francisco, CA in 1973. In association with community activists and ecologists, they developed the concept of a bioregion. Planet Drum works to research, promote and disseminate information about bioregionalism, a grassroots approach to ecology that emphasizes sustainability, community self-determination and regional self-reliance.

🔵 Pioneer Valley Grows

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

This initiative provides flexible financing and technical support to farmers and food entrepreneurs in the Pioneer Valley, helping them grow resilient, community-rooted businesses. Their Pioneer Valley Agriculture Investment Fund is a community investment fund which provides due diligence and financing. Local resident investors are drawn by the potential for a local and viable food system, creating a pool of 'social impact notes' returning 0-2% per annum, with loan amounts ranging from $1,000 to $250,000 for all types of local farm and food businesses.

Bioregional Context

The Pioneer Valley, located in western Massachusetts along the Connecticut River, is a historically agricultural region known for its fertile soils, small farms, and strong local food movement.

Place
Springfield, MA, United States
links
https://www.bioregion.org.uk/blog-posts/financing-a-bioregion-from-the-ground-upwards

🟡 Panama Restoration Lab

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Panama Restoration Lab is an emerging field-building effort working to demonstrate that ecological restoration can serve as a strategic tool for climate resilience, drought mitigation, and livelihood regeneration—not only for Panama, but as a model for the world.

Bioregional Context

Panama's global relevance stems from its ecological importance and economic centrality via the Panama Canal, where a historic drought recently disrupted trade and exposed deep vulnerabilities in water systems.

Place
Panama Canal, Panama
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw

Tags

💧 Watershed_Repair 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🐮 Grassland_Management 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site 🥼 Formal_Scientific_Process ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🪴 Venture_Cultivation

Background

Panama packs exceptional biological diversity into a small territory—forests, wetlands, grasslands, and soils that once buffered hydrology and livelihoods. Those systems are now stressed: rainfall shifts, weakened infiltration, and canal operations exposed to water risk. A severe drought recently throttled the Panama Canal, costing billions of dollars in trade disruption.

Climate is part of the story; land use is another—deforestation, erosive farming, and poor water governance reduce local rainmaking and storage. Functional ecosystems regulate moisture; degraded ones amplify scarcity.

The Panama Restoration Lab exists to show how deliberate restoration can tie landscape repair to drought relief, canal resilience, and community well-being—an argument pitched at local, national, and global audiences.

Origins and Network

The Lab frames restoration as multi-scale infrastructure: neighborhood and farm benefits; Panama Canal watershed security; and an exportable template for climate adaptation through regenerative land use.

Staff and allies combine restoration science, policy, and finance. EcoRestoration Alliance supplies technical depth; Biodiversity for a Livable Climate carries education and advocacy; CCT Foundation anchors Panama-based coordination; ForTerra Panama pilots commercial soil and reforestation pathways, including carbon revenues to recycle into work on the ground. Together they form the Lab’s joint platform for implementation, monitoring, and learning.

Activities

🔸

La Arenosa Pilot Site

 🔸

Forest Regeneration and Cattle Ranching

 🔸

Panama Venture Studio + Restoration Fund

Vision and Legacy

The Lab has stood up the farm pilot and is deepening ties with communities and agencies. Next steps sketched include more sites across the canal basin and Azuero Peninsula, stronger hydrologic evidence, a restoration center of excellence, and global dissemination—showing that landscape repair can stabilise both water for shipping and wellbeing for people.

The ambition parallels Panama’s historic canal role: once a chokepoint for trade, now potentially a reference for climate and stewardship infrastructure.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 1

🟣 Open Future Coalition

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

Open Future Coalition is a global initiative supporting place-based climate and community resilience by providing shared digital infrastructure, ecosystem-aligned funding, and coordination tools. Through the Open Impact platform, they help hundreds of organizations across dozens of countries align around shared goals, track progress, and access resources. Their work emphasizes bioregional approaches—supporting local stewards, regenerative initiatives, and landscape-level collaboration—while fostering long-term systems change through regional fellowships, learning exchanges, and the development of resilient funding models that prioritize equity, trust, and ecological integrity.

🟣 Open Civics

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

OpenCivics convenes and facilitates a civic innovation network of leaders, innovators, change agents, and public servants actualized in service to the public good.

🔵 OHAI Tonga

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

OHAI Tonga is a non-governmental organization working to strengthen climate resilience in the Kingdom of Tonga and across the South Pacific. Their Tongan Whale Corridor initiative establishes a whale corridor through the Tongan Archipelago as part of a broader plan to regenerate ocean ecosystems from the Arctic to the Antarctic. Building on the 2024 "He Whakaputanga Moana" declaration granting whales legal personhood, OHAI combines legal innovation, ecological stewardship, and Indigenous leadership through a Marine Keystone Species Treaty, a regional protection center, and a pioneering "Whales Bank" to finance whale conservation, treating whales as ecosystem engineers vital to ocean health and Pacific cultural identity.

Bioregional Context

Tonga is a Polynesian archipelago in the South Pacific comprising 169 islands, with a population of just over 100,000 people. In recent decades, Tonga has experienced rising sea levels, declining rainfall, rising air temperatures, and growing uncertainty in weather patterns, placing pressure on traditional livelihoods and community resilience.

Place
French Polynesia

🟡 Northeast Bioregioning Collective

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Also known as (or closely overlapping with) Alliance for the Mystic River Watershed, this collective brings together Indigenous and settler organizations across New England to regenerate land, housing, and livelihoods through Indigenous governance, ecological restoration, and regenerative infrastructure.

Bioregional Context

Covering the forests, rivers, and coastlines of the Northeastern U.S., this bioregion includes Algonquin-speaking territories and legacy industrial zones. It is abundant yet fractured, requiring cultural repair and integrated climate resilience strategies.

Place
ME, United States
links
https://youtu.be/y9TJNPmz2Lc|https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject|https://www.wellspringcommons.org/bioregioningcollective|https://biobasedcollective.org/

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🔀 Grants_Programs 🦏 Rights_of_Nature

Background

The Northeast biome—often called New England—runs from forested interior to North Atlantic coast. Algonquin-speaking nations shaped and were shaped by these lands and waters for millennia; species and cultural practices still echo that co-evolution across Native and settler-descended communities.

Shared language and storylines help explain recurring ecological patterns across the region.

Colonial and industrial history sits close to the surface: early mills, including Sam Slater’s Pawtucket, Rhode Island, installations, exported a model of extraction that scaled across the continent. Today the region is materially wealthy yet sharply unequal—Connecticut, in greater New York’s orbit, long ranked among the most income-concentrated jurisdictions in the United States. Repair and climate resilience require cultural as well as technical work.

Origins and Network Formation

The Northeast Bioregioning Collective (overlapping the Alliance for the Mystic River Watershed) gelled when the [BioFi Project]] invited bioregional financing concepts and six [Northeast groups answered—largely unaware of one another. BioFi’s community of practice helped cross wires.

Participants converge on intertribal relationship and right relations as a compass. The collective acts as a network of networks: the Connecticut River Bioregioning Collaborative (weaving and mapping), Center for an Ecology-Based Economy (CEBE) in Maine, Wellspring Commons as a place-learning hub, and the Bio-Based Materials Collective (regenerative building from soil to structure), among others.

Activities

🔸

Northeast Bioregional Strategy & Intertribal Governance Framework

 🔸

Northeast BFF & Venture Studio

 🔸

Alliance for the Mystic River Watershed

Vision and Legacy

The collective meets often—hybrid convenings, shared drafting, storying in place—moving toward common principles and a shared strategic vision.

Ingredients are present—systems maps, finance prototypes, land work, learning centers, currency experiments, university partnerships—but relationship is treated as the decisive asset. The bet is that this layered, confederated effort can advance right relations across the Northeast and beyond.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Forests of the Northeast

 🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟣 NoVo Foundation

Funder
🌐 Visit website

A key funder in the bioregional ecosystem.

🟡 Nila Vena Organizing Team

Bioregional Organizer

A tribal-led initiative focused on protecting the Nila Vena (Lake Iliamna) region through Indigenous land trusts, cultural revitalization, and economic regeneration in partnership with health and conservation institutions.

Bioregional Context

The Lake Iliamna region in Alaska is home to Yup'ik, Dena'ina, and Alutiiq peoples and some of the world's last wild sockeye salmon runs. The area is ecologically vital and under threat from industrial mining claims, despite being surrounded by national parks and protected ecosystems.

Place
Iliamna, AK, United States
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1VXzDAQLXPgoaNgo0Nzb0RlUfpjPUWOql69qagKkHKF0/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🔘 Governance_Bodies 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 🤝🏾 Agreements_and_Legal_Instruments

Background


"I belong to this place called Igyagig in Yupik, which means 'where Lake Iliamna is swallowed by the Queijek river,' which then flows into Bristol Bay, Alaska."

For millennia, Yup’ik, Dena’ina, and related communities have lived with Nila Vena (Lake Iliamna)—Nila in Dena’ina, nunbukbuk in Yup’ik. Six federally recognized tribes—Newhalen, Iliamna, New Halen, Pedro Bay, Kokhanok, and Igyagig—maintain that relationship today.

America’s seventh-largest lake by area, it runs about 90 miles end to end and anchors a global sockeye stronghold. Tributaries sustain multiple distinct sockeye lineages, major bird migrations, and the planet’s last freshwater seals; the system also draws anglers and conservation scrutiny. For many villages, the lake is the drinking-water source.

Since ANCSA (1971), leaders describe a persistent squeeze: title and authority splintered; village life tied more tightly to fixed parcels while corporations hold large blocks. Tribes remain federally recognized yet exercise limited control over lands and waters relative to state-chartered Native corporations. They sit between ~8 million acres of national park (Lake Clark, Katmai) and intense mineral interest—including what would be North America’s largest open pit—plus scattered claims staked after ANCSA.

The organising imperative is dual: defend a globally significant salmon nursery and rebuild Indigenous authority over fragmented estates.

Origins and Network

The Igyagig-scale team links the Native Corporation, tribal councils and stewardship offices, Alaska Venture Fund (sponsor of the Nila Vena Sustainability Forum), a health consortium, and Bristol Bay Heritage Land Trust—actors already accustomed to joint, multi-million-dollar federal packages. The shift now is toward an Indigenous land trust pathway that re-centers stewardship in tribal hands.

Activities

🔸

Nila Vena Sustainability Forum

 🔸

Nila Indigenous Land Trust Strategy

Vision and Legacy

Organisers draw on 92+ combined years of board service across partner bodies. The land trust is framed as repair for ANCSA’s fractures: stronger Native authority over land and water, salmon systems protected, and culture sustained.

The theory of change is familiar governance plus strategic acquisitions and easements—parcel by parcel, table by table—toward durable tribal control in a landscape occupied for more than 8,000 years.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

🟡 Mira River Guardians

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Mira River Guardians is a collective of concerned inhabitants working to regenerate the Mira River watershed in Portugal by focusing on its potential rather than its problems. Through mapping, relationship-building, and collaborative projects, the Guardians connect diverse stakeholders to create a polycentric network of bioregional stewards addressing desertification and ecological regeneration.

Bioregional Context

The watershed of the Mira River is one of the regions in Europe that is most at risk of desertification. Located in the Alentejo region of Portugal, this area faces significant ecological challenges from climate change, land use changes, and water scarcity. The Mira River watershed encompasses diverse landscapes and communities, each with unique relationships to the river and its ecosystems.

Place
Odemira, Portugal
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGc1cZg818/QTw8PNm_Z6GG98iW_aCqBg/edit

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Social_or_Network_Mapping 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 🎒 Learning_Programs 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling

Background

The Mira runs through Portugal’s Alentejo—a basin of farms, forests, and towns that ranks among Europe’s areas most at risk of desertification. Climate shifts, water scarcity, and land degradation already press the catchment; without deliberate care, dryland dynamics could deepen.

Origins and Network

In 2019, residents mobilised under Project Earth around a different emphasis: less fixation on damage lists, more attention to latent potential—relationships, collaboration, and the distinctive strengths of each village and reach of the river.

Activities

🔸

Social Ecosystem Mapping

 🔸

River Guardians

 🔸

Collaborative Knowledge Projects

Vision and Legacy

The Guardians treat potential as the starting frame: cultivate relationships first, invite many centres rather than one hub, and let regeneration emerge from mutual obligation across difference.

That stance implies more than pipes-and-plants restoration—it implies social ecology: diversity, reciprocity, and networks that behave less like command chains and more like living systems. Desertification risk remains real; the bet is that shared stewardship can meet it without flattening the basin’s varied places.

Sources

📊

Bioregional Governance - European Case Studies & Framework

🟣 Ma Earth

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Ma Earth is a values-driven network and movement committed to reweaving humanity's relationship with the Earth through the principles of care, reciprocity, and regeneration. With an emphasis on Indigenous wisdom, sacred activism, and embodied learning, Ma Earth supports transformative change by curating immersive experiences, creating aligned media content, and funding local leaders.

🟡 MIKIA Kenya

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

My Indigenous Knowledge in Action (MIKIA) works to document, preserve, and regenerate indigenous knowledge in Northern Kenya, particularly among Maasai pastoralist communities. Through intergenerational knowledge transfer, integration with scientific knowledge, and community-led initiatives, MIKIA supports climate resilience, biodiversity conservation, and indigenous self-determination.

Bioregional Context

Northern Kenya encompasses vast pastoralist landscapes where indigenous Maasai communities have lived for generations. This region faces severe climate change impacts, with communities experiencing increasing droughts, changing rainfall patterns, and ecosystem degradation. The landscapes are interconnected across national borders, with traditional migration routes and wildlife corridors that have been disrupted by conservation fencing and development.

Place
Lake Turkana Academy-Kalokol, Isiolo, Kenya
links
https://youtu.be/RhXCT1toHIs

Tags

🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🎒 Learning_Programs 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity

Background

Northern Kenya’s Maasai pastoralists have long moved with seasonal grass, water, and wildlife—knowledge and practice tuned to semi-arid rangelands. That social–ecological system is under strain.

Climate change brings deeper droughts, erratic rains, and degraded range. Mobility in search of pasture raises friction with other users, access barriers, and safety risks. Conservation fencing has sliced migration routes; elephants and people are squeezed into tighter spaces, inflating conflict. Meanwhile, expert-led fixes sometimes sideline intergenerational methods that treat people, livestock, and wildlife as one connected landscape.

Origins and Network

My Indigenous Knowledge in Action (MIKIA) takes its name from a Maasai phrase meaning, roughly, “don’t take me away”—a plea to keep Indigenous knowledge present. Transmission practices are themselves threatened; if they erode, so does the chain from elder to child.

MIKIA documents, safeguards, and revives knowledge through intergenerational exchange and deliberate pairing of Indigenous and scientific insight. Founder Ramson Karmushu works from within Maasai communities in Northern Kenya toward land use that keeps biodiversity and livelihoods viable across coming decades.

Activities

🔸

Decolonizing Human-Wildlife Conflicts

 🔸

Indigenous Taxonomy & Zoonosis

 🔸

Indigenous Learning Centers and Mapping

Vision and Legacy

MIKIA’s programme mix—centres, maps, and intergenerational curricula—treats Indigenous knowledge as something to keep, not extract or replace. The horizon is stronger self-determination for Maasai institutions and lifeways, with customary and scientific knowledges held in deliberate conversation rather than substitution.

🔵 Long Tom Watershed Council

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The council takes a collaborative, science-based approach to improving water quality, fish and wildlife habitat, and community resilience. Recognized as a model watershed council in Oregon, LTWC integrates rigorous data collection with public engagement to ensure that land and water stewardship efforts are effective, inclusive, and grounded in place.

Bioregional Context

The Long Tom Watershed lies in the upper Willamette Valley of western Oregon, draining into the Willamette River and ultimately the Columbia River system. The watershed spans a diverse mosaic of ecosystems—from upland forests and oak savannas to agricultural lowlands and urbanizing areas near Eugene. Historically stewarded by the Kalapuya people, the region is now shaped by a mix of land uses, including farming, forestry, and suburban development.

Place
LTWC, 751 S Danebo Ave, Eugene, OR 97402, United States

🟡 Landschaftspflegeverein

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Works to preserve and restore one of the most species-rich regions in Europe through network building, community involvement, and sustainable grassland management. They connect people and organizations to create a network of natural areas.

Bioregional Context

The Thermenlinie Wiener Becken (Vienna Basin Thermal Line) region is one of the most species-rich regions in Europe, featuring former pasture areas and wetlands. The region's exceptional biodiversity comes from its position between the Alps and the Pannonian lowlands, hosting species from both areas. Grasslands store about 30% of global carbon in biomass.

Place
Austria
links
https://youtu.be/k5OiXPqz8pM

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🎒 Learning_Programs 🐮 Grassland_Management 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center

Background

The Thermenlinie Wiener Becken—south of Vienna—is easy to read as sprawl, intensive farms, and busy roads. Under that surface sit major biological hotspots.

The belt is among Europe’s most species-rich landscapes: former pastures such as Bertholdsdorf Heide, Güberheide, and Symposium Linderbrunn, plus scattered wetlands. Alps meet Pannonian lowlands here, so flora and fauna blend alpine and steppic elements.

Many hotspots are grasslands—dry meadows, old pastures, wet meadows, and fens—holding rare and protected species. Dry grasslands are especially rich in flowers and insects; wet or dry, grasslands lock up large soil carbon stocks. Globally, grasslands hold roughly 30% of carbon stored in biomass—important for climate mitigation.

Without large wild grazers (aurochs, wisent long gone), these open habitats now depend on continued human care; left alone, they scrub over and succeed to forest—and much of the open-habitat diversity vanishes.

Origins and Network

Founded June 2017, the Landschaftspflegeverein is a non-profit devoted to long-term biodiversity in the Thermenlinie–Wienerwald–Wiener Becken corridor.

Work began with a practical question: how to manage greenspaces differently from routine frequent mowing—what would be sustainable? The aim is to rekindle local enthusiasm for “nature next door.”

Activities

🔸

Network Naturregion Building

 🔸

Nature Tours and Experiences

 🔸

School Education Programs

 🔸

Sustainable Grassland Management

Vision and Legacy

Network Naturregion keeps widening—people and parcels woven together. Modest changes such as less frequent mowing have already yielded plant and insect assemblages many residents no longer recognise from childhood.

Grasslands remain carbon-rich and biologically extraordinary; maintaining the mosaic still depends on many hands—volunteers, educators, farmers, and public partners sharing the load.

Sources

🎬

Bioregional Weaving Lab - Thermenlinie Wiener Becken (Austria)

Network Affiliation

🟣 Bioregional Weaving Labs

🟣 Landscapes for 1 Billion People

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

A global initiative that aims to restore 500 million hectares of degraded land and support one billion people through nature-based solutions. The initiative focuses on regenerative landscape approaches—such as agroforestry, farmer-managed natural regeneration, and watershed restoration—that increase food security, enhance biodiversity, and sequester carbon.

🔵 Laconia

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A bioregional network in the Peloponnese region of Greece, connecting cultural initiatives, community associations, and local creators to honor life in all its forms and create transformative change through shared cultural experiences and collaborative action.

Bioregional Context

Laconia is a region in the southern Peloponnese, encompassing historic communities like Sparta, Monemvasia, Neapolis, and the island of Kythera. This area has been shaped by millennia of cultural exchange and agricultural traditions, but faces challenges of rural depopulation and cultural preservation.

Place
Sparta, Peloponneso, Palaiologou Konstantinou, 231 00 Sparti, Greece
links
https://youtu.be/HwJc6RsBa18

Sources

🎬

Bioregional Weaving Lab - Laconia (Greece)

🔵 Kwiakah Nation

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A small First Nation in British Columbia led by Chief Steven Dick, focused on developing an Indigenous-led, conservation-focused regenerative economy in their ancestral territory. They're implementing regenerative forestry practices, developing sustainable businesses from kelp farming to ecotourism, and creating replicable models for climate-resilient economic systems.

Bioregional Context

The Kwiakah territory encompasses the coastal rainforest ecosystems of Phillips Arm and Frederick Arm, located an hour by boat from Campbell River on Vancouver Island. This bioregion includes the Phillips Estuary/ᕈNacinuxᵂ Conservancy and the Pałəmin/Estero Basin Conservancy, protecting 4,439 hectares of interconnected marine, estuary, lake, and river systems. The area represents significant ecological value with temperate rainforests, salmon-bearing rivers, and coastal marine environments that support rich biodiversity including grizzly bears and diverse aquatic species.

Place
Vancouver, BC, Canada

🔵 Kotahitanga mō te Taiao Alliance

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

KMTT is a partnership of 17 iwi (Māori tribes), councils, and government agencies working collaboratively to regenerate land and sea ecosystems across the top of New Zealand's South Island. They blend Indigenous knowledge and Western science to deliver large-scale biodiversity restoration, climate adaptation, and regenerative economic development. The alliance strengthens local initiatives by creating shared strategies, aligning efforts across diverse actors, and facilitating community-led action.

Bioregional Context

The northern region of New Zealand's South Island encompasses 3.4 million hectares of mountains, rivers, forests, and coastline. This region holds immense ecological significance, with native flora and fauna found nowhere else on Earth, alongside culturally significant landscapes stewarded by Māori for generations. Historically impacted by colonization, commercial forestry, and extractive industries, the region is now a focal point for regenerative restoration.

Place
Lake Tekapo, Domain Rd, Lake Tekapo 7999, New Zealand

🟡 Kogayon Association

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Oltenia de sub Munte is a collective of people working to preserve heritage, living traditions, and authentic ways of life in the mountainous region of Oltenia, Romania. Through guided tours, silk weaving workshops, traditional agriculture, and community-based tourism, they bring traditions into daily life rather than transforming them into museums.

Bioregional Context

Oltenia de sub Munte (Oltenia under the Mountain) encompasses the mountainous region of southern Romania, an area described as "a place loved by God" with diverse relief forms, rich nature, artists, and craftsmen. This region maintains living traditions that are still part of daily life—people raising animals with passion, taking them to pasture, making hay, and working with wild nature.

Place
Olănești, Băile Olănești, Romania
links
https://youtu.be/i6QhjPB8des

Tags

🎒 Learning_Programs 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 🐙 More_Than_Human_Knowledge 🧦 Clothing_and_Product_Manufacturing 🏗️ Construction_and_Infrastructure

Background

In Romania’s Oltenian mountains, Oltenia de sub Munte (“Oltenia under the Mountain”) is described by locals as a landscape that feels “loved by God”—folded relief, mixed forest and pasture, and everyday lifeways that still feel traditional: herding, seasonal haymaking, and work with more-than-human neighbours.

Villages carry strong built heritage and craft knowledge tied to national history. Residents and visitors alike seek deeper understanding of where they live or travel; the open question is how to protect what remains, showcase architecture and story, and pass skills to the next generation without freezing culture in amber.

Origins and Network

The network formed as guides, farmers, craftspeople, hospitality hosts, historians, and neighbours pooled efforts to welcome outsiders without turning the mountains into a stage set. Participants include Kogayon Association–led tours, women’s silk-weaving cooperatives, households that still process milk and fruit on the agricultural calendar, and partners who document buildings and oral history—each contributing a different thread to a shared territorial story.

Activities

🔸

Guided Tours

 🔸

Heritage Preservation

 🔸

Traditional Horse Experiences

 🔸

Silk Weaving Workshop

Vision and Legacy

The collective still bets on living culture over museification—heritage as practice, not display. Looking ahead, one organiser sketches a decade horizon: more visitors, guesthouses, and local gastronomy hubs—“an area full of tourists,” with the valley developing in step, “and clearly we all benefit.” The hope is that growth feeds household economies—food from neighbours’ kitchens first—so authenticity pays those who maintain it.

Sources

🎬

Bioregional Weaving Lab - Oltenia de sub Munte (Romania)

🟡 Koalisi Ekonomi Membumi

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A collective movement catalyzing sustainable investment ecosystems across Indonesia through their Value Chain Collaboration Canvas approach. KEM connects local governments, smallholder farmers, businesses, and financial institutions to build nature-based commodity value chains.

Bioregional Context

Bali is a volcanic island in the Indonesian archipelago known for its forested highlands, terraced rice fields, and coastal areas. The island faces critical environmental challenges including water scarcity, with tourism-intensive areas experiencing severe deficits.

Place
Bali, Indonesia
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 💧 Watershed_Repair 🔘 Governance_Bodies 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols

Background

Bali combines rich forest endowment with acute resource pressure. Forests cover more than 249,000 hectares; Buleleng, Jembrana, and Tabanan hold 63% of tree cover. Those forests underpin high-value crops—vanilla, cacao, coffee, spices—and buffer biodiversity and livelihoods.

Tourism growth and land conversion are squeezing water supplies. In 2021, the sharpest deficits appeared in tourism-heavy districts with over 250 lodging businesses each. Keeping forest-rich regencies such as Jembrana and Tabanan from wholesale conversion to resorts is part of island-wide water security.

Origins and Network

Koalisi Ekonomi Membumi (KEM) organises as a movement to speed up investment that fits Indonesian place-based wisdom. It takes Tri Hita Karana—“three causes of prosperity”: harmony with spirit, among people, and with nature—as a governing ethic for weaving spiritual, social, and ecological aims in bioregional work.

Members such as Supernova Ecosystem, Katalys, Swadaya, Pandegha, and PBF host collaboration and delivery. KEM works in Bali, West Kalimantan, Central Sulawesi, and South Sumatra, linking districts, enterprises, and finance through the Value Chain Collaboration Canvas (VC3).

Activities

🔸

Value Chain Collaboration Canvas

 🔸

100 Regenerative Village Program

 🔸

Multi-Stakeholder Forest Governance

 🔸

Planning Assistance to Local Governments

Vision and Legacy

KEM seeks proof-of-concept bioeconomy and nature-based value chains that reduce dependence on extractive industry and industrial monoculture: 100 jurisdictions committed to sustainable development, 100+ nature-based enterprises, connected chains, and about USD 200 million in funding and transactions. By May 2023, roughly USD 35 million was unlocked, toward a USD 50 million target by 2025 (rolling disbursement).

The bet is that Tri Hita Karana-style alignment—people, nature, and policy—can show how forest water, farmer income, and visitor economies co-evolve at scale across Indonesia.

Sources

📊

Bioregional Governance - Asian Case Studies & Framework

🔵 Inuit Circumpolar Council (ICC)

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

An international non-governmental organization representing approximately 180,000 Inuit across Alaska, Canada, Greenland, and Chukotka (Russia). Founded in 1977, ICC was created to unify Inuit voices across the Arctic and advocate for their shared cultural, environmental, and political interests on the global stage. With Consultative Status at the United Nations, ICC works to uphold Inuit rights, safeguard the Arctic environment, and promote self-determined development throughout the circumpolar North.

Bioregional Context

The Arctic is defined by extreme seasonal cycles, permafrost, sea ice, and a fragile ecosystem that is warming at more than twice the global average. The Arctic is home to diverse Indigenous peoples whose cultures and subsistence livelihoods are deeply tied to land, ice, and animal migrations.

Place
Greenland

🟡 Inabita

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A bioregional initiative in central Italy's Fiastra Valley, working to strengthen collaborative relationships and shared identity between villages through cultural celebration and participatory planning. Born from the Borgofuturo festival, the initiative has evolved into "Qui ValdiFiastra"—a 15-project action plan addressing built infrastructure, culture, environmental education, and local production.

Bioregional Context

Nestled in the foothills of the Sibillini mountains in central Italy, the Fiastra valley is a region populated by several rural municipalities facing challenges common to Italy's "inner areas"—depopulation, infrastructure needs, and maintaining cultural identity while building economic resilience. The villages share a strong identity rooted to place but also face long-standing rivalries that have limited collaboration.

Place
Lago di Fiastra, Via Circonvallazione del Lago, 62035 Fiastra MC, Italy
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGc1cZg818/QTw8PNm_Z6GG98iW_aCqBg/edit

Tags

📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🪢 Network_Weaving 🍥 Governance_Design 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices 🗂️ Portfolio_Development

Background

The Fiastra valley lies in the Sibillini foothills in central Italy—roughly three hours from Rome—across several small municipalities. People describe a shared identity tied tightly to place, threaded through scattered hill villages.

Those communities sit in Italy’s “inner areas”: rural territories juggling ageing populations, infrastructure gaps, and economic pressure while trying to keep culture alive. Needs cluster in four strands: built infrastructure; culture; environmental education; and local production and craft.

Origins and Network

Since 2010, Ripe San Ginesio has hosted the biennial Borgofuturo festival. In 2020—the tenth edition—young organisers working with the municipality faced COVID-19 lockdowns instead of a usual programme.

They used the pause to look beyond one town: mapping needs and possibilities across the whole valley and asking how the festival could respond to both. They confirmed strong place-based identity—and also long-standing rivalries that had limited cooperation.

Activities

🔸

Cultural Celebration Across Villages

 🔸

Participatory Planning and Visioning

 🔸

Action Plan Implementation

Vision and Legacy

Inabità remains a cooperative of researchers, planners, architects, and environmental scientists working through integrated, participatory methods. The emphasis is developmental: enabling different stewards to contribute fully while the valley’s own qualities emerge over time.

Planning continues with explicit attention to how people contribute together—a diversification of roles (likened to an ant colony) so each group works from its strengths. Process and outcome stay paired: bioregional governance is treated as ongoing learning, not a one-off plan.

Sources

📊

Bioregional Governance - European Case Studies & Framework

🟣 Hylo

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Hylo is a community-led, prosocial coordination platform for purpose-driven groups, with a specific focus for place-based and bioregional organizing

🟡 Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

HKY Intercultural Reserve is an Indigenous-led nonprofit initiative in Huni Kuin territory, dedicated to restoring Amazonian ecosystems while preserving and celebrating Huni Kuin culture. It aims to create sustainable livelihoods through agroforestry and cultural revitalization.

Bioregional Context

Situated in the Brazil-Peru border region of Acre, this area is part of the western Amazon and home to rich biocultural diversity. It faces threats from deforestation, extractive industries, and cultural erosion, with significant potential for regenerative Indigenous stewardship.

Place
AC, Brazil
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YPJ2Thbgbdc&ab_channel=TheBioFiProject|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1VXzDAQLXPgoaNgo0Nzb0RlUfpjPUWOql69qagKkHKF0/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🌰 Seeds_and_Nurseries 🎒 Learning_Programs 📖 Knowledge_Artefacts 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 📷 Visitor_Experiences

Background

Along the Brazil–Peru border in Acre, Huni Kuin communities have long stewarded Amazon forest. About 19,000 Huni Kuin people live across 12 reserves here, carrying shamanic practice, arts, and deep ecological knowledge—experience that matters for biocultural futures across the western Amazon.

Threats include illegal clearing, timber extraction, conventional agriculture, soil loss, and hunting pressure—forces that can displace communities from territory. At the same time, roughly 107,000 ha of unprotected land in the area could host regenerative models able to spread more widely in the basin.

Origins and Network

Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve forms around a dual aim: sustain Huni Kuin culture and spirituality while restoring forest ecosystems. Mapu Huni Kuin, spiritual leader and president of the Indigenous Association Yoshibu, is a visible advocate—linking political voice, ceremony, and defence of territory.

The mission is intergenerational self-sufficiency through viable enterprises rooted in Huni Kuin lifeways and adequate infrastructure—starting with wild cacao and wild pepper harvest and trade, strengthening identity, and applying forest coexistence knowledge to habitat recovery and cultural sovereignty.

Activities

🔸

Native Crop Regeneration and Supply Chain Development

 🔸

Youth Agroforestry and Cultural Training Program

 🔸

Native Tree Reforestation Campaign

 🔸

Sustainable Ethnobotanicals Research Program

 🔸

Huni Kuin Intercultural Exchange Hub

Vision and Legacy

The reserve is assembling a capital plan toward a $3.75 million, multi-tier allocation for restoration, enterprise integration, and the hub. Success is measured partly in replication: a model other Amazon forest communities might adapt—showing that agroforestry rooted in Huni Kuin practice can regenerate ecosystems and livelihoods together, with sovereignty and territory protection moving in parallel.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Supports regenerative transition through a guild-based approach focused on food, shelter, ecology, and culture, working across the Hudson River estuary region to foster local collaboration, fund place-based solutions, and steward long-term bioregional resilience.

Bioregional Context

The Hudson River Estuary stretches 5,312 square miles from New York City to Troy, a tidal freshwater region hosting rare biodiversity, vibrant communities, and cultural history, but facing threats from pollution, housing crises, and climate impacts like flooding and sea level rise.

Place
New York, NY, United States
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving ⭕ Community Governance ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🔀 Grants_Programs 🛠️ Resource_Hub 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🎉 Congressing_and_Events

Background

Lenape tradition names part of this estuary Mawa—a meeting place. The Hudson is a tidal freshwater wedge where Adirondack runoff meets Atlantic salt: twice daily the current reverses. It is also a social crossroads—Appalachian uplands, farms and cities, global diplomacy at the river’s mouth.

The estuary covers 5,312 square miles from upper Manhattan to Troy—HERE’s home ground for shared bioregional work. Most of the basin lies in New York, with extensions into Connecticut, New Jersey, and Massachusetts. It holds roughly 200 fish species, 19 rare-bird types, 140 rare plants, and 11 significant biodiversity areas—and figures in the early U.S. conservation story. The Appalachian Trail’s 2,000-mile route crosses the region, drawing long-distance hikers each year.

Pressures include pollution affecting drinking water for more than 100,000 people, combined sewer overflows, flood and sea-level risk, housing stress, invasives, and—less visible to many—rising forest-fire exposure in the Northeast.

Origins and Network

The Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort (HERE) grew out of Partners for Climate Action Hudson Valley. For roughly five years, that collaboration concentrated grants on four interlinked domains—ecology, food, culture, and shelter—testing how place-based funding could track cross-cutting resilience in the estuary. HERE carries that guild-shaped logic forward as its own organising frame.

Activities

🔸

Pollinate HV

 🔸

Library of Local Network

 🔸

Guild System Development

 🔸

Hudson Valley Bioregional Trust & Fund

 🔸

HERE Magazine

 🔸

Bioregional Congress

Vision and Legacy

HERE treats the tidal Hudson as both ecology and social infrastructure: guilds, fund, and narrative media as ways to align regeneration with the communities who live here.

The aim is organising and capital that stay near the problem—here, in the estuary’s “meeting place.” A timeline ties guild development, fund-building, and congress to a single transition strategy: local action linked to regional-scale resilience.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🔵 Hansalim

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Hansalim is South Korea's largest consumer-producer food cooperative, founded in the 1980s at the intersection of the pro-democracy movement and grassroots agricultural reform. What began as a small store offering pesticide-free rice and eggs has grown into a cooperative with over 900,000 members. Its model centers on a reciprocal relationship– producers safeguard consumer health through ecologically grown food, while consumers support the livelihood and dignity of farmers. Hansalim embodies a philosophy of interconnectedness—summed up in its mantra, "the universe in a bowl of rice."

Bioregional Context

Hansalim operates across South Korea, a country shaped by rapid industrialization, rural depopulation, and the legacy of authoritarian rule. Emerging during a time of political upheaval in the 1980s, the movement arose in response to health crises caused by pesticide use and the loss of smallholder livelihoods. South Korea's mountainous terrain and limited arable land have long challenged its food sovereignty, making farmer-consumer solidarity vital for both ecological resilience and social cohesion.

Place
South Korea
links
https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/77083023.pdf,|https://www.sussex.ac.uk/broadcast/read/65052

🔵 Guayabillas DAO

Bioregional Organizer

Pioneers a regenerative governance and financing model using blockchain technology for community-led conservation and restoration in the CABR biodiversity hotspot, combining participatory design, nested governance structures, and innovative funding mechanisms.

Bioregional Context

The CABR (Chocó-Andean biodiversity region) is a globally important biodiversity hotspot facing deforestation, degradation from unsustainable agriculture, and limited governance capacity at local levels, requiring new approaches to conservation financing and community empowerment.

Place
Guayabillas, Bolívar, Colombia

Background


Guayabillas de Pacto sits in the Chocó-Andean Biodiversity Region, a globally important biodiversity hotspot facing deforestation, degradation from unsustainable agriculture, and limited governance capacity at local levels.

Origins and Network


Traditional conservation models often rely on external organizations managing funds and making decisions for communities, creating dependency and limiting local agency. The Guayabillas DAO project emerged to address core challenges: how can communities gain real governance power over funding and priorities? How can individual conservation actions be connected to collective regenerative work? How can innovative financing mechanisms support community-led regeneration while ensuring transparency and accountability?

Through a pioneering approach that combines participatory design, blockchain-based governance, and novel funding mechanisms like NFTree conservation payments, the project demonstrates how local communities can manage their own regenerative work with both autonomy and transparency—proving that community-led conservation can be both effective and sustainable.

Vision and Legacy

The DAO is operational under local stewardship. Training stresses both off-chain and on-chain practice, with smartphones and WhatsApp lowering technical barriers and deliberate outreach to women, elders, and youth.

The pilot is meant to scale across neighbouring communities and parishes—nested governance that can link smallholder practice to wider ecological and finance systems.

Sources

📊

Developing a DAO for Regenerative Landscape Governance in Ecuador’s Chocó-Andean Biosphere Reserve

🔵 Green Releaf Initiative

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Green Releaf designs and implements climate resilience programs through labs and learning journeys. They integrate indigenous knowledge, permaculture principles, and arts-based approaches to support communities recovering from climate disasters such as Category 5 supertyphoons. Their work spans watershed restoration, sustainable agriculture, and building collaborative partnerships among government, civil society, and local leaders to strengthen ecological and social resilience.

Bioregional Context

The Philippines archipelago sits in one of the world's most disaster-prone regions, positioned along the Pacific Ring of Fire and within the typhoon belt. The country faces multiple climate hazards including 20+ typhoons annually, flooding, landslides, and drought cycles, with its coastal areas particularly vulnerable to rising sea levels. Deforestation and watershed degradation have intensified these issues, especially affecting rural and indigenous communities.

Place
Philippines
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

🟣 Garrison Institute

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Working toward biodiversity stewardship, ecological cultures, regenerative economies, and the common good. Organizer of bioregional efforts in the Hudson Valley through the Pathways to Planetary Health program.

Place
Garrison, NY, United States

🟡 Forever Sabah

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A 25-year transformational program facilitating Sabah's shift to a diversified, equitable circular economy. Operating as a social collaborative movement rather than a traditional NGO, they weave connections across watersheds, forests, and coastal zones.

Bioregional Context

Sabah occupies the northern portion of Borneo, the world's third-largest island, characterized by ancient rainforests, diverse marine ecosystems, and Mount Kinabalu. This biodiversity hotspot has faced extensive deforestation due to industrial palm oil and timber extraction, losing over 30% of its primary forest since the 1970s.

Place
Sabah, Malaysia
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

Tags

🐙 More_Than_Human_Knowledge 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 💧 Watershed_Repair 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 🔋 Renewable_Energy 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🍥 Governance_Design 📣 Political_Advocacy 🦏 Rights_of_Nature 🎒 Learning_Programs 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center

Background

Sabah lies on the northern third of Borneo—rainforest, reefs, and Mount Kinabalu—home to many Indigenous communities, especially Dusun peoples, whose land rights and livelihoods face extraction and climate pressure.

Industrial palm and timber have cleared large areas: more than 30% of primary forest since the 1970s. Fragmentation has disrupted wildlife movement, strained watersheds, and weakened cultural and economic foundations for those communities.

Origins and Network

Forever Sabah grew from a two-year, cross-sector question: where Sabah lands in fifty years on a business-as-usual path. That process set priorities and early projects. It operates less as a conventional NGO than as a social collaborative movement steering a 25-year shift toward a diversified, equitable circular economy.

Philosophy & Approach

Change is not treated as something grants alone can buy. The group foregrounds shared experience and “currencies” such as responsibility, participation, reciprocity, and trust to align actors at scale. A forever horizon and an Indigenous imperative—to belong to and be accountable to place—run through the programme design.

Activities

🔸

Human-Elephant Harmony

 🔸

Watershed Conservation and Payment for Ecosystem Services

 🔸

Renewable Energy Transition

 🔸

Mangrove Conservation and Spatial Planning

 🔸

Legal Innovation and Policy Development

 🔸

Learning and Capacity Building

Vision and Legacy

The 25-year programme still aims at a diversified, equitable circular Sabah. Climate responses are increasingly institutionalised, yet communities remain on the front line. Watershed work, mangrove and riparian care, rice revival, renewable energy—threads that once ran in parallel—now need to be joined, scaled, and woven into mitigation and adaptation in a coordinated way.

Sources

📄

The SPaCES Project

 📄

 📄
