🟡 Findhorn Watershed Initiative

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

They practice "stealth bioregioning" - working within existing structures like the Rivers Trust while gradually introducing systemic change. Their approach integrates ecology, culture, and economics through careful relationship building and stakeholder engagement.

Bioregional Context

Located within the Moray Firth bioregion, the area presents a diverse cultural landscape including traditional fishing villages, the Findhorn Eco Village, military bases, conservative agricultural areas, and sporting estates. The region marks a cultural and linguistic boundary between Scots and Gaelic languages, creating a threshold between different Scottish cultural traditions.

Place
Findhorn, Forres, Scotland, United Kingdom
links
https://youtu.be/m47HX3U2Auo

Tags

💧 Watershed_Repair 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🐉 Species_Control 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background

Findhorn Bay combines the Findhorn Ecovillage with military bases, farmland, and sporting estates. Along the coast, everyday speech tends toward Scots; in the headwaters, Gaelic is stronger—a cultural and linguistic edge as well as an ecological one.

The watershed also sits between oil-and-gas Aberdeen and the Gaelic Highlands, between entrenched land tenure and today’s restoration agenda, and between the Findhorn’s source-to-sea story and neighbouring Speyside—where concentrated land ownership meets urgent nature-recovery pressure.

Origins and Network

In 2021, the initiator approached Clare Cooper Marcus and scoped local institutions that could host catchment-scale work. The Findhorn and Nairn Rivers Trust (FNL Rivers Trust) had already shifted from a fisheries trust toward wider rivers-trust remit: holistic catchment management and habitat work rather than salmon-and-trout counts alone. It held a management plan but not yet a full action plan to implement revised articles; as part of Scotland’s rivers-trust network, it could also help spread the model if it succeeded.

A trust contact agreed to host fundraising and vision-building for a restoration programme. The Findhorn Watershed Initiative (FWI) was then framed as an integrated weave of ecology, culture, and economy. Hosted under the FNL Rivers Trust, it runs collaborative habitat restoration, carbon sequestration, and river stewardship across nature recovery, nature connection, and a nature-positive local economy.

Activities

🔸

Riparian Watershed Recovery

 🔸

Gaelic Cultural Heritage

 🔸

Monitoring Strategy

 🔸

Training & Green Jobs

 🔸

Findhorn Watershed Deer Forum

Vision and Legacy

“Stealth bioregioning”—using existing institutions such as rivers trusts—has bought traction while keeping ecology, culture, and economy in one frame. Relationships trusts already hold with landowners, plus shared concerns such as salmon, widen the room for integrated catchment work.

FWI is building its three programme areas behind an explicit, shareable blueprint: local detail with lessons that could travel. Scotland’s network of rivers trusts offers a natural channel for that exchange.

Sources

📄

Nature-Positive Economy

 🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 1

 📊

Bioregional Governance - European Case Studies & Framework

🔵 Fibershed

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Fibershed began in California as a non-profit movement to connect regional fiber farmers, natural dyers, mills, designers, and wearers in a "farm-to-closet" value chain. The concept defines a strategic geography for fiber (a "fibershed"), similar to a foodshed, within which raw materials and textile production are linked... biodegradable textiles produced and eventually composted within the same bioregion, blending traditional crafts with innovation to meet local needs sustainably.

Place
California St, San Francisco, CA, United States

🟡 Enlivened Cooperative

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A global network (with roots in Hawaii) dedicated to reimagining learning, research, and leadership as relational, regenerative practices. Rooted in diverse knowledge systems and shaped by place, the collective designs community-led, cross-cultural learning experiences that support collective action and ecological-cultural renewal.

Bioregional Context

Hawaii Island features diverse ecosystems ranging from true deserts receiving less than 150mm of annual rainfall to some of the wettest locations on the planet. The islands provide an exceptional natural experiment for understanding bioregional relationships due to highly organized and broad environmental gradients.

Place
HI, United States
links
https://youtu.be/4VG88nc2s6E

Tags

🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 📍 Territorial_Zoning 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🤝🏾 Agreements_and_Legal_Instruments 🔀 Grants_Programs 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background

Hawaii works as a natural laboratory for bioregional thinking: a stationary hot spot built the islands in sequence, so age marches from fresh lava on Hawaiʻi Island to older landscapes such as Kauaʻi, roughly 5.5 million years old.

Rainfall and age together produce extreme gradients—from true desert (under about 150 mm of rain a year) to some of the wettest places on Earth (up to about 6,000 mm annually). On Kohala Mountain, one can cross from about 170 mm/year rainfall to about 6,000 mm/year in roughly four hours—much of the planet’s moisture gradient in a single hike.

Those gradients shape soils (Hawaiʻi spans 12 of the 13 global soil orders), ecosystems (Hawaiʻi Island alone holds about two-thirds of Holdridge life zones on one island), and high endemism. Human practices and norms have tracked that variety—an illustration of how culture and environment co-evolve.

Origins & Network

The Enlivened Cooperative is a worker-owned, not-for-profit ecosocial organization: members work in different parts of the world, with projects anchored in their own regions. It has roots in Hawaiʻi and operates as a global network for place-based regenerative work.

Philosophy & Approach

The cooperative reimagines learning, research, and collaboration so that people, rooted communities, and organizations can co-build intercultural and ecological worlds. Many members come from teaching and academia while also working with restoration and social movements where they live.

Core principles braid cooperativism (worker ownership and cooperative practice), learning with and from living systems (including biomimicry), place-based knowledge, and ecosocial learning and action research—“enlivening” how we learn, relate, and inquire.

A central challenge is bridging knowledge systems—scientific, local, and Indigenous—sometimes called “two-eyed seeing.” That work runs into power, history, and colonial legacies; it requires honoring difference while building shared understanding.

Activities

🔸

Hala Traditional Agriculture Mapping

 🔸

Traditional Knowledge in Governance

Vision and Legacy


The Hala Traditional Agriculture Mapping project demonstrates how Indigenous knowledge systems accurately identified ecological relationships that align with modern scientific measurements, how traditional bioregional management can inform contemporary land use and agricultural funding decisions, and how historical land management systems provide valid frameworks for current environmental stewardship.

This work exemplifies the Enlivened Cooperative's mission of combining knowledge systems—showing how traditional Hawaiian political-ecological management, validated through modern geospatial and archaeological methods, can inform current governance while honoring indigenous knowledge and practices.

🟣 Ecosystem Restoration Communities

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

A global network that harnesses the collective power of individuals and communities to restore ecosystems.

🟣 ETH Zurich

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

ETH Zurich, one of Europe's leading science and technology universities, includes interdisciplinary research centers and programs focused on sustainability, climate adaptation, and regenerative land use. ETH Zurich supports the design of bioregionally attuned solutions for food systems, water management, urban metabolism, and ecological restoration.

Activities

🔸

Strategy Games

🟡 Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium unites a diverse collective of community leaders, Indigenous allies, and regenerative projects in Costa Rica's southern region. Their focus is on land rematriation, water protection, and grassroots bioregional governance.

Bioregional Context

This bioregion encompasses the area from Chirripó (Costa Rica's highest point) through the Térraba-Sierpe wetlands to the Marino Ballena National Park. The region holds approximately half a million different species—5% of the world's biodiversity—concentrated in just 0.03% of Earth's land mass.

Place
Costa Rica
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc|https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE

Tags

🔘 Governance_Bodies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 🏕️ Territorial_Monitoring 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site 🎋 Materials_Transformation 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background

Water defines the Diamante Barú region. From Chirripó at 3,820 meters above sea level, it falls, infiltrates, and returns at the Diamante waterfall—about 650 feet high, Central America’s tallest, rising as a spring from the ground.

It moves through the Térraba–Sierpe wetlands, Costa Rica’s largest mangrove system, and reaches Marino Ballena National Park, where formations create a “whale’s tail.” There, currents meet and split—the only place where coastal currents run both north and south year-round.

In a very small area sits extraordinary diversity: roughly half a million species—about 5% of global biodiversity—on 0.03% of Earth’s land. Corcovado National Park alone holds about 2.5% of the world’s biodiversity, tied to wetlands, rivers, and the agricultural basin below Chirripó around Pérez Zeledón.

That richness sits alongside sharp threats. The basin is largely pasture yet leads the world in per-capita use of pesticides and petrochemical fertilizers; runoff moves through the watershed toward wetlands and the Osa Peninsula. For Indigenous nations—including the Boruca, Térraba, Bribrí, and Ngöbe—more than half of their territories are now occupied by non-Indigenous settlers.

Origins and Network

Danny Bell co-founded the Diamante Bridge Collective in 2019 to channel crypto philanthropy toward grassroots regenerative work that often stays invisible—making care for the earth a regenerative livelihood.

The core team describes itself as "a network of ambassadors… in service to this shared mission of bio-regioning (the verb)." Because the Diamante Barú region borders the Osa Peninsula, they see "this fractal element going on here." Eight Indigenous peoples hold territories covering about 7% of Costa Rica, all under extractive pressure.

Activities

🔸

Diamante Luz

 🔸

RECenter the Resource Recycling Center San Salvador

 🔸

Sacred Shield Initiative

 🔸

Participatory Governance Development

 🔸

Diamante Bioregional Trust Development

Vision and Legacy

The consortium frames its purpose in participants’ own words: "We're co-starting a living system of regeneration, and we have the opportunity to protect territory and unify cultures by shifting capital today to fuel a decentralized movement restoring nature and rights with us."

Day to day, that means making "care for the earth a regenerative livelihood"—not a slogan but work supported by decentralized, transparent systems that pay grassroots effort.

Indigenous knowledge and contemporary tools—cryptocurrency, sociocracy, monitoring—are brought together to defend a hyper-biodiverse region by trusting people who already know the watershed and are doing the work.

The bet is direct: capital can reach grassroots action; governance can follow Indigenous protocols and community consensus; and protecting “this tiny little space on Earth” is protecting "2.5% of the world's biodiversity"—planetary stakes rooted in a single watershed.

Sources

📄

Projects

 🎬

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟣 Design School for Regenerating the Earth

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

A global effort founded by Joe Brewer, focused on regenerating Earth's bioregions through place-based action and a growing planetary network of learning exchanges.

🔵 De Plaatsen

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Dutch Foundation De Plaatsen (translated– The Places) is working with farmers and various system players (including banks, marketing cooperatives, supermarkets and citizens) on the realization of 700,000 hectares of sustainable soil management. A vital soil contributes to a better climate, more biodiversity, an attractive landscape, a healthier environment and the regionalization of our food system. There will be at least four Places for the four different soil types in the Netherlands. Cooperative "De Kleine Aarde" in Boxtel is the first Plaats in the Netherlands, focussing on the Zuidelijke Zandgronden bioregion.

Bioregional Context

A region characterized by a mix of fertile agricultural landscapes, historic cities, river deltas, and cross-border cultural exchange with Belgium and Germany. Historically shaped by Catholic traditions, industrial development, and intensive farming, the region is now at the forefront of transitions in agriculture, water management, and spatial planning in response to climate change, biodiversity loss, and soil degradation.

Place
Boxtel, North Brabant, Netherlands

🟣 Dark Matter Labs

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Dark Matter Labs is a transdisciplinary research and design organization focused on reimagining the foundational systems of society—such as governance, finance, infrastructure, and legal frameworks—to support a regenerative and equitable future. Operating globally with a strong European base, Dark Matter Labs collaborates with cities, governments, and civil society to prototype new civic infrastructures and shift societal defaults toward long-term stewardship.

🔵 Cuyahoga River Area of Concern (AOC)

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Cuyahoga River Area of Concern is a major restoration effort focused on bringing life back to 46 miles of river. Local and state partners are working to remove long-standing environmental impairments—like poor water quality and habitat loss—through a Remedial Action Plan. The effort is guided by a diverse coalition of agencies, nonprofits, and community members, all contributing to a healthier, more resilient watershed through practical actions like wetland restoration, dam removals, and neighborhood-scale stewardship.

Bioregional Context

The Cuyahoga River flows through northeast Ohio, winding through Cleveland before emptying into Lake Erie. The Area of Concern encompasses a heavily urbanized and industrial corridor that was once emblematic of environmental neglect—the Cuyahoga famously caught fire in 1969. Today, the watershed represents a symbol of ecological renewal, where efforts to address historical pollution intersect with urban resilience, community health, and biodiversity recovery.

Place
Cuyahoga River Bridge, Riverview Rd, Peninsula, OH 44264, United States
links
https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/f8eabaed2c584255952328f6c03af878,|https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1462901121003750|https://www.notion.so/Urban-Ecologies-1fad03fcfeeb80de8278cf45b91205e0?pvs=21|https://www.notion.so/River-Watersheds-1f9d03fcfeeb80ac9ca5d4de926ce12c?pvs=21

🟡 Curridabat Sweet City

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The city follows a distinctive "Sweet City" framework that treats pollinators and other non-human life as citizens– vital members of the urban community. This approach reimagines urban governance by recognizing biodiversity—and even emotion—as essential infrastructure.

Bioregional Context

Curridabat is an urban area in Costa Rica that has pioneered an innovative approach to city management and biodiversity integration. The region faces typical urban challenges including resource consumption exceeding planetary boundaries and disconnection from natural systems.

Place
Curridabat, San Jose, Costa Rica
links
https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE

Tags

🪶 Interspecies_Mutualism 🦏 Rights_of_Nature 🍥 Governance_Design 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies

Background

Curridabat’s “Sweet City” experiment began from a stubborn urban fact: cities cover only about 3% of Earth’s surface yet draw far more than their share of planetary resources. Leaders framed the problem as a design question: how can we grow a city and a way of living that adds value to the planet instead of extracting value from it?

That question cannot be settled by human systems alone. Curridabat therefore took seriously two dimensions often sidelined in conventional politics: feelings and biodiversity as infrastructure of place—what makes a city livable and what makes it ecologically viable.

Origins and Network

The initiative took shape under 12 years of mayoral leadership by Edgar Mora. What began as familiar urban administration gradually became a different framework—one that reimagines development and ties city health to planetary health.

As that shift deepened, Curridabat rebranded as “Sweet City” (Ciudad Dulce), signaling that pollinators and biodiversity were central to its identity, not decorative extras.

Activities

🔸

Pollinator Citizenship Program

 🔸

Governance Platform Methodology

 🔸

Archetype-Based Planning

Vision and Legacy

Curridabat’s Sweet City work has drawn wide recognition and helped put the municipality on the map internationally. It illustrates how urban governance can shift from extractive to regenerative logics—adding value to planetary systems rather than only drawing them down.

By treating emotion and biodiversity as infrastructure, Curridabat widened what counts as a legitimate public concern and made room for more holistic development. Cities are many systems at once; engaging non-human systems such as pollination yields information that can reshape how people govern.

Sources

🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 7

🔵 Cultivate Ireland

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A workers' cooperative committed to sustainability and regenerative practices based in the Cloughjordan Ecovillage. Their Community Climate Coaches initiative trains local facilitators to engage communities in climate resilience planning through a collaborative evaluation program. The program develops competency frameworks, toolkits, and training manuals for climate coaches who facilitate dialogues in four bioregional hubs. These coaches document community-led initiatives through specialized mapping systems, ensuring community perspectives directly inform local climate policy and create socially and politically binding outcomes.

Bioregional Context

County Tipperary in central Ireland is characterized by a mix of agricultural landscapes, rolling countryside, and the Galtee Mountains. The region has strong farming traditions and a growing focus on sustainable approaches to land management and community development. Home to the pioneering Cloughjordan Ecovillage, this rural area has become a hub for ecological innovation while maintaining deep connections to its agricultural heritage.

Place
County Tipperary, Ireland
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGc1cZg818/QTw8PNm_Z6GG98iW_aCqBg/edit

🔵 Crown Managers Partnership (CMP)

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A voluntary collaboration among federal, state, provincial, Tribal, and First Nation agency managers and universities spanning Montana, Alberta, and British Columbia. The partnership works across jurisdictional boundaries to address shared ecological challenges in the Crown of the Continent Ecosystem, recognizing that no single agency has the mandate or resources to independently address the region's environmental concerns.

Bioregional Context

The Crown of the Continent Ecosystem spans approximately 18 million acres across the USA-Canada border, encompassing the shared Rocky Mountain region where Montana, Alberta, and British Columbia meet. This bioregion is characterized by rugged mountains, glaciers, diverse forests, and headwaters for three major continental river systems. The area supports diverse wildlife populations including grizzly bears, wolves, and wolverines.

Place
MT, United States

🟡 Costa Rica Regenerativa

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A holistic development initiative launched in 2018 that focuses on demonstrating regenerative practices as viable alternatives to extractive systems, shifting from passive to participatory societies, upholding feminine principles and cultural preservation, and supporting locally led decision-making and inclusive economic models.

Bioregional Context

The Guanacaste region comprises approximately 380,000 hectares of primarily cattle land, with a history of livestock farming spanning 400 years. The area faces significant environmental challenges, including being a world leader in pesticide use per capita, with 64% of fresh vegetables containing unsafe pesticide levels. The region has experienced substantial soil degradation, losing approximately 200 tons of topsoil per hectare annually.

Place
Guanacaste, Costa Rica
links
https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE

Tags

🍽️ Community_Food_Access 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🐮 Grassland_Management 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🎒 Learning_Programs 📉 Ecological_Monitoring

Background


Costa Rica’s global reputation for conservation and carbon neutrality sits uneasily beside Guanacaste’s reality: 380,000 cattle hectares after four hundred years of ranching, heavy pesticide use, and soil loss—75% of national soil already gone, 95% loss by 2050 projected, with brown river plumes visible far offshore after rain. Latin America and the Caribbean, one of Earth’s most biodiverse regions, have lost 94% abundance. As Eduardo Muñoz puts it, the crisis is less about charismatic megafauna than about the sheer volume of life slipping away: "So when people look at me with squared eyes—oh, it's not about the panda bear, it's not about the polar bear—no, it's about the amount of life that we're losing."

Origins and Network


Costa Rica Regenerativa began in 2018 to show regeneration on the ground—not only in theory—at a scale that could eventually reach the country. Muñoz frames the aim as building a future from abundance rather than scarcity through regeneration: "What we're trying to do is build a better future and a future that is based on abundance and not scarcity, and do this through regeneration."

Philosophy & Approach


The group’s mental model places a living Earth at the base of a torus and spiritual connection at the top—dimensions that make some academics uncomfortable but that Muñoz treats as necessary for any transition: lasting change needs the ongoing integration of nature with a sense of something larger than ourselves.

Regenerative development, in their usage, is holistic rather than reductionist: benefits for many kinds of life, measured in hope beyond GDP metrics—hope, education, transparency, quality, peace, and conditions that enable life, not only minimized harm. A core shift is from “humans and nature” to humans as nature—simple to say, hard to live, and prerequisite to real progress. They argue for local cooperative democracy in place of democracy-as-usual that no longer delivers, and for bringing in youth—especially young women—while moving aside exhausted political styles.

Culture is identity glue, not spectacle, and it is eroding faster than biodiversity. The “feminine principle” ties food, care, and heritage to how landscapes are treated. Philosophically they move from a vertical, human-centered hierarchy toward an ecosystem frame and then toward a life-centered ethic—knowledge, technology, and wisdom in service of life, reversing the old order. Responsibility for that future is collective, not ministers; it sits with people on the ground.

Activities

🔸

Community Food Security Project

 🔸

Savory Hub Initiative

 🔸

Regenerative Agriculture Program

 🔸

Educational Hive

 🔸

MRV Collective Development

Vision and Legacy


Costa Rica Regenerativa keeps demonstrating regeneration across food, grasslands, education, and measurement—often ahead of government, regrettably, but visibly enough to persuade. Muñoz ends on intergenerational urgency: hope that humanity can still change course in time, that a new human–nature relationship is possible, and a father’s question—whether his son will be ready for that future.

The initiative continues to show that restoring land, communities, and biodiversity can be practical, healing, and economically credible—not optional.

Sources

🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 7

🔵 Coral Triangle Initiative

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Coral Triangle Initiative on Coral Reefs, Fisheries and Food Security (CTI-CFF) is a multilateral partnership launched in 2009 to safeguard the rich marine and coastal ecosystems of the Coral Triangle region. Comprised of six countries—Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, and Timor-Leste—the initiative supports collaborative governance, ecosystem-based management, and community resilience across some of the most biodiverse waters on Earth.

Bioregional Context

The Coral Triangle is a vast marine bioregion covering nearly 6 million square kilometers across the tropical waters of Southeast Asia and the western Pacific. It is often called the "Amazon of the Seas" due to its extraordinary marine biodiversity, hosting over 75% of the world's coral species and more than 2,000 species of reef fish. This region supports millions of livelihoods tied to fishing, tourism, and coastal agriculture, while also serving as a cultural and ecological keystone for Indigenous and coastal communities. However, it faces mounting threats from overfishing, coral bleaching, habitat loss, and climate change.

Place
Denpasar, Indonesia

🟡 Comunidad Indigena José Luis Caniulef

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A Mapuche community in southern Chile working to document environmental changes, strengthen biocultural memory, and develop regenerative land management practices through the integration of traditional knowledge and scientific approaches.

Bioregional Context

Located in southern Chile at the boundary of the Los Ríos region, within the traditional Wallmapu territory. The area is characterized by high biodiversity, abundant water bodies, and evergreen forests. Situated near the Pacific Ring of Fire, with cultural and ecological significance tied to active volcanoes, particularly the Villarrica volcano.

Place
Parques de Afunalhue, 4930000 Villarrica, Araucania, Chile
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EcpcXG1iGr0&t=7s&ab_channel=ComunidadIndigenaJos%C3%A9LuisCaniulef|https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8

Tags

🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 👁️‍🗨️ Storying_Place 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🎒 Learning_Programs 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🔘 Governance_Bodies

Background


The Comunidad Indígena José Luis Caniulef descends from people who moved inland from Lican Ray on Lake Calafquén centuries ago. The name Caniulef—“fast vultures,” perhaps from spring flocks—stuck to this river-crossed forest. The place is Afunalhue: in Mapudungún, afu is what has ended or rotted and alhue is soul—a name for souls that once were here and no longer are, echoing traces of earlier inhabitants when the family arrived.

The community sits in Wallmapu (Mapuche territory from ocean to ocean), at the edge of Chile’s Los Ríos region, near the Villarrica volcano, on 562 collective acres in the Pacific Ring of Fire.

After the War of the Pacific, Chile and Argentina’s campaigns stripped and partitioned territory; roughly 10% stayed with Mapuche communities. Forests fell for export; later, dictatorship-era pine plantations and climate stress fed thirty years of megafires. At the head of the Cruces basin, Afunalhue faces tourism pressure, parcel sales, deforestation, polluted lakes, and mining—patterns repeated across southern lake country.

Origins and Network


José Luis Caniulef filed to protect Mapuche lands under Chilean law as the state pressed dispossession. José Luis Caniulef Huanquimil—leader and father of present members—defended the land on horseback and went to Santiago to secure title against Spanish-descended settlers.

About 67 people, average age near 50, contend with illness, aging and outmigration, weak territorial planning, frayed participation, poverty and few jobs, and fading biocultural memory. On collective land they farm, host visitors, and keep bees.

Post-pandemic, the Afunalhue Project (2021–) asks how Indigenous knowledge and ecology can together track change and build resilience. It has Ministry of Science funding across three linked lines of work.

Activities

🔸

Oral History Collection and Memory Mapping

 🔸

Ecological Assessment and Monitoring

 🔸

Community Engagement and Education

 🔸

Participatory Strategy Development

Vision and Legacy


The project has strengthened bonds under pressure. One elder likened rivers to community life: waters branch and reunite, sometimes stall, then move—history, memory, and ties remain the channel that orients them.

What helps: spaces for youth, intergenerational work, and treating social knowledge, science, and education as one fabric—transdisciplinarity as a basis for regeneration. Leading from the territory and earning trust matters; so does moving from transactional ties or “being voters” to active stewardship.

Hard parts include sharing technical results where many elders do not use phones or read. Dependence on external corporate-style funding risks cycles of obligation; the community wants stronger local finance rather than serving as a “last fix” for global harm. Participation can stay reactive; capacity stays thin without money; technical language is hard to land; and loss of Mapuche practice complicates intervention.

The horizon is still clear: work across scales, follow water to the sea, and hold regeneration as shared work—neither simple nor one single agenda, but anchored in consensus that gives the effort meaning.

Sources

🎬

El lewfu que volverá a correr

🟣 Commonland

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

Commonland brings people together to restore landscapes with their holistic approach to landscape restoration - the 4 Returns framework.

Network Affiliation

🟡 Alvelal 🟡 Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca

🟡 Colombia Regenerativa

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A network of regions working from the grassroots level to promote ecological restoration and agroecological production, focusing on returning fertility to soil and communities. The initiative collaborates with the Global Network of Regenerative Communities to share knowledge and experiences with other regions worldwide, particularly in Latin America.

Bioregional Context

Colombia encompasses diverse bioregions from Andean mountains and valleys to Caribbean and Pacific coastlines, Amazon rainforest, and extensive plains. This megadiverse country faces significant challenges from deforestation, soil degradation, and climate change impacts.

Place
Colombia
links
https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8

Tags

🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 🪢 Network_Weaving 💬 Peer_Learning 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 📖 Knowledge_Artefacts 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


Colombia stands as one of the world's most megadiverse countries, encompassing dramatically varied bioregions that range from Andean mountain systems and high-altitude valleys to Caribbean and Pacific coastlines, dense Amazon rainforest, and vast eastern plains. This biological and cultural richness has made Colombia home to extraordinary biodiversity and dozens of Indigenous and Afro-Colombian communities who have stewarded these lands for millennia.

Yet this same diversity faces mounting pressure. Decades of deforestation, soil degradation, extractive economies, and climate change impacts have placed immense strain on Colombia's ecosystems and the communities that depend on them. As the country commits to reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 50% by 2030, the need for regenerative approaches that restore both land and communities has never been more urgent.

Origins and Network


Colombia Regenerativa emerged from a vision to unite bioregions across the country and generate impact at a national scale. While each region maintains its unique identity and grassroots connection, the initiative seeks to give impulse to a broader narrative about bioregionalization.


Philosophy & Approach


The organization bases its work on Eduardo Muñoz's regenerative development model, which centers Mother Earth as the foundation of the bioregion. This model recognizes that personal and spiritual connections flow outward to create governance and regenerative economic systems that operate in cycles within a territory, fundamentally about the connection between territories and people. This grounding in regenerative principles distinguishes Colombia Regenerativa from conventional development approaches, honoring what life itself is revealing about how to thrive in each place.

Activities

🔸

Network Building and Learning Exchange

 🔸

Three Rings Evaluation Process

 🔸

Virtual Gaia Digital Mapping Platform

 🔸

Bioregionalization Course Development

Vision and Legacy


Financing regeneration remains the hardest gap: capital exists, but channels rarely reach living landscapes. The team describes a possible opening—a way to “touch the membrane” between financial abstraction and concrete bioregional work—so resources can flow toward repair without pretending ecosystems are ordinary assets.

Discussion keeps returning to capacity and governance: not only technical “ecosystemic assistance,” but investment in how communities assemble, decide, and hold territory—with social, governance, and economic processes aligned to planetary limits rather than extractive defaults.

Across evaluation, mapping, education, and experiments in finance, Colombia Regenerativa is weaving a vision of many bioregions contributing national resilience—where diversity of place becomes a source of wisdom instead of a ledger of loss.

🟡 Carolinian Canada

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Carolinian Canada is a cross-sector network working to regenerate the unique ecosystems of the Carolinian Zone in southern Ontario, from Toronto to Windsor. Grounded in the principles of reconciliation, the organization connects science, community, and business to support healthy landscapes, biodiversity, and climate-resilient communities.

Bioregional Context

The Carolinian Zone is the warmest and most biodiverse region of Canada, stretching across the southernmost parts of Ontario from Toronto to Windsor. This area represents less than 1% of Canada's landmass but is home to over 40% of its native plant species and a high concentration of rare and endangered wildlife. Its temperate climate, fertile soils, and proximity to the Great Lakes have made it a center for agriculture, urban development, and transportation—putting immense pressure on its ecosystems.

Place
ON, Canada

Tags

🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science

Background


The Carolinian Zone (Lake Erie Lowland) stretches Windsor–Toronto—Canada’s southernmost ecoregion, at the northern edge of the eastern deciduous forest. Between the Great Lakes, its mild climate supports exceptional diversity: ~2,200 herbaceous plants, ~400 bird species, and some of the country’s most productive farmland.

It is the traditional territory of Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, Lunaapeew, Wendat, and Attawandaron nations, many of whom remain present with long-standing treaty relationships.

Pressures are acute: the zone covers only about 0.25% of Canada’s land but roughly 25% of its population. About 85% of the ecoregion is now cropland, pasture, or urban; forest cover has fallen from ~80% to ~11% and wetlands from ~28% to ~5%. The zone holds more than half of Canada’s federally listed species at risk—Ontario’s most imperiled region—while still supporting millions of people and high-yield agriculture. Action must fit that multi-use, multi-claim landscape.

Origins and Network


Formed in 1984 by scientists and local groups alarmed by habitat loss, Carolinian Canada advances collaborative conservation across the zone. The 2000 “Big Picture” partnership with Ontario’s Natural Heritage Information Centre shifted planning from scattered “islands of green” toward a connected natural heritage system—still central to the Healthy Landscape Strategy and used by governments, conservation authorities, landowners, and communities.

The coalition names its settler-conservation roots and, since a 2018 board directive, is Indigenizing the network—Two-Eyed Seeing, ethical space, and expanded Indigenous leadership in land and water stewardship.

Philosophy & Approach


Carolinian Canada integrates Indigenous and Western knowledge systems in what they call a "Two-Eyed Seeing" approach. Rather than simply consulting Indigenous communities (as many conservation projects do), their projects are co-developed from the ground up in "ethical space" where Indigenous and Western worldviews are given equal weight. This is transformative because:

- It acknowledges Indigenous rights, sovereignty, and contributions to land stewardship
- It operates in "ethical space" where neither knowledge system dominates
- It incorporates traditional ecological knowledge into both implementation and evaluation
- It goes beyond consultation to active engagement, acknowledging Indigenous rights and land sovereignty, Indigenous contributions to land stewardship and conservation, and the cultural and relational significance of the land

Activities

🔸

Healthy Landscape Strategy

 🔸

Deshkan Ziibi Conservation Impact Bond

 🔸

In the Zone Habitat Tracker

Vision and Legacy


Carolinian Canada’s long-term aim is community-led, climate-resilient, healthy landscapes across southern Ontario—in reconciliation, not only in technical conservation. The DZCIB is one prototype for blended, outcome-based finance, but the network’s wider role is to convene Indigenous, civic, land, and finance partners; make biodiversity easier to fund; and embed Two-Eyed Seeing in practice. Scaling will adapt metrics to place while keeping holistic intent. Where finance appears, it is framed as repairing relationships—between peoples and with land—not only transacting for acres.

Sources

📊

Growing a Prosperous Green Future Together - Strategic Plan 2024-2028

 📊

The Deshkan Ziibi Conservation Impact Bond Project

🟣 Capital Institute

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

This initiative brings together a global ecosystem of over 1,500 changemakers who blend modern science, storytelling, and timeless wisdom traditions to guide the shift from an extractive to a regenerative economy. Their work focuses on fostering economic systems that support the long-term well-being of people, the planet, and business.

🟣 COBALT

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

Collaborative for Bioregional Action Learning & Transformation (COBALT) guides fellows through a shared journey of discovery centered on bioregionalism. Their work focuses on three core areas– immersive Bioregional Learning Journeys, the use of Data & Storytelling to deepen understanding and connection, and the development of Bioregional Digital Twins to support place-based systems thinking and decision-making.

🟡 CEDPO

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Centro Estratégico de Desarrollo de la Península de Osa (CEDPO) is a collaborative platform working to unify local, Indigenous, and institutional actors around a shared vision for a regenerative future in Costa Rica's Osa Peninsula, rooted in system mapping, community governance, and ecological stewardship.

Bioregional Context

The Osa Peninsula, located on the southern Pacific coast of Costa Rica, is one of the most biodiverse regions on Earth. Home to roughly 2.5% of the planet's terrestrial species in less than 0.001% of its land area, it includes Corcovado National Park, extensive lowland rainforests, mangroves, and marine ecosystems.

Place
Costa Rica
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc|https://youtu.be/Ip_SmDZgC2o

Tags

🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 🔘 Governance_Bodies 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🗂️ Portfolio_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles

Background


The Osa Peninsula stretches along Costa Rica's southern Pacific coast, a landscape that National Geographic has recognized as one of the most biologically intense places on Earth. This remarkable region contains 2.5% of the world's biodiversity and 5% of all plant and animal species within just three millionths of Earth's surface—a concentration of life that defies comprehension. The peninsula encompasses Corcovado National Park, the largest undeveloped stretch of lowland rainforest globally, Central America's largest Pacific coast mangrove forest, and the Gulf of Dulce, one of only four tropical fjords in the world. This ecological richness exists alongside Ngäbe Indigenous communities and Costa Rican cultures who have stewarded these lands for generations.

Yet this extraordinary place faces mounting pressures: unsustainable development patterns, accelerating international land acquisition, poverty existing alongside conservation efforts, and cultural erosion. These challenges create vicious cycles where economic desperation drives illegal deforestation, poaching, and mining—activities that undermine the very ecosystems communities depend upon.

Origins and Network


The Centro Estratégico de Desarrollo de la Península de Osa (CEDPO) emerged from a decade-long collaboration between Regenerative Earth and local Osa Peninsula communities, formally established in June 2022. The organization represents a strategic response to the region's complex challenges, bringing together diverse actors around a shared vision for regenerative development.

Activities

🔸

Participatory Systems Mapping

 🔸

Multi-Stakeholder Governance

 🔸

Project Portfolio Development

 🔸

Bioregional Financing Facility Development

Vision and Legacy


CEDPO's three-year plan (2025-2027) includes establishing the Costa Rican Bioregional Financing Facility, building out the core operations team, and continuing strategic investments in projects that promote a regenerative future for the Osa Peninsula. The organization aims to ensure that development serves local communities while preserving the region's extraordinary ecological integrity, avoiding the extractive patterns seen elsewhere in Costa Rica.

Osa’s challenges do not map neatly onto other regions: deep inequality, youth pulled into illicit economies for income, and tourism pressure that can repeat Guanacaste-style displacement when capital is foreign and skills stay imported. CEDPO points instead to Arenal San Carlos—locally owned hotels and restaurants—as the counter-pattern: build capabilities so residents lead development and keep value and stewardship in place.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Osa Peninsula

 🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Burrenbeo Trust

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Burren Programme supports sustainable farming in western Ireland through its "pocket, head, and heart" approach– creating viable economic incentives for farmers ("pocket"), offering technical guidance and knowledge-sharing ("head"), and fostering trust and emotional connection with local communities ("heart").

Bioregional Context

Located in the west of Ireland along the Atlantic coast, the Burren is characterized by its unique limestone landscape. The region contains approximately 70% of Ireland's native flora, including a remarkable mix of Arctic, Alpine, and Mediterranean plants. This biodiverse landscape has been continuously farmed for approximately 6,000 years, creating a delicate balance between human activity and natural systems.

Place
Burren, Ennis, Ireland

Tags

🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 🔀 Grants_Programs 👩‍🌾 Farmer_Support ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🪢 Network_Weaving 💬 Peer_Learning 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🎒 Learning_Programs 🎉 Congressing_and_Events

Background


The Burren is a bare karstified limestone landscape in the west of Ireland, on the Atlantic coast—a rare and unique place that contains about 70% of Ireland's native flora. The region is remarkable for its mix of Arctic, Alpine, and Mediterranean plants growing together on these grasslands. In a country facing a declared biodiversity crisis and climate crisis, the Burren represents one of the last great landscapes that is relatively intact and highly biodiverse.

Yet this landscape is fundamentally a farmed one—people have lived and farmed using livestock here for approximately 6,000 years, with archaeological sites dating to that period. The Burren has always existed in a delicate balance with farming: too much farming and the flowers start to disappear due to overly intensive management, but too little farming and the Burren tends to revert to scrub and woodland, losing much of its biodiversity as well.

Origins and Network


The mission of the Burren Programme has been to work with farmers, viewing them as a resource and mobilizing that resource to improve the ecosystem services they deliver from this landscape. This means moving from a position where farmers have just been rewarded and encouraged to produce food, to one where they produce food in a way that also produces these other increasingly rare and important services for society.

Philosophy & Approach


The Burren Programme's approach has crystallized around three fundamental principles that are essential for mobilizing farmers as an environmental resource: the pocket, the head, and the heart.

Activities

🔸

Results-Based Farming Program

 🔸

Farm Infrastructure Support Initiative

 🔸

New Revenue Development

 🔸

Burren Peer-to-Peer Learning Network

 🔸

Farming Hub

 🔸

Burren Public Engagement Program

Vision and Legacy


Fifteen years of field monitoring under the results-based model show year-on-year gains in environmental health since 2010. For under one million euros a year, the programme has bent the curve on biodiversity decline. Evaluations underline a wider point: when the relationship between people and place works, farming can deliver for nature, for rural economies, and for social outcomes together—not as trade-offs.

🟣 Buckminster Fuller Institute

Support Provider
🌐 Visit website

The Buckminster Fuller Institute (BFI) supports systemic approaches to solving global challenges through the lens of regenerative design. In its bioregional work, BFI focuses on enabling place-based transformation by fostering "trimtab" initiatives—small, strategic interventions with outsized impact. The institute supports bioregional movements by curating learning exchanges, mapping regenerative projects, and advancing design principles rooted in local ecological and cultural systems.

Bioregional Context

BFI's bioregional work spans multiple regions globally, supporting place-based transformation through regenerative design principles. The institute focuses on enabling strategic interventions that can catalyze systemic change across diverse bioregional contexts.

links
https://youtu.be/Vgt9g24gi7Q

🟡 Bosque Fractal

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Bosque Fractal is a biocultural regeneration initiative rooted in the Andean headwaters of Colombia, on sacred Muiska territory where male and female rivers meet. It began as a grassroots effort to protect an 80-hectare forest from private sale, and has since evolved into a living laboratory for land restoration, spiritual healing, and commons governance. Deeply grounded in place, the project weaves ecology, ritual, and relational finance into a vision of territory as teacher. Bosque Fractal does not aim to scale, but to inspire fractal repetition — a pattern of community-held regeneration that can ripple across other watersheds and sacred sites.

Bioregional Context

The Valle de Zaquencipá is a high-altitude valley nestled in the Eastern Andes of Colombia's Boyacá department. This bioregion, once a sacred and strategic territory of the Muisca civilization, features a mix of dry forests, mountain slopes, and riparian corridors — many of which are now degraded due to centuries of deforestation, colonial enclosure, and monoculture. Despite ecological pressure and cultural erosion, the valley holds a powerful legacy of memory, biocultural knowledge, and spiritual significance.

Place
Observatorio Astronómico de Zaquencipá: Villa de Leyva, colombia, 154247 Santa Sofía, Colombia
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Ow3Vv0MsSzIaAfJOx1y5bHyWWN9bmMaU56zhuoJFVRs/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices 📷 Visitor_Experiences 👁️‍🗨️ Storying_Place 📖 Knowledge_Artefacts 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models 💲 Structuring_and_Capitalization 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 💧 Watershed_Repair ⛲ Water_Management

Background

The Valle de Zaquencipá is considered the heart of Colombia and of the Americas. Nestled high in the Andes, this region is not only ecologically vital—it is profoundly spiritually significant, carrying the ancestral wisdom of the Muisca people, who were known as the People of Water. This valley is where the desert meets the lush Andean forests, a place where masculine and feminine energies converge, and where profound opportunities exist for peace and regeneration.

For the Muisca ancestors, this valley was a sacred territory, balanced between the masculine lands (marked in yellow on ancestral maps) and the feminine lands (in green). The pink region of Zaquencipá represented the union of masculine and feminine, a sacred harmony that sustained life for millennia. This land is also connected to the Kogi, Arhuaco, and other indigenous knowledge keepers of Colombia, forming part of a global water reserve.

The valley's ecological importance cannot be overstated. Although this ecosystem occupies just 1.7% of Colombia's páramos (high-altitude wetlands), it provides 85% of the country's drinking water. It is also Colombia's food pantry, producing over half the food consumed in the capital, Bogotá, and is recognized as a potential UNESCO Geopark for its geological and cultural heritage.

The region's history is unique: unlike much of Colombia, which has endured 60 years of armed conflict, Zaquencipá has remained a land of peace. This peaceful legacy creates fertile ground for transforming past mining culture into a regenerative economy, inspiring Colombia—and the world—toward a future rooted in harmony with nature.

Origins and Network

Bosque Fractal began as a grassroots effort to protect an 80-hectare forest from private sale. What started as an act of land protection has since evolved into a living laboratory for land restoration, spiritual healing, and commons governance. Deeply grounded in place, the project weaves ecology, ritual, and relational finance into a vision of territory as teacher.

The initiative sees this valley as a place of cascading potential for regeneration. The mission is to weave together individual efforts into a collective movement for ecological restoration, spiritual development, and peacebuilding and cultural renewal. Bosque Fractal does not aim to scale in the traditional sense, but to inspire fractal repetition—a pattern of community-held regeneration that can ripple across other watersheds and sacred sites.

This work is not only ecological—it is profoundly spiritual. Years ago, the founder led a peace camp in Bogotá, sleeping 45 nights on sacred ground until they signed a peace accord. That experience led to Zaquencipá, where a Temple of Peace was built, represented by a sacred dome.

Regeneration starts from within. Spiritual practices like ceremonial gatherings, tamascal (sweat lodges), and sacred pipe traditions guide the work. The oak forests surrounding the valley are themselves ancient elders, teaching how to heal and regenerate both the land and the human spirit together.

Activities

🔸

Regenerative Sanctuary

 🔸

The Living Atlas

 🔸

Bosque Fractal Governance and Finance

 🔸

Ecological Restoration of Valle de Zaquencipá

Vision and Legacy

The first priority is to unite and strengthen bonds among the many regenerative projects already active in the region. Rather than imposing new solutions, the work is about bringing together and amplifying what's already working, creating cascading potential for regeneration.

Bosque Fractal's funding priorities are sequenced to build long-term regeneration:

Phase 1: Bioregional Organizing ($300K) - This is the main focus for the first 2 years. The priority is to unite and strengthen bonds among the many regenerative projects already active in the region. This is not about imposing new solutions but about bringing together and amplifying what's already working. This phase includes uniting projects, weaving relationships, and developing the Living Atlas.

Phase 2: Learning & Exchange - Building a guest house for workshops and cross-bioregional collaboration, sharing regenerative practices with visitors from around the world.

Phase 3: Community Infrastructure - Constructing the rainwater plant and developing a bioregional demonstration zone in Villa de Leyva (a historic town and tourism hub) to showcase regenerative solutions and attract investment.

The vision invites partnership in three ways:
- As a partner, co-creating the Living Atlas and participating in regenerative learning journeys
- As a funder, helping seed this transformation with resources needed to unite projects, restore oak forests, and build water and education infrastructure
- As a visitor, coming to Zaquencipá to witness the whispers of Mother Earth and experience this living temple of peace

Bosque Fractal does not aim to scale in the traditional sense, but to inspire fractal repetition—a pattern of community-held regeneration that can ripple across other watersheds and sacred sites, demonstrating how territory itself can be a teacher when approached with reverence, ritual, and relational finance.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 1

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Blueprint for Impact

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Blueprint for Impact is a cross-sector collaborative formed in response to the catastrophic flooding of 2021 in Southern Appalachia. The initiative emerged as a grassroots response to the failures of conventional recovery systems and evolved into a long-term, community-driven effort to catalyze regeneration across multiple systems — including food, housing, health, forestry, water, and arts. Blueprint brings together over 50 organizations, practitioners, artists, healers, and policy advocates to build resilient, bioregionally grounded systems that can withstand future shocks.

Bioregional Context

Blueprint for Impact is rooted in Southern Appalachia, a region defined by the ancient Appalachian Mountains stretching across western North Carolina, eastern Tennessee, and surrounding areas. This bioregion is known for its rich biodiversity, deep cultural heritage, and resilient land-based communities. Despite its ecological abundance and traditions of mutual aid, the region has experienced centuries of extractive economies, from coal mining to industrial forestry, alongside structural neglect.

Place
GA-2, Blue Ridge, GA 30513, United States
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Ow3Vv0MsSzIaAfJOx1y5bHyWWN9bmMaU56zhuoJFVRs/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🌋 Disaster_Relief 🫂 Health_and_Social_Care 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🗂️ Portfolio_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development

Background


Southern Appalachia is a region of profound contrasts and contradictions. It is home to the most visited U.S. national park (the Great Smoky Mountains) and the most visited scenic parkway (the Blue Ridge Parkway), attracting 14 million visitors annually to a region where Asheville, North Carolina—the heart of this landscape—has just 280,000 residents. For centuries, this region has been a hub for healing, arts, music, and culture, with forests and mountains deeply woven into its identity.

Yet beneath this surface of natural beauty and cultural richness lies an untold story of economic fragility. The economy relies heavily on tourism driven by outsiders, while nonprofits and community organizations depend on federal and state funding because local wealth is scarce.

In September 2024, Hurricane Helene devastated the region, causing $60 billion in damages. With federal funding now constrained, businesses and nonprofits are struggling. Helene made clear: the region must empower itself from within, taking back the narrative and gaining control over its destiny.

Origins and Network


Blueprint for Impact is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization formed by a team of financing experts and storytellers who collaborate with high-impact initiatives throughout Southern Appalachia. Blueprint operates with a small, dedicated team of four, backed by many partners across the region.

Philosophy & Approach


The organization believes innovative financing can help solve the region's structural imbalances by building the financial infrastructure that nonprofits and communities need to seed long-term regeneration. The work focuses on three interconnected pillars:
1. Collaborate – Build portfolios of regenerative projects that form an interconnected ecosystem
2. Finance – Design funding solutions that go beyond philanthropy to tap private investment and build financial infrastructure
3. Amplify – Use media to shift narratives and elevate local stories to national attention

Activities

🔸

Cross-Sector Collaborative Infrastructure

 🔸

Post-Disaster Regenerative Recovery Model

 🔸

Appalachian Cultural Infrastructure

 🔸

Development Portfolio

 🔸

Appalachia Community Capital Design

Vision and Legacy


The organization is raising $650,000 in seed funding to build financing and visibility, attract long-term funders and investors, and empower the region to control its narrative and resources. Success would mean capital flows that answer to Appalachian priorities—land, culture, and interdependence—rather than extractive cycles that return only briefly after each shock.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 1

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Blue Ridge Foodshed Fund

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Blue Ridge Foodshed Trust emerged in the aftermath of Hurricane Helen as a response to the mounting instability of the global food system and the deep vulnerabilities of rural communities. The Trust's mission is to localize food systems by supporting small-scale farmers, building regenerative distribution infrastructure, and addressing food access through community-centered investment.

Bioregional Context

The Blue Ridge bioregion spans the ancient Appalachian Mountains of the eastern United States, encompassing parts of Virginia, North Carolina, and Tennessee. Characterized by temperate forests, rolling hills, and biodiverse watersheds, the region has long supported resilient agricultural traditions and community-based food systems.

Place
Southern Blue, 132 W Bankhead St, New Albany, MS 38652-3313, United States
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Ow3Vv0MsSzIaAfJOx1y5bHyWWN9bmMaU56zhuoJFVRs/edit?usp=sharing

Tags

🍽️ Community_Food_Access 🚚 Regional_Sourcing ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models 🍥 Governance_Design

Background


The Blue Ridge Mountains are among the oldest and most biodiverse landscapes on Earth—over 1.2 billion years old, older than the Atlantic Ocean. Cherokee people and their ancestors have stewarded these mountains for roughly 12,000 years. In September 2024, Hurricane Helene tore through the region and exposed a structural tension: western North Carolina still hosts more than 5,000 family farms, numerous aggregators and distributors, and 40 farmers' markets, yet only about 4% of food purchased locally is produced in the region—underscoring how fragile long supply chains are and why many residents are recommitting to bioregional food sovereignty.

Origins and Network


The Blue Ridge Foodshed Trust was formed as a nonprofit corporation with a mission to rapidly localize the regional food supply. The Trust brings funding and resources into the region through trusted relationships with mission-aligned partners. Rooted in the Western North Carolina community, the Trust serves as a valuable intermediary with deep knowledge of the foodshed's challenges, needs, and assets.

Activities

🔸

Farm Food Access Program

 🔸

WNC Food Hub Collaborative

 🔸

Institutional and Commercial Purchasing

 🔸

Blue Ridge Community Land Agreements

 🔸

Blue Ridge Foodshed Capital Infrastructure

 🔸

Producer-Centered Governance Design

Vision and Legacy


Localizing western North Carolina’s food system offers measurable returns in four areas: economic vitality, public health, ecological repair, and community resilience. The Trust treats those returns as mutually reinforcing—farm income, eater access, watershed health, and civic capacity rising together when capital, land, and governance align around producers.

Sources

📊

Food Economy

 🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 1

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟣 Bloom Network

Learning Network
🌐 Visit website

Place-based social network that pays grants and dividends to bioregional and other scale place-based community development work who are members of its network.

🟡 Bioregioning Tayside

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Bioregioning Tayside takes a non-anthropocentric, place-based approach to regional regeneration, centering ecological systems alongside human communities. Guided by the principle "change the frame, change the story," the initiative aims to cultivate new narratives of place that support resilience in the face of climate disruption, biodiversity loss, and economic instability.

Bioregional Context

A substantial region in Central Scotland centered around the River Tay catchment system, extending from mountains through to the Tay estuary. The area encompasses diverse landscapes, including one of Europe's most important Atlantic salmon spawning grounds. Their bioregion represents a significant portion of central Scotland's landmass, with varied ecosystems from highland to coastal areas.

Place
Perth, Scotland, United Kingdom
links
https://storymaps.arcgis.com/collections/c12190aeb01b47679345aa4c1d45a2e0|https://erichtcatchment.scot/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Stocktake-and-Gap-Analysis_summary-compressed.pdf

Tags

🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 💧 Watershed_Repair 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 📋 Finance_Assesments 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Social_or_Network_Mapping

Background

Tayside is a large swath of central Scotland organized around the River Tay catchment—from mountain headwaters to the estuary—where Atlantic salmon and varied ecosystems tie upland, strath, and coast into one hydrological story. The initiative treats that whole gradient as the practical frame for regeneration work.

Origins and Network

Bioregioning Tayside grew out of the Cateran Ecomuseum’s “museum in the landscape,” which prepared the lead organizers to think at bioregional scale. They stay connected across Tayside and with peer initiatives such as [Bioregional Learning Centre South Devon]], which helped them get started. [The core team is small, held together by long collaboration, friendship, and shared care for the living world.

A theory of change and catchment-wide strategic goals are still in draft. Learning is mostly organic—convened when an issue forces collective attention—and technical support is bought in when grants allow (for example, a leading Scottish food journalist serves as Food Ambassador).

Activities

🔸

Tayside Community Science Initiative

 🔸

River Ericht Catchment Restoration

 🔸

River Detectives

 🔸

Strategy Games

 🔸

Stocktake and Gap Analysis

Vision and Legacy

Priorities on the horizon include deeper mapping (including carbon sequestration options), a possible nature-finance aggregation platform so varied restoration projects can connect to capital, regenerative tourism, and more intentional choices about digital tools—alongside persistent questions of skills and governance. Funding remains the binding constraint: work is overwhelmingly grant-funded, nature-finance experiments are fragile under policy and corporate uncertainty, and no dedicated bioregional financial institutions exist yet. Engagement has been project-led and trust-based; a full bioregion-wide visioning process is still pending. Land and power in Scotland, thin investment in relationships, capacity gaps for restoration at pace, burnout, and the sheer complexity of restoration all slow change—yet visible projects and community voice are shifting what Tayside believes is possible, with arts and culture seen as under-used levers when resources allow.

🟡 Bioregional Weaving Alliance Ireland

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

BWL South East Ireland is a bioregional initiative functioning as a transition hub, learning centre, and living lab. It brings together a network of Irish stakeholders, partners, and the living systems of the region itself. Established as a non-profit in 2022 and formally constituted in 2024.

Bioregional Context

The bioregion is centered around the Bride Valley in Ireland and includes the village of Dunhill, which is part of the Copper Coast UNESCO Geopark. The landscape is primarily agricultural, with a mix of dairy farms, grazing lands, and areas being transformed for nature conservation.

Place
County Waterford, Ireland
links
https://bioregion.ie/|https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i5GEa804rO0|https://bioregion.ie/bioregional-finance/

Tags

📍 Territorial_Zoning 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 💬 Peer_Learning 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Social_or_Network_Mapping 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 👛 Universal_Basic_Income 🔀 Grants_Programs

Background

The Bride Valley, which is part of the Copper Coast UNESCO Geopark, is characterized by a primarily agricultural landscape—a mix of dairy farms, grazing lands, and areas being transformed for nature conservation. Multiple generations of family farms form the backbone of the local economy, creating a context where sustainable transitions must work with existing farming communities rather than replacing them.

Origins and Network

The Bioregional Weaving Alliance Ireland (BWA Ireland) is a bioregional initiative functioning as a transition hub, learning centre, and living lab. Established as a non-profit in 2022, the organization brings together a network of Irish stakeholders, partners, and the living systems of the region itself.

The organization is involved in a 'deep demonstration' partnership with the Irish government to test landscape approaches to the future of sustainable dairy, positioning them to establish the Bioregional Weaving Alliance as an entity designed to support learning and initiatives leading to bioregional regeneration, restoration, and protection, especially in the South East.

Activities

🔸

Family Land Biodiversity Index

 🔸

Sustainable Dairy Index

 🔸

Dunhill Ecopark Development Initiative

 🔸

Regenerative Agriculture Demonstration Network

 🔸

Waterford Bioregional Mapping

Vision and Legacy

Through their deep demonstration partnership with the Irish government and their portfolio of initiatives, BWA Ireland is positioned to serve as a vessel for change—inspiring farmers, communities, and visitors to participate in regenerative practices that benefit nature, farmers, economy, and inspiration. The work demonstrates how locally-driven initiatives can scale up through policy influence (as with the FBI model), while maintaining strong community roots and collaboration at the bioregional level.

Sources

📄

Bioregional Finance

 🎬

Bioregional Weaving Lab - Bioregional Weaving Alliance Ireland

Network Affiliation

🟣 Bioregional Weaving Labs

🟡 Bioregional Learning Centre South Devon

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Bioregional Learning Centre (BLC) in South Devon works at the intersection of geosystems, human systems, and ecosystems to build long-term climate resilience and civic capacity. Its work is structured around four key pillars– developing place-based funding ecosystems; enabling citizen-led data collection and analysis to track vitality and adaptive capacity; supporting community groups with evidence for effective representation in governance; and fostering systems thinking through education and cultural engagement.

Bioregional Context

The region is defined by five rivers, with the River Tamar in the west (serving as the historic 7th century Cornwall-Devon boundary) and River Tean in the east. All rivers rise from Dartmoor in the north, flowing to the English Channel in the south. They recognize potential connections to a wider "Channel bioregion," noting stronger commonalities with French coastal communities than northern neighbors. The landscape is characterized by multiple river valleys that shape both the physical environment and human settlement patterns.

Place
River Dart, Greenway Road, Brixham, TQ5 0, United Kingdom
links
https://youtu.be/m47HX3U2Auo|https://devondoughnut.org/

Tags

👁️‍🗨️ Storying_Place 🍩 Doughnut_Assessment 💧 Watershed_Repair 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 🦏 Rights_of_Nature 🤝🏾 Agreements_and_Legal_Instruments 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


The Bioregional Learning Centre (BLC) in South Devon operates in a region defined by five rivers flowing from Dartmoor in the north to the English Channel in the south. The River Tamar in the west has served as the historic boundary between Cornwall and Devon since the 7th century, while the River Teign marks the eastern boundary.

Origins & Network


The Bioregional Learning Centre prioritizes relationships in how they describe and work with bioregions—focusing on the ability to build relationships, create connective tissue, and understand natural boundaries. They work at the intersection of geosystems, human systems, and ecosystems, understanding how living systems are changing, and how to adapt.

Philosophy & Approach


The organization has identified four foundational pillars necessary to sustain their work long-term:

1. Finance: Developing place-based funding ecosystems for bioregional work.
2. Data and Metrics: Enabling citizen-led data collection, use, access, sharing, and interrogation. .
3. Governance: Supporting community groups with solid information and evidence for effective representation.
4. Arts and Culture: Using creative engagement to connect communities with place and ecological issues they cannot directly experience.

Together, these pillars form resilient infrastructure: data informs governance, which strengthens community agency, and attracts contextually appropriate funding.

Activities

🔸

Story of Place

 🔸

Devon Doughnut Project

 🔸

Dartington Charter & Dance

 🔸

Dancing in Salt Marshes

 🔸

Climate Adaptation Learning Journey

Vision and Legacy


The BLC has been doing extensive projects, particularly on rivers, engaging local people in thinking about drought, water conservation, salt marsh restoration, and river charters. Increasingly, they recognize the need to put a platform or pillars underneath their work to sustain it long-term—the four pillars of finance, data and metrics, governance, and arts and culture. This approach has produced tangible outcomes—from river charter co-development and Devon Doughnut economic frameworks to regional ecosystem interventions and innovative arts-based engagement connecting communities with place.

Celebrating the launch of the Bioregional Learning Alliance
