Network Affiliation

🟡 Sinal do Vale 🟡 Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance 🟡 Blueprint for Impact 🟡 Diamante Barú Bioregional Consortium 🟡 Blue Ridge Foodshed Fund 🟡 ReVillage Green Valley 🟡 Huwa Karu Intercultural Reserve 🟡 Northeast Bioregioning Collective 🟡 Acción Serrana 🟡 CEDPO 🟡 Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort 🟡 Bosque Fractal 🟡 Regenerate Cascadia 🟡 ReGenerOsa Collective 🟡 Salmon Nation 🔵 ReFi Uganda 🟡 Bioregioning Tayside

🟡 Bay Delta Trust

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Bay-Delta Trust, fiscally sponsored by the Buckminster Fuller Institute, operates as a weaving and convening initiative focused on improving network coordination and increasing regenerative investments in the bioregion. Their approach builds on a rich history of bioregional work in the area, including Peter Berg's influential contributions through the Planet Drum Foundation in the 1970s, which helped establish early municipal environmental programs.

Bioregional Context

The San Francisco Bay-Delta bioregion encompasses the largest tidal estuary on the west coast of the Americas, centered around the San Francisco Bay and Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta. The region is defined by its watershed drainage basin, which extends well beyond the immediate bay area to include multiple counties in California. The Mediterranean climate shapes key regional concerns around water, food, and fire management.

Place
California St, San Francisco, CA, United States
links
https://www.notion.so/c59dc12d6ce14f858e6bfdfbe4fb7d54?pvs=21|https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vgt9g24gi7Q&t=1s&ab_channel=BioregionalTV

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 🎒 Learning_Programs 🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 📣 Political_Advocacy

Background


In the San Francisco Bay–Sacramento–San Joaquin River Delta bioregion, Coast Miwok and Ohlone peoples and their ancestors sustained relations with the land and water over countless generations—interwoven, like coast redwoods that support one another though their roots are shallow, so the grove stands.

More recently, Peter Berg—an early voice for bioregionalism—made San Francisco a base. In the mid-1980s he convened Bay Area groups to draft the Green City Program for urban sustainability, published in 1989, and helped organize annual Shasta Bioregion Gatherings across Northern California. Today’s initiatives draw on both Indigenous continuity and that later bioregional lineage.

Origins and Network


Lawrence Grodeska founded the Bay-Delta Trust, a weaving and convening initiative seeking to improve network coordination and increase regenerative investments in their bioregion. The organization, fiscally sponsored by the Buckminster Fuller Institute, explores new place-based models of pluralistic governance and collaboration.

Activities

🔸

Bay Delta Community Learning Ecosystem

 🔸

Bay Delta Asset Mapping

 🔸

Bay Delta Governance Innovation

Vision and Legacy


The Trust describes itself as still in a discovery phase: listening, interviewing, and mapping partners with humility before large commitments. The aim is a 10,000-day horizon toward a 100-year vision for the Bay–Delta—a container where anyone can contribute how they imagine quality of life and a livable watershed.

Sources

🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 4

🔵 Barichara Regenerativa

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Fundación Barichara Regenerativa aims to regenerate the regional climate system of the Northern Andes through cultural regeneration, watershed restoration, regenerative agriculture, and economic development using traditional knowledge.

Bioregional Context

Located in the Santander department of northeastern Colombia, the Barichara region lies within the Northern Andes, a transitional zone between highland cloud forests and drier tropical ecosystems. This area is shaped by steep topography, seasonal water scarcity, and increasing vulnerability to climate change. Barichara is also a cultural heritage site, known for its colonial architecture and deep-rooted campesino traditions.

Place
Barichara, Colombia

🔵 Bangladesh Environment and Development Society

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Bangladesh Environment and Development Society (BEDS) is an organization implementing integrated ecovillage solutions in Bangladesh's vulnerable coastal regions. Their holistic approach encompasses mangrove restoration, renewable energy provision, clean water systems, climate-resilient housing, and sustainable livelihoods. BEDS engages communities through participatory design processes, connecting ecological conservation with economic empowerment while preserving local cultural practices. Their work has restored 150 hectares of mangroves and provided climate adaptation benefits to thousands of families.

Bioregional Context

The Sundarbans region encompasses the world's largest contiguous mangrove forest spanning the Bangladesh-India border at the Bay of Bengal delta. This UNESCO World Heritage site serves as a crucial buffer zone against cyclones and storm surges for millions of people. Local communities primarily depend on farming, fishing, and forest resources for subsistence, with limited economic alternatives.

Place
Sundarbans, Kashipur Road, Khulna, Bangladesh
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit|https://www.notion.so/Forests-1fad03fcfeeb8008bc25f5e7bdbec9ba?pvs=21|https://www.notion.so/Ecosystem-Management-Regeneration-1f9d03fcfeeb800080fad8c93663c87e?pvs=21|https://www.notion.so/Food-Systems-1f9d03fcfeeb8015b43ce765662e8ab5?pvs=21|https://www.notion.so/Construction-Infrastructure-1f9d03fcfeeb802e8bc2ce0cba692bf5?pvs=21

Sources

📊

Bioregional Governance - Asian Case Studies & Framework

🔵 Australian Earth Laws Alliance (AELA)

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Australian Earth Laws Alliance (AELA) is a group of volunteer lawyers connecting earth jurisprudence with indigenous knowledge to critique western industrialized systems and address root causes of ecological and social justice problems through a bioregional lens.

Bioregional Context

Australia is a continent with remarkable diversity—from desert and semi-desert regions of central western Queensland to rainforests and coastal areas. Western science classifies Australia as having 89 bioregions and 415 sub-regions, with major catchments reflecting the continent's ancient geology. The modern nation-state was built with straight lines on top of millennia of Indigenous care and connection to place.

Place
Melbourne, VIC 3004, Australia
links
https://youtu.be/ntsUgQDkts0

Sources

🎬

r3.0 Bioregional Open Dialogue 4 - Bioregioning in Australia

🟡 Atelier LUMA

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Atelier LUMA operates as a biodesign lab in the Camargue region of southern France, connecting human and non-human elements through design interventions. The organization transforms undervalued local resources into valuable materials and products while maintaining a multidisciplinary team of approximately 20 people.

Bioregional Context

Located 45 kilometers from the Mediterranean Sea in southern France, the region sits between the sea and Camargue National Park. The area is characterized by its former industrial heritage, operating from a converted railway station that once housed industry.

Place
The Camargue, Lieu-dit Arnella, 13460 Saintes-Maries-de-la-Mer, France
links
https://www.luma.org/en/arles/atelierluma.html

Tags

🎋 Materials_Transformation 🧦 Clothing_and_Product_Manufacturing 🏗️ Construction_and_Infrastructure 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site 🪢 Network_Weaving 💬 Peer_Learning ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development

Background


The Camargue region of southern France stretches between the Mediterranean Sea and Camargue National Park, a landscape shaped by both natural processes and industrial heritage. Atelier LUMA operates from a converted railway station in Arles, part of the Luma Arles cultural institute.

Origins and Network


Atelier LUMA functions as a biodesign lab where "bio" encompasses both human and non-human elements and how they live together. Their core principle guides their approach: materials are heavy and should stay local, while ideas and people are light and should be global. The organization emerged from Jan Boelen's work developing biolabs across Europe, including one in Ljubljana inspired by projects like the Santian Eco City Lab.

Atelier LUMA discovers undervalued resources in a region—heritage, science and technology, crafts, existing needs—and connect them through design processes. The organization maintains a multidisciplinary team of approximately 20 people—designers, biologists, engineers, and lab technicians—enabling work across disciplines from material research and development to prototyping and knowledge transmission.

Activities

🔸

Resource Transformation and Material Development

 🔸

Sustainable Building Demonstration

 🔸

Biofabric Places Network

Vision and Legacy


All this methodology is written down in their book, Atelier Luma: Bioregional Design Practices. This publication shares their approach to finding, connecting, implementing, and transmitting bioregional design knowledge with broader communities, demonstrating how locally-driven material innovations can scale while respecting bioregional specificity—enabling knowledge to flow globally while materials remain rooted in place.

Sources

🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 3

🔵 Association Française d'Agroforesterie

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Association Française d'Agroforesterie (AFAF) supports the agroecological transition across France by promoting agroforestry practices. Since 2007, it has worked with over 130 partners through research and development, farmer training, knowledge dissemination, territorial coordination, and policy engagement.

Bioregional Context

The Adour Garonne watershed in France is home to 1/3 of French farms and provides 230,000+ jobs. After decades of intensive farming and with climate change, key ecological concerns of the region include severe erosion, drought, and flooding as well as nitrogen loading and other pollution.

Place
Toulouse, Occitanie, France

🔵 Anou

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Anou Cooperative is a collective of over 600 artisans representing cooperatives, associations, and workshops throughout Morocco. It is owned and operated by members of the Moroccan artisan community and is open to artisans who demonstrate a commitment to quality craftsmanship and transparent practices.

Bioregional Context

Morocco's diverse geography has long nurtured a rich tradition of artisan crafts, particularly in weaving, dyeing, and metalwork. Artisans often inherit skills through intergenerational transmission, with materials and motifs shaped by local ecologies and histories. In recent decades, global market shifts and urban migration have threatened traditional livelihoods.

Place
Casablanca, Casablanca - Settat, Morocco

🟡 Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Established in 2017, the Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance unites indigenous nationalities across Ecuador and Peru to protect their ancestral territories. The alliance operates through indigenous-led governance, cross-border collaboration, integration of traditional decision-making, and strategic partnerships between indigenous communities and NGOs to safeguard one of the Amazon's most biodiverse regions.

Bioregional Context

The region encompasses 35 million hectares spanning Ecuador and Peru, where the Andes mountain range meets the Amazon basin. This creates dramatic elevation changes from 6,000 meters in the Andean glaciers to less than 500 meters in the Amazon lowlands. The area represents one of the Amazon's most biodiverse hotspots, containing approximately 50 distinct ecosystems and numerous indigenous territories.

Place
Ecuador
links
https://sacredheadwaters.org/|https://youtu.be/4VG88nc2s6E|https://cuencasagradas.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/ENG_PLAN-BIOREGIONAL_10_2021.pdf|https://medium.com/regen-network/indigenous-led-group-launches-cutting-edge-biocultural-jaguar-credits-ac981c74c37a|https://app.regen.network/project/sharamentsa-pilot

Tags

📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🔘 Governance_Bodies 🪢 Network_Weaving 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🏕️ Territorial_Monitoring 📣 Political_Advocacy 📉 Ecological_Monitoring ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators

Background


Where the Andes' glacial peaks descend to meet the Amazon basin, an experiment in intercultural governance is taking shape across 35 million hectares of one of Earth's most biodiverse regions. The bioregion encompasses more than 50 distinct ecosystems, fed by rich hydrology flowing from Andean glaciers into the Amazon. The region plays a crucial role in carbon absorption, with 60% of the bioregion composed of protected areas and Indigenous territories, creating a unique governance landscape where conservation and indigenous sovereignty intersect.

Across these territories spanning the Ecuador-Peru border, more than 30 indigenous peoples and nationalities maintain their own territories and languages: Andas, Achuaras, Kofanes, Secoyas, Shuar, Kukama, Iquito, Siona, Kichwa, and others. While each community faces distinct challenges, they share systemic pressures from extractive industries, infrastructure development, and environmental degradation.

Origins and Network


The Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance was born in 2017 when indigenous nations from Ecuador and Peru issued an urgent call to protect this megadiverse bioregion. Their vision: develop a bioregional plan that articulates realistic solutions to address the economic and social challenges facing the region while protecting its ecological and cultural integrity.

The initiative maps how indigenous territories connect across national borders, creating what participants describe as a "mosaic of interculturality and plurinationality." This vision aligns with Ecuador's constitutional recognition of interculturality and plurinationality as foundational principles of the state. However, the alliance faces a fundamental challenge: Peru's legal framework differs significantly from Ecuador's, creating governance complexities when states fail to recognize indigenous rights equally.

Activities

🔸

Cross-Border Indigenous Alliance Building

 🔸

Reforesting Edible Forests

 🔸

Amazon Headwaters Territorial Monitoring

 🔸

ASHA International Advocacy

 🔸

Biocultural Jaguar Credits Program

Vision and Legacy


The vision articulated toward 2030 envisions this bioregion permanently protected and restored as a living bioregion, inspired by indigenous people's stewardship vision and practices. The plan envisions people and forests flourishing with a prosperous and inclusive well-being economy. The region would contribute to reversing global warming and biodiversity loss, serving as an inspiring model for a just ecological transition.

The comprehensive approach to ecosocial transition represents a significant effort to balance environmental conservation with social development, while centering indigenous rights and knowledge systems. Its success depends on sustained political will, adequate funding, and effective implementation of the proposed mechanisms, all while maintaining the cultural and ecological integrity of the Sacred Headwaters region.

Sources

📄

Indigenous-Led Group Launches Cutting-Edge Biocultural Jaguar Credits

 📊

Cuencas Sagradas Bioregional Plan

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Alvelal

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A collaborative alliance operating across over 1 million hectares in southeastern Spain, using Commonland's Four Returns Framework to reverse desertification and restore prosperity. Since 2014, AlVelAl Association has grown to more than 490 members including 280 farmers, developing regenerative businesses and coordinating landscape-scale restoration through the Altiplano Regeneration Manifesto, community machinery banks, and regenerative education programs that merge art, biodiversity, and emotional connection to land.

Bioregional Context

The Altiplano Estepario spans five districts at the intersection of Almería, Murcia and Granada, three provinces belonging to Spain's southernmost region, Andalucía. It is a semi-arid steppe and transition zone between desert and Mediterranean forest. The bioregion encompasses over 1 million hectares across more than 80 municipalities. Water has always been scarce in this landscape, yet the area has been home to human settlements for centuries. Desertification and extreme climate conditions, combined with unsustainable land management practices, have reduced local biodiversity and climate resilience, making this a challenging landscape for local communities.

Place
15506 County Road 22, Doniphan, NE 68832, United States
links
https://alvelal.es/|https://youtu.be/QL35HCi30Ac|https://commonland.com/landscapes/reversing-desertification-with-regenerative-practices/|https://alvelal.es/certificacionecoregenerativa/

Tags

📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 4️⃣ 4_Returns 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 💧 Watershed_Repair 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 👩‍🌾 Farmer_Support 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site 🛠️ Resource_Hub 💬 Peer_Learning ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🎒 Learning_Programs 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 📋 Finance_Assesments 💲 Structuring_and_Capitalization

Background

The Altiplano Estepario spans five districts at the intersection of Almería, Murcia and Granada, three provinces belonging to Spain's southernmost region, Andalucía. This landscape is a semi-arid steppe—a large area of flat unforested grassland—that forms a transition zone between desert and Mediterranean forest. The bioregion encompasses over 1 million hectares across more than 80 municipalities, creating a vast territory where water scarcity has shaped human settlement patterns for centuries.

Despite these challenging conditions, the economy today is mainly based on agriculture, with traditional farming and pastoral activities playing important cultural and economic roles. Yet the landscape tells a story of both loss and potential. Mediterranean forests once covered vast areas of the territory—trees like oaks and evergreen shrubs such as thyme and rosemary. However, desertification and extreme climate conditions, combined with unsustainable land management practices, have reduced local biodiversity and climate resilience. This leads to both social and economic losses as young people leave and entrepreneurship becomes increasingly difficult.

Origins and Network

Since 2014, Commonland's landscape partner AlVelAl has been using the 4 Returns Framework to bring together local farmers, conservationists, government, and entrepreneurs to create a common vision for the territory. The alliance is made up of several entities that have a common vision of achieving the regeneration of this territory.

![[Plan-AlVelAl-2024-5 (arrastrado).jpg]]

As one director reflects: "At the moment people move away from the region to find work. I'm inspired by being part of a project that transforms a territory for the younger generation, so that my daughters can choose to live here."

Activities

🔸

Altiplano Regeneration Manifesto

 🔸

Natural Ecosystems Restoration

 🔸

Regenerative Agriculture Transition

 🔸

Ecoregenerative Certification

 🔸

Regenerative Agriculture Machinery Bank

 🔸

Regenerative Value Chain Development

 🔸

Ecotourism Development

 🔸

Education Alliance

 🔸

Landscape Finance Strategy

Vision and Legacy

The vision by 2034 is clear: the AlVelAl Territory becomes a regenerative landscape where nature and agriculture are in balance. An ecological corridor connects the region's mountain ranges. Farms with both natural and productive lands form a mosaic of biodiversity hotspots, which act as stepping zones between natural areas. Rural abandonment is reversed as young people return and find new opportunities. The area continues to regenerate, led by a group of diverse, inspired local stakeholders.

Current progress includes: 71 initiatives including art projects, demo pilots, and farms, available to raise awareness for local communities; 216 farmers implementing improved management practices in line with regenerative agriculture; 14,509 hectares under direct improved management and restoration in combined and natural zones; and 43 locally run businesses benefiting from increased access to new markets and value chain infrastructure.

The Altiplano Estepario continues to serve as a lighthouse example of holistic landscape restoration based on the 4 Returns Framework in drylands areas, inspiring other initiatives across Spain and Portugal as they work toward creating a 4 Returns Network across the Iberian Peninsula. This approach recognizes that ecological restoration must work alongside economic activities, creating a mosaic where natural areas connect through productive landscapes managed regeneratively.

Sources

📄

Territorios Regenerativos La Red

 📄

Certificación Ecoregenerativa

 🎬

Bioregional Weaving Lab - Altiplano Estepario (Spain)

 📄

Network Affiliation

🟣 Commonland

🟡 Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

A partnership of 25 organizations committed to collaborative action for Scotland's temperate rainforest. Formed to address one of the world's most biodiversity-depleted countries, the Alliance works to restore and double the size of Scotland's rainforest by 2045, creating a more resilient and better functioning ecosystem.

Bioregional Context

Scotland's temperate rainforest zone stretches along the country's western coast and islands, characterized by high rainfall, mild temperatures, and remarkable biodiversity. This globally rare ecosystem—found on only about 1% of the planet's land surface—contains ancient woodlands some more than 8,000 years old, with exceptional moss, liverwort, and lichen communities found in few other places worldwide. An estimated 30,000 hectares of rainforest remain in Scotland—a mere 2% of woodland cover and only a fraction of the area that could support rainforest.

Place
Edinburgh, Scotland, United Kingdom
links
https://savingscotlandsrainforest.org.uk/blog/the-common-ground-forum-what-difference-can-an-award-make|https://thecommongroundforum.scot/|https://www.act-now.org.uk/Handlers/Download.ashx?IDMF=daab8ba6-02a2-44e8-9f3c-4b3cb406e148

Tags

🌱 Vegetation_Planting 🐉 Species_Control 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 🔘 Governance_Bodies ♾️ Commons_Governance_Protocols 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms

Background

Scotland's temperate rainforest woodlands are one of its most precious resources, some more than 8,000 years old, and all of them bursting with life. Hundreds of different species are found here in exceptional quantities, many rare elsewhere—from resident jays and woodpeckers to migrant pied flycatchers and wood warblers, from rare pearl-bordered fritillaries to badgers, red squirrels and pine martens hiding among the trees and crags. It is an incredibly rare habitat, found on only about 1% of our planet's land surface, and the west of Scotland is one of those special places with just the right conditions.

Much of Scotland's temperate rainforest has been lost over the centuries, with an estimated 30,000 hectares remaining—a mere 2% of woodland cover. Despite a plethora of environmental organisations working for decades, with thousands of members, hundreds of nature reserves and countless initiatives, Scotland is still one of the most biodiversity-depleted countries on Earth.

That loss runs deeper than the landscape—it has faded from our collective imagination too. When a community engagement officer asked a group of 27 primary-age children if they knew of the Amazon rainforest, most put their hands up. But when asked if they knew about Scotland's rainforest, only two hands went up.

Origins and Network

The Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest (ASR) was formed in 2021 to address this challenge. The Alliance now has 25 diverse partner organisations and works collaboratively with 20 more, all of whom are committed to pooling their resources for the benefit of Scotland's rainforest. Partners include a range of conservation and environmental organisations, specialist societies and government agencies. In 2021, the Scottish Government made a commitment to expand and restore Scotland's rainforest, and then worked closely with ASR to develop a strategic approach to deliver on that commitment, published in 2024.

Much of the work is about coordinating landscape-scale projects, securing long-term funding, building capacity to deliver on-the-ground work, and communicating about the rainforest and the threats it faces. The partners work together with local people to provide the expertise, resource and capacity needed to deliver the vision for Scotland's rainforest.

Activities

🔸

Landscape-Scale Restoration Projects

 🔸

The Common Ground Forum

 🔸

Our Rainforest Futures

 🔸

Nature Finance for Scotland's Rainforest

Vision and Legacy

By 2045, the vision is clear: all of Scotland's rainforest restored, its size doubled, creating a more resilient ecosystem. Local communities will be championing the rainforest, wildlife will be more diverse, and at least 30 landscape-scale projects will be working to fully restore Scotland's rainforest. A £500 million Rainforest Restoration Fund will be established, with every £1 spent on rainforest restoration bringing £2 into the local economy.

Restoring Scotland's rainforest will take decades, and the vision of a healthy network of woodlands across west Scotland is the work of generations. Yet, with so many people and organisations now working together, that possibility feels closer than ever. As one land manager put it: "I've been filled with hope by the number of different people coming together with the aim of saving the rainforest. Our rainforest really could be saved if we continue the momentum!"

Sources

📄

Saving Scotland's Rainforest with Natural Capital Investment

 📄

The Common Ground Forum wins a Nature of Scotland Award

 📊

Saving Scotland's Rainforest

🟡 Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Bioregional regeneration work on Mallorca operates through multiple interconnected initiatives, from the quiet facilitation work of Daniel Christian Wahl to the formal Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca formed in 2022. The work centers on cultivating conditions for deep, place-based transformation through convening, listening, and connecting.

Bioregional Context

Mallorca is an island within the Mediterranean's Balearic archipelago. Its island nature creates what seemed like clear bioregional boundaries at first, though the work has revealed complexities in managing it as a "fractal bioregion" of various islands within an archipelago biome. The region's ecological balance and social cohesion are severely threatened by over-tourism and climate change.

Place
Palma, Balearic Islands, Spain
links
https://youtu.be/VEORXmWyKkE|https://commonland.com/landscapes/restoring-land-and-seascapes-in-a-tourist-hotspot/

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🛐 Cultural_and_Spiritual_Practices 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 🚚 Regional_Sourcing 🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


Mallorca's ecological balance and social cohesion are severely threatened by over-tourism and climate change. Climate change happens at a rate 20% higher in the Mediterranean than elsewhere in the world. Many of Mallorca's native species are facing existential challenges from ecological changes, such as warming seas. The boom in land speculation from tourism has also caused a rapid increase in land and housing prices, negatively affecting local communities. There is growing recognition of the need to regenerate the island's land- and seascapes and tackle negative socio-economic trends resulting from human activities.

Origins and Network


The Aliança Mar i Terra di Mallorca was formed in 2022 by a consortium of local organisations facing similar challenges who decided to work towards aligned objectives together. The alliance aims to support the regeneration of regional land and sea habitats on the island of Mallorca in the Mediterranean Sea. Support from Commonland as of 2022 enabled the alliance to start working together formally.

The alliance looks toward a more balanced socioeconomic future: a more connected community co-creating biodiverse, thriving local ecosystems; a local economy grounded in a healthy agroecosystem able to supply a substantial share of food and other resources; and a systemic view in which land and sea are addressed together.

Activities

🔸

Tourism Industry Transformation Initiative

 🔸

Sacred Pathways Revitalization Project

 🔸

Foodshed and Energyshed Mapping

 🔸

Educational Work with Schools

Vision and Legacy


On Mallorca, this understanding informs a patient, relationship-based approach to bioregional regeneration—one that dances with the system rather than trying to control it, and that celebrates when others take up the work as their own. The work recognizes that the education and stewardship efforts of the organizations are already very strong and can be linked to physical places with active ongoing projects.

Opportunities ahead include: restoring land and seascapes holistically as interconnected systems; ending industry silos that currently reduce impact; creating a clear, long-term landscape vision that enables Mallorca to shift and diversify its economic model to the benefit of local communities and nature alike; developing new regenerative business models that bring revenues to local communities, including new eco-tourism models that involve outsiders in supporting landscape restoration; and creating greater impact through the Alliance—collaboration allows for synergies between different organisational projects and greater resilience to collective obstacles.

Sources

📄

Restoring land and seascapes in a tourist hotspot

 🎬

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 6

Network Affiliation

🟣 Commonland

🟡 Acción Serrana

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Acción Serrana focuses on biocultural restoration, reforestation, community organizing, and building long-term ecological and cultural resilience in mountainous regions as part of the larger Acción Andina coalition.

Bioregional Context

The Sierras Grandes of Córdoba are part of the Andes' southernmost reaches, a vital source of freshwater and biodiversity. This region is threatened by deforestation, desertification, and climate change, strongly impacting indigenous and gaucho communities who rely on its ecological health.

Place
Cordoba, Argentina
links
https://youtu.be/zS367PdPNqw|https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc

Tags

🌰 Seeds_and_Nurseries 🌱 Vegetation_Planting 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways 🔘 Governance_Bodies 🔀 Grants_Programs ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development

Background


In the heart of Argentina, the Sierras Grandes of Córdoba represent the southernmost extension of the Andean mountain system. This high-altitude landscape shelters the Polylepis australis forest—known locally as "tabaquillo"—the highest-growing native trees in the region, forming the highest forest on Earth. These forests are critically important: they protect watershed headwaters, improve soil quality through fallen leaves and root systems, retain soil against erosion, and provide habitat for many endemic species adapted to the harsh mountain environment.

Yet these forests face severe threats: overgrazing by cattle and other animals, forest fires, and accelerating soil erosion. For generations, this ecosystem has provided water for the province of Córdoba and supported indigenous and gaucho communities who rely on its ecological integrity. The Sierras Grandes encompass 155,000 hectares of provincial water reserve and 37,000 hectares of national park—the Quebrada del Condorito National Park and the Pampa de Achala provincial water reserve—a territory where restoration work must balance ecological recovery with cultural continuity.

Origins and Network


Acción Serrana operates as a fractal of the larger Acción Andina alliance, a large-scale multinational initiative to restore high-altitude Polylepis ecosystems across the Andean region. Founded in 2018, Acción Andina aims to restore 1 million hectares) over the next 25 years. This network operates across six countries: Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, Colombia, Chile, and Argentina. Together, the alliance has planted more than 12.5 million trees, representing a coordinated effort to restore ecosystems that are critical water sources for major South American rivers.

Activities

🔸

Participatory Forest Restoration

 🔸

Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning

 🔸

Culture and Employment

 🔸

Academia Biosférica

 🔸

Governance and Values

 🔸

Capital Allocation and Financial Strategy

 🔸

Regenerative Products & Services Portfolio

Vision and Legacy


By 2025, the organization expects to reach the milestone of 1 million trees planted in the Sierras Grandes. But the deeper impact extends beyond tree counts: the initiative has restored forests where all the water for the province of Córdoba originates, while simultaneously contributing to Acción Andina's work restoring ecosystems that feed major South American rivers. The project aims to offer a replicable approach to high-altitude forest restoration that can inform work across Andean landscapes and beyond.

Sources

🎬

BioFi Field Building Call: Regenerating the Argentinian Andes

 🎬

BioFi Cultivator Presentations - Day 2

Network Affiliation

🟣 BioFi Project

🟡 Abundance Project

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Abundance Project in the Valley of Grace is working to create a living laboratory of different kinds of economies incentivizing all kinds of regenerative activities. Located in Western Cape, South Africa, the initiative addresses deep racial tensions from apartheid, climate change impacts from deforestation, and the challenge of building regenerative systems through a stewardship-based approach rather than ownership models.

Bioregional Context

The Valley of Grace is located at the southernmost tip of Africa, where the two oceans meet, about 90 minutes west of Cape Town. The valley is part of the Breede River Valley, with the specific area of focus being 10,000 hectares with 25,000 people from very different socioeconomic backgrounds. The region has experienced dramatic climate change due to deforestation, converting from indigenous forest to commercial farming, which has changed rainfall patterns from gentle winter rain to heavy downpours and regular floods.

Place
Western Cape, South Africa
links
https://youtu.be/RhXCT1toHIs

Tags

☮️ Community_Reconciliation 🪢 Network_Weaving 💧 Watershed_Repair 🌀 Systems_and_Resource_Mapping 💠 Collective_Ownership_Models

Background


At the southernmost tip of Africa, about 90 minutes west of Cape Town, the Valley of Grace lies where two oceans meet. It is a bounded area of 10,000 hectares with 25,000 people across a wide socioeconomic range—from the poorest to the wealthiest. The valley sits on the Raft River (Rafson, “river without end”) and contains six small towns within the wider Breede River Valley.

Roughly a century or two ago, much of the south was indigenous forest. The plateau stands between 1,200 and 1,500 meters; coastal air moved over the trees, forest spores encouraged condensation at low altitude, and the region had a Mediterranean pattern of soft winter rain. Commercial farming replaced much of that forest, weakening the condensation cycle: air recirculates over the ocean, atmospheric moisture builds, and the valley now sees heavy downpours and floods instead of gentle winter rain.

European mission presence began in 1737; in 1838 the valley hosted the first teachers’ training college in Africa. The twentieth century layered apartheid onto that landscape—the 1913 Natives Land Act barred non-white people from buying land across southern Africa; the 1950 Group Areas Act restricted where people could live regardless of ownership, segregating communities and driving forced removals in the 1960s. Many people of Khoisan heritage were displaced from land now occupied by white residents; well-meaning environmental work led by white outsiders can meet deep resentment.

Origins and Network


The work began with one aim: to become a living laboratory of economies that reward many kinds of regenerative activity. It has been a 10-year journey. An organization leads a structured process to bring the community together; four workshops have been held so far. Progress is uneven, but gathering the whole community has mattered a great deal.

Activities

🔸

Community Reconciliation and Forgiveness

 🔸

Watershed Restoration Planning

 🔸

Land Stewardship Tokenization

Vision and Legacy


Work continues through workshops and joint processes; convening the community still outpaces the pace of structural change. The forgiveness process runs on both sides of the racial divide that has most constrained the initiative.

Funding remains difficult. Time in family offices and with insurers seeking models to lower their risk has not yet yielded a mechanism that fits the valley’s model. The objective is unchanged: a living laboratory of economies that incentivize regenerative activity across the Valley of Grace.

🟡 ASPROCIG

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

This community-based organization has operated for over 32 years. The organization explicitly rejects conventional development models in favor of an aesthetics-first approach that prioritizes beauty and harmonious living. Their framework emphasizes collective knowledge construction and views humans as indivisible parts of nature rather than owners of it. They take comprehensive approach to empower younger generations through innovative governance and financial structures.

Bioregional Context

Located in the Caribbean region of Colombia, inland from the coast in Córdoba. Centered around the Sinú River basin, which defines the ecological and cultural boundaries of the region. The area represents a rich ecological zone with diverse landscapes and traditional agricultural areas.

Place
90 Chima, 232017 Chimá, Colombia
links
https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8

The Bioregional Conversations - Conversation 2

🟡 7-Generation GTB

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

An initiative fostering social and ecological regeneration in the Greater Tkaronto Bioregion through a Bioregional Learning Center, portfolio of regenerative projects, and bioregional funding ecosystem, weaving together Indigenous and Western knowledge systems.

Bioregional Context

The Greater Toronto Area is Canada's most populous urban region, encompassing Toronto and surrounding municipalities along Lake Ontario. The region includes dense urban centers, suburban sprawl, agricultural lands, and remnants of the Carolinian forest zone—one of the most biodiverse ecosystems in the country.

Place
Junction Area, Toronto, ON, Canada
links
https://legacyproject.org/7gen/bioregionallearning.html|https://legacyproject.org/7gen/bioregionalfunding.html

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🗂️ Portfolio_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 🔘 Governance_Bodies 🪢 Network_Weaving 🎒 Learning_Programs 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🔀 Grants_Programs 💲 Structuring_and_Capitalization

Background


The Greater Toronto Area (GTA) is Canada's most populous urban region, encompassing the city of Toronto and surrounding municipalities across the Golden Horseshoe. Situated along the northwestern shore of Lake Ontario, the GTA includes a mix of dense urban centers, suburban sprawl, agricultural lands, and remnants of the Carolinian forest zone—one of the most biodiverse ecosystems in the country.

The name "Tkaronto" comes from the Mohawk word meaning "where there are trees standing in water," referring to the original Indigenous name for the area. This bioregion anchors the work of 7-Generation GTB, an initiative growing a metaphorical Tree of Life through social and ecological regeneration for ecopsychosocial wellbeing—people, community, and planet.

Origins and Network


The initiative began with a foundational question: "Where will we be in 50 years, if we continue our current development trajectory?" This inquiry, explored across various sectors over two years, shaped the organization's founding process and guided the formation of its first projects. The Legacy Project serves as the core team for 7-Generation GTB, coordinated by Brian Puppa. In the context of the GTB as part of Canada, the initiative weaves together holistic Indigenous knowledge systems with Western scientific thinking to find a "third way" forward.

The network includes partnerships with Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA)—the largest conservation authority in the GTB and largest provider of outdoor education in Canada—Toronto Nature Stewards, York Regional Forest, Rouge National Urban Park, universities, libraries, regenerative farms, and community organizations.

Vision and Legacy


7-Generation GTB works at once locally and in a glocal frame (place-based and globally connected), pairing social with ecological regeneration. It takes guidance from the Earth Charter while staying rooted in this bioregion. The GTB Bioregional Learning Center can operate as a 7-generation space that brings different ways of knowing together, puts life at the center of choices and action, and builds a shared story of place and bioregional identity that supports collective action across social and ecological work.
